provenance.corpus: Tier-1 open (OpenStax University Physics, Fitzpatrick, and peers)
provenance.triage: Frank-approved: 446 auto-clean + 36 flagged KEEP, minus 4 empty/refused cards and one of each of 3 near-duplicate pairs.
provenance.notes: Corpus-grounded; no held-out/gold items. Choice letter prefixes normalized off; three garbled citation labels fixed. difficulty is the authored 0..1 fraction (higher is harder); the seeder maps it to the 1..5 Performance scale. The 15 triage-KEEP problems the generator left uncited (conservative-verifier false positives) were re-grounded against the corpus index in the L5.9 P4b provenance pass. All 15 cleared the cite-or-refuse floor and earned a real corpus citation, so every bundled problem now carries a source_ref and none were dropped.

cards.front: Work-energy theorem: When analyzing motion along an arbitrary curved surface, why can the normal force be ignored in the work calculation, and what relation is used instead of solving Newton’s second law directly?
cards.back: For motion along a surface, the normal force does no work because it is perpendicular to the displacement. Instead, add the work done by all forces that do work and set the total work equal to the change in kinetic energy: \(W_{\mathrm{total}} = \Delta K\). If \(W_{\mathrm{total}}\) is positive, kinetic energy increases; if negative, kinetic energy decreases; if zero, there is no change in kinetic energy.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 1, pp. 337-338, §7.3 Work-Energy Theorem

cards.front: In Newtonian dynamics, what relation follows from taking the scalar product of Newton’s second law with the velocity of a point object, and how is kinetic energy defined?
cards.back: Taking the dot product with velocity gives \(\frac{dK}{dt} = \vec{f} \cdot \vec{v}\), where \(K = \tfrac{1}{2}mv^2\). The quantity \(\vec{f} \cdot \vec{v}\) is the rate at which the force does work on the object, so work done by an external force increases the object’s kinetic energy.
cards.source_ref: Fitzpatrick: Newtonian Dynamics, pp. 13-14, §2.5 Newton’s Second Law of Motion

cards.front: For a particle constrained to move along a surface, why does the normal force not contribute to the work in the work–energy theorem?
cards.back: The normal force is perpendicular to the particle’s displacement along the surface, so it does no work. Thus, in the work–energy theorem, only forces with a component along the displacement contribute to the net work, and the net work equals the change in kinetic energy.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 1, pp. 336-337, §7.3 Work-Energy Theorem

cards.front: Work–energy theorem: What quantity does the net work done on a particle equal, and what does the sign of the net work tell you about its motion?
cards.back: The net work done on a particle equals its change in kinetic energy: \(W_{\mathrm{net}} = \Delta K = K_f - K_i\), with \(K = \tfrac{1}{2}mv^2\). Positive net work means the particle’s kinetic energy increases and it speeds up; negative net work means its kinetic energy decreases and it slows down; zero net work matches zero change in kinetic energy.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 1, pp. 321-323, §6.4 Drag Force and Terminal Speed

cards.front: For a Newtonian system of particles, how is the system’s total kinetic energy defined from the constituent particles?
cards.back: Add the kinetic energies of all particles in the system: each particle has \(K = \tfrac{1}{2}mv^2\), and the system’s total kinetic energy is the sum of these individual kinetic energies.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 1, pp. 332-333, §7.2 Kinetic Energy

cards.front: For a particle moving along an arbitrary 3D trajectory, how is the net work related to the particle’s kinetic energy?
cards.back: The net work is the line integral of the net force along the displacement: \(W_{\mathrm{net}} = \int_A^B \vec{F}_{\mathrm{net}} \cdot d\vec{r}\). By the work-energy theorem, \(W_{\mathrm{net}} = \Delta K = K_B - K_A = \tfrac{1}{2}m(v_B^2 - v_A^2)\), where \(v^2\) is the sum of the squares of the Cartesian velocity components. Positive net work means the particle speeds up; negative net work means it slows down.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 1, p. 336, §7.3 Work-Energy Theorem

cards.front: In Newtonian dynamics, how does taking the dot product of Newton’s second law with velocity lead to the work–energy theorem for a point object?
cards.back: Starting from \(m\,\frac{d\vec{v}}{dt} = \vec{f}\), take the scalar product with \(\vec{v}\): \(m\,\vec{v}\cdot\frac{d\vec{v}}{dt} = \vec{f}\cdot\vec{v}\). Since \(K = \tfrac{1}{2}mv^2\), this becomes \(\frac{dK}{dt} = \vec{f}\cdot\vec{v}\). The right side is the rate at which the force does work, so work done by the external force increases the object’s kinetic energy.
cards.source_ref: Fitzpatrick: Newtonian Dynamics, pp. 13-14, §2.5 Newton’s Second Law of Motion

cards.front: A particle has initial kinetic energy 5. The total (net) work done on it over a displacement is +12. By the work-energy theorem, what is its final kinetic energy?
cards.back: The work-energy theorem says net work equals the change in kinetic energy: \(W_{\mathrm{net}} = \Delta K = K_f - K_i\). Thus \(K_f = K_i + W_{\mathrm{net}} = 5 + 12 = 17\). The positive net work means the kinetic energy increases.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 1, pp. 336-337, §7.3 Work-Energy Theorem

cards.front: In Newtonian mechanics, does exerting a force on an object always mean you do physical work on it?
cards.back: No. Work is done only when a force acts on something that undergoes a displacement. Infinitesimally, work is the dot product of force and displacement. For example, pushing on a wall that does not move involves force but no displacement, so no physical work is done on the wall.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 1, p. 918, §17.7 The Doppler Effect

cards.front: In Newtonian dynamics, what does the work–energy theorem state for a particle?
cards.back: The net work done on a particle by all forces acting on it is equal to the change in its kinetic energy. For a non-relativistic particle, kinetic energy is one-half the mass times the square of the speed.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 1, p. 343, §7.1 Work

cards.front: In Newtonian dynamics, how is the kinetic energy of a system of particles defined?
cards.back: Add the kinetic energies of all constituent particles: \(K_{\mathrm{system}} = \sum_i \left(\tfrac{1}{2} m_i v_i^2\right)\). This is consistent with treating an extended object as a collection of point objects. Kinetic energy has the same units as work: joules.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 1, pp. 332-333, §7.2 Kinetic Energy

cards.front: For a point particle in 3D, what work–energy relation follows by taking the scalar product of Newton’s second law with the velocity?
cards.back: From Newton’s second law, taking the dot product with velocity gives \(dK/dt = \vec{f} \cdot \vec{v}\), where \(K = \tfrac{1}{2}mv^2\). The quantity \(\vec{f} \cdot \vec{v}\) is the rate at which the force does work on the object, so work done by an external force increases the object’s kinetic energy.
cards.source_ref: Fitzpatrick: Newtonian Dynamics, pp. 13-14, §2.5 Newton’s Second Law of Motion

cards.front: Work-energy theorem: For a particle, what does the net work done by all forces equal, and what does the sign of the net work tell you about the particle’s speed?
cards.back: The net work done on a particle equals the change in its kinetic energy: \(W_{\mathrm{net}} = \Delta K\). If \(W_{\mathrm{net}}\) is positive, the object speeds up; if \(W_{\mathrm{net}}\) is negative, it slows down. When applying the theorem, include all forces acting on the object.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 1, p. 336, §7.3 Work-Energy Theorem

cards.front: What does the work-energy theorem state about the net work done on a particle, and what does the sign of the net work indicate?
cards.back: The net work done on a particle is equal to the change in the particle’s kinetic energy. If the object slows down, its final kinetic energy is less than its initial kinetic energy, so the kinetic-energy change and the net work are negative. If the object speeds up, the net work is positive. When calculating net work, include all forces acting on the object.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 1, p. 336, §7.3 Work-Energy Theorem

cards.front: In simple harmonic motion (SHM), how are the restoring force and acceleration related to displacement from equilibrium?
cards.back: They are proportional to the displacement and act in the opposite direction. For a spring oscillator, the restoring force obeys Hooke’s law, and the motion is about the equilibrium position.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 1, pp. 755-756, §15.1 Simple Harmonic Motion

cards.front: For a damped harmonic oscillator, what distinguishes underdamping, critical damping, and overdamping in terms of oscillation and return to equilibrium?
cards.back: Underdamped: the system oscillates while its amplitude decays exponentially. Critically damped: the system returns to equilibrium as quickly as possible without oscillating or overshooting. Overdamped: the system does not oscillate, but returns to equilibrium more slowly than in the critically damped case.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 1, pp. 748-749, §15.5 Damped Oscillations

cards.front: Driven damped oscillator: For \(\frac{d^2 x}{dt^2} + 2\nu \frac{dx}{dt} + \omega_0^2 x = \omega_0^2 X_1 \cos(\omega t)\), what is the steady-state motion after transients die out, and what happens near \(\omega \approx \omega_0\) for weak damping?
cards.back: The steady-state response is periodic with the same angular frequency as the drive: \(x(t) = x_1 \cos(\omega t - \phi_1)\), where \(x_1 = \frac{\omega_0^2 X_1}{\sqrt{(\omega_0^2 - \omega^2)^2 + 4\nu^2\omega^2}}\). For weak damping (\(\nu \ll \omega_0\)), the amplitude becomes large when the drive frequency is close to the resonant/natural frequency \(\omega_0\).
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 1, pp. 750-751, §15.6 Forced Oscillations

cards.front: In a weakly damped, sinusoidally driven harmonic oscillator, what happens at resonance, and how do the resonance peak’s height and width scale with the quality factor Q?
cards.back: Resonance occurs when the driving angular frequency equals the oscillator’s natural angular frequency, \(\omega = \omega_0\), giving a large response amplitude. At resonance the response lags the driving force by \(\pi/2\). For a weakly damped oscillator, \(\frac{x_1(\omega_0)}{x_1(0)} = Q = \frac{\omega_0}{2\nu}\), and the resonance width is \(\Delta \omega = 2\nu\), so \(\frac{\Delta \omega}{\omega_0} = \frac{1}{Q}\). Thus the peak height scales as \(Q\) and the fractional width scales as \(1/Q\).
cards.source_ref: Fitzpatrick: Newtonian Dynamics, pp. 33-34, §One-Dimensional Motion

cards.front: What conditions characterize simple harmonic motion (SHM)?
cards.back: The restoring force is proportional to the displacement and acts opposite the displacement. In ideal SHM there are no drag or friction forces, and the oscillation frequency does not depend on amplitude.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 1, p. 940, §8.11 Conceptual Questions

cards.front: For a weakly damped oscillator with damping parameter \(\nu \ll \omega_0\), how does the free-oscillation amplitude decay, and what is its quality factor \(Q\)?
cards.back:
The displacement can be written as \(x = x_0 \mathrm{e}^{-
u t} \cos(\omega_r t - \phi_0)\), where \(\omega_r = \sqrt{\omega_0^2 - 
u^2}\). Thus the amplitude decays exponentially as \(\mathrm{e}^{-
u t}\). The quality factor is defined as \(2\pi\) times the energy stored divided by the energy lost in one oscillation period, and for weak damping \(Q =  rac{\omega_r}{2
u} \approx  rac{\omega_0}{2
u}\).
cards.source_ref: Fitzpatrick: Newtonian Dynamics, p. 31, §3.6 Quality Factor

cards.front: For a weakly damped driven harmonic oscillator, how do the amplitude and phase of the response change as the driving frequency passes through the natural/resonant frequency?
cards.back: The response amplitude is largest at resonance, when the driving frequency equals the natural frequency; far below or far above resonance the amplitude is small. For weak damping, the response is approximately in phase with the driving force well below resonance, in phase quadrature at resonance, and approximately in anti-phase well above resonance. The resonance peak height scales like the quality factor \(Q\), while its fractional width scales like \(1/Q\).
cards.source_ref: Fitzpatrick: Newtonian Dynamics, pp. 33-34, §One-Dimensional Motion

cards.front: For a weakly damped driven harmonic oscillator, what happens near resonance, and how do the resonance peak height and width scale with quality factor Q?
cards.back: Resonance occurs when the driving frequency is close to the natural frequency \(\omega_0\). A weakly damped oscillator can be driven to large amplitude by a relatively small periodic force near \(\omega_0\). For \(Q \gg 1\), the resonance peak height scales as \(Q\), while its width scales as \(Q^{-1}\). More specifically, the fractional width is \(\Delta \omega/\omega_0 = 1/Q\), with \(\Delta \omega = 2\nu\) when the edges are defined by the amplitude falling to \(1/\sqrt{2}\) of its maximum.
cards.source_ref: Fitzpatrick: Newtonian Dynamics, pp. 33-34, §One-Dimensional Motion

cards.front: What condition on the restoring force defines simple harmonic motion (SHM)?
cards.back: In SHM, the restoring force is proportional to the displacement from equilibrium and acts in the direction opposite to that displacement.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 1, p. 754, §15.6 Forced Oscillations

cards.front: For a damped harmonic oscillator, how do underdamping, critical damping, and overdamping differ in their motion toward equilibrium?
cards.back: Damping comes from nonconservative forces that dissipate the oscillator’s energy, so the amplitude decreases over time. An underdamped system oscillates through equilibrium while its amplitude decays. Critical damping returns the system to equilibrium as fast as possible without overshooting. An overdamped system moves toward equilibrium more slowly than a critically damped system.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 1, p. 756, §15.5 Damped Oscillations

cards.front: Driven damped harmonic oscillator: In steady state under a sinusoidal driving force, what frequency does the oscillator move at, and what happens to the amplitude near resonance as damping is reduced?
cards.back: The oscillator executes sinusoidal motion at the same frequency as the driving force, with a fixed amplitude and phase lag. The amplitude is small when the driving frequency is far below or far above the natural frequency, but becomes large as the driving frequency approaches the natural frequency. Resonance occurs when the driving frequency equals the natural frequency; reducing damping makes the resonance peak higher and narrower.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 1, pp. 751-752, §15.6 Forced Oscillations

cards.front: For a weakly damped driven oscillator (Q ≫ 1), how do the height and width of the resonance peak scale with the quality factor Q?
cards.back: The resonance peak height scales as Q, while its width scales as Q⁻¹. Therefore, the area under the resonance curve stays approximately constant as Q varies.
cards.source_ref: Fitzpatrick: Newtonian Dynamics, pp. 34-35, §3.8 Periodic Driving Forces

cards.front: For a mass m supported by two light vertical springs with spring constants k1 and k2, what is the angular frequency of oscillation if the springs are in parallel? What if they are in series?
cards.back: Parallel: \(\omega = \sqrt{\frac{k_1 + k_2}{m}}\). Series: \(\omega = \sqrt{\frac{k_1 k_2}{(k_1 + k_2)m}}\).
cards.source_ref: Fitzpatrick: Newtonian Dynamics, pp. 42-43

cards.front: Damped harmonic oscillator: in terms of damping coefficient \(\nu\) and undamped natural angular frequency \(\omega_0\), what are the underdamped, critically damped, and overdamped regimes, and how does each behave?
cards.back:
Underdamped: \(
u < \omega_0\). The motion oscillates at \(\omega_r = \sqrt{\omega_0^2 - 
u^2}\), slightly below \(\omega_0\), while its amplitude decays exponentially as \(e^{-
u t}\). Critically damped: \(
u = \omega_0\). The motion returns to equilibrium without oscillating; this is the fastest return without overshooting. Overdamped: \(
u > \omega_0\). The motion also decays without oscillating, but more slowly than critical damping, with two independent decay rates.
cards.source_ref: Fitzpatrick: Newtonian Dynamics, pp. 29-30, §One-Dimensional Motion

cards.front: For a rigid body rotating about a fixed axis, what is Newton’s second law for rotation, and what sign convention is commonly used for the angular acceleration/torque?
cards.back: The rotational dynamics equation is net torque about the rotation axis equals the moment of inertia about that axis times the angular acceleration: \(\tau = I\alpha\). The torque/angular acceleration is treated as a signed scalar for fixed-axis rotation: counterclockwise is positive, while clockwise is negative.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 1, pp. 502-503, §10.7 Newton’s Second Law for Rotation

cards.front: For a rigid body rotating with fixed angular velocity \(\boldsymbol{\omega}\) about an axis through the origin, what is the velocity of a mass element at position \(\mathbf r_i\), and how is the total angular momentum written in terms of the inertia tensor?
cards.back: Each mass element has velocity \(\mathbf v_i = d\mathbf r_i/dt = \boldsymbol{\omega}\times \mathbf r_i\). The total angular momentum about the origin is \(\mathbf L = \sum_i m_i\,\mathbf r_i\times(\boldsymbol{\omega}\times\mathbf r_i)=\sum_i m_i\left[r_i^2\boldsymbol{\omega}-(\mathbf r_i\cdot\boldsymbol{\omega})\mathbf r_i\right]\), which can be written as the matrix equation \(\mathbf L=I\boldsymbol{\omega}\). For example, \(I_{xx}=\sum_i m_i(y_i^2+z_i^2)=\int (y^2+z^2)\,dm\).
cards.source_ref: Fitzpatrick: Newtonian Dynamics, pp. 105-106, §8.1 Introduction

cards.front: For a rigid body rotating with fixed angular velocity \(\omega\) about an axis through the origin, how is its total angular momentum about the origin written in terms of its mass elements, and what compact matrix relation does this imply?
cards.back: Using \(\vec{v}_i = d\vec{r}_i/dt = \vec{\omega} \times \vec{r}_i\), the total angular momentum is \(\vec{L} = \sum_i m_i \vec{r}_i \times (\vec{\omega} \times \vec{r}_i) = \sum_i m_i [r_i^2 \vec{\omega} - (\vec{r}_i \cdot \vec{\omega}) \vec{r}_i]\). This can be written compactly as the moment-of-inertia tensor relation \(\vec{L} = \mathbf{I}\vec{\omega}\), i.e. \((L_x, L_y, L_z)^T = I_{\mathrm{tensor}} (\omega_x, \omega_y, \omega_z)^T\).
cards.source_ref: Fitzpatrick: Newtonian Dynamics, pp. 105-106, §8.1 Introduction

cards.front: For point particles rotating about a fixed axis, what determines the moment of inertia, and how does moving mass farther from the axis affect it?
cards.back: The moment of inertia is \(I = \sum m_i r_i^2\), where \(r_i\) is each particle’s distance from the rotation axis. For one point particle, \(I = mr^2\). Because of the \(r^2\) factor, concentrating more mass farther from the axis gives a larger moment of inertia, meaning greater resistance to changes in angular velocity.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 1, p. 483, §10.4 Moment of Inertia and Rotational Kinetic Energy

cards.front: For a rigid body rotating about a fixed axis, what is Newton’s second law for rotation, and what sign convention is used for angular acceleration/torque?
cards.back: The net torque about the rotation axis equals the moment of inertia about that axis times the angular acceleration: \(\sum \tau = I\alpha\). Counterclockwise angular acceleration/torque is taken as positive; clockwise torque is negative.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 1, pp. 502-503, §10.7 Newton’s Second Law for Rotation

cards.front: For a rigid body in static equilibrium, what two conditions must be satisfied?
cards.back: Both translational and rotational equilibrium must hold: the net external force on the body is zero, and the net external torque on the body is zero. Equivalently, the center of mass has zero linear acceleration and the body has zero rotational acceleration about the rotation axis.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 1, p. 566, §12.1 Conditions for Static Equilibrium

cards.front: For a cylindrically symmetric rigid body rotating about its symmetry axis with angular velocity \(\omega\), what is the direction and magnitude of its angular momentum?
cards.back: The perpendicular components of angular momentum from opposite mass segments cancel, leaving only the component along the rotation axis. Thus the angular momentum is directed along the axis of rotation by the right-hand rule, with magnitude \(L = I\omega\).
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 1, pp. 541-542, §11.2 Angular Momentum

cards.front: For a system of point particles rotating about a fixed axis, what determines the moment of inertia, and how does distance from the axis matter?
cards.back: The moment of inertia is found by summing each point mass multiplied by the square of its distance from the rotation axis. Thus, mass farther from the axis contributes much more: the contribution increases as the square of the distance.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 1, p. 514, §10.3 Relating Angular and Translational Quantities

cards.front: For a rigid body rotating about a fixed axis, what does Newton’s second law for rotation state?
cards.back: The sum of the torques on the rotating system equals its moment of inertia times its angular acceleration.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 1, pp. 511-512, §10.8 Work and Power for Rotational Motion

cards.front: For a rigid body in an inertial frame, what conditions characterize equilibrium and static equilibrium?
cards.back: Equilibrium: both the linear acceleration of the center of mass and the angular acceleration are zero. Equivalently, the net external force is zero and the net external torque is zero. Static equilibrium is the special case in which the rigid body is at rest in the selected inertial frame.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 1, pp. 565-566, §12.1 Conditions for Static Equilibrium

cards.front: For a rigid body, what is the relation between torque and the angular momentum derivative when using a body-fixed frame that co-rotates with the body?
cards.back: If the body frame co-rotates with angular velocity \(\omega\), then the fixed-frame derivative and body-frame derivative are related by \(\frac{d\vec{L}}{dt} = \frac{d\vec{L}}{dt'} + \vec{\omega} \times \vec{L}\). Since torque \(\vec{T} = \frac{d\vec{L}}{dt}\) in the fixed inertial frame, in the body frame: \(\vec{T} = \frac{d\vec{L}}{dt'} + \vec{\omega} \times \vec{L}\).
cards.source_ref: Fitzpatrick: Newtonian Dynamics, pp. 112-113, §8.7 Euler’s Equations

cards.front: For a rigid body, what is the relationship between the moment of inertia tensor’s eigenvectors and the principal axes of rotation, and why are these axes special?
cards.back: The moment of inertia tensor is a real symmetric 3D matrix, so it has three mutually orthogonal eigenvectors with real eigenvalues. The directions of these eigenvectors define the principal axes of rotation. In a coordinate system aligned with these axes, the inertia tensor is diagonal with entries \(I_{xx}\), \(I_{yy}\), and \(I_{zz}\), the principal moments of inertia, and all products of inertia are zero. If the body rotates about one principal axis, the angular momentum \(\vec{L}\) is parallel to the angular velocity \(\vec{\omega}\).
cards.source_ref: Fitzpatrick: Newtonian Dynamics, pp. 109-110, §Rigid Body Rotation

cards.front: For a rigid body rotating about a fixed axis, what is Newton’s second law for rotation, and what does it imply in rotational equilibrium?
cards.back: The net external torque equals the moment of inertia times the angular acceleration: \(\sum \tau = I\alpha\). In rotational equilibrium, \(\alpha = 0\), so the net torque is zero: \(\sum \tau = 0\).
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 1, p. 512, §10.8 Work and Power for Rotational Motion

cards.front: For a rigid body constrained to rotate about a fixed axis with constant angular velocity, what is the steady torque from the supports in the body frame, and what happens if the rotation axis is a principal axis?
cards.back: With \(\dot{\omega}_{x'} = \dot{\omega}_{y'} = \dot{\omega}_{z'} = 0\), Euler’s equations imply the steady body-frame torque satisfies \(\vec{T} = \vec{\omega} \times \vec{L}\), where \(\vec{L} = (I_{x'x'}\omega_{x'}, I_{y'y'}\omega_{y'}, I_{z'z'}\omega_{z'})\). Thus \(\vec{T}\) is perpendicular to both \(\vec{\omega}\) and \(\vec{L}\). If the rotation axis is a principal axis, all three torque components are zero.
cards.source_ref: Fitzpatrick: Newtonian Dynamics, p. 113, §Rigid Body Rotation

cards.front: For a particle in a circular orbit of radius \(r_c\) in a general attractive central force field with radial force per unit mass \(f(r)\), what is the small-radial-perturbation stability criterion?
cards.back:
Let \(x = r - r_c\). Using the circular-orbit condition \(-\frac{h^2}{r_c^3} = f(r_c)\), the linearized radial equation is

\[\ddot{x} + \left[ -\frac{3 f(r_c)}{r_c} - f'(r_c) \right] x = 0.\]

The circular orbit is stable to small perturbations if the bracketed quantity is positive, giving bounded simple-harmonic motion. If it is negative, perturbations grow exponentially and the orbit is unstable.
cards.source_ref: Fitzpatrick: Newtonian Dynamics, pp. 70-71, §5.12 Motion in a General Central Force-Field

cards.front: In a general attractive central force field with radial force per unit mass \(f(r)\), how do you test the stability of a circular orbit of radius \(r_c\) against small radial perturbations?
cards.back:
Let \(x = r - r_c\). The circular orbit satisfies \(-\frac{h^2}{r_c^3} = f(r_c)\). To first order in \(x\), the perturbation obeys

\[
\ddot{x} + \left[ -\frac{3 f(r_c)}{r_c} - f'(r_c) \right] x = 0.
\]

If the bracket is positive, \(x\) executes bounded simple harmonic motion and the circular orbit is stable. If the bracket is negative, \(x\) grows exponentially and the circular orbit is unstable.
cards.source_ref: Fitzpatrick: Newtonian Dynamics, pp. 70-71, §5.12 Motion in a General Central Force-Field

cards.front: In a general central force field with radial force per unit mass \(f(r)\), what is the small-perturbation stability condition for a circular orbit of radius \(r_c\)?
cards.back: Let \(x = r - r_c\) be a small radial perturbation. The linearized radial equation is \(\ddot{x} + \left[-\frac{3 f(r_c)}{r_c} - f'(r_c)\right] x = 0\). The circular orbit is stable to small perturbations if the bracketed quantity is positive, giving bounded simple harmonic motion; it is unstable if that quantity is negative, giving exponential growth.
cards.source_ref: Fitzpatrick: Newtonian Dynamics, pp. 70-71, §5.12 Motion in a General Central Force-Field

cards.front: In a general central force field with radial force per unit mass \(f(r)\), what is the small-radial-perturbation stability criterion for a circular orbit of radius \(r_c\)?
cards.back: Let \(x = r - r_c\). To first order, the perturbation obeys \(\ddot{x} + \left[-\frac{3 f(r_c)}{r_c} - f'(r_c)\right] x = 0\). The circular orbit is stable if the bracketed quantity is positive, giving bounded simple-harmonic motion; it is unstable if that quantity is negative, giving exponentially growing solutions.
cards.source_ref: Fitzpatrick: Newtonian Dynamics, pp. 70-71, §5.12 Motion in a General Central Force-Field

cards.front: In a Newtonian gravitational orbit with potential energy set to zero at infinity, how does the sign of the total energy per unit mass relate to the orbit type and eccentricity?
cards.back: Elliptical orbits have negative total energy and eccentricity e < 1; parabolic orbits have zero total energy and e = 1; hyperbolic orbits have positive total energy and e > 1. This matches the fact that elliptical orbits are bounded, while hyperbolic orbits are unbounded.
cards.source_ref: Fitzpatrick: Newtonian Dynamics, pp. 65-66, §5.10 Orbital Energies

cards.front: For an object in an inverse-square gravitational orbit with potential energy set to zero at infinity, how does the sign of the total energy per unit mass relate to the orbit type?
cards.back: Elliptical orbits have negative total energy, parabolic orbits have zero total energy, and hyperbolic orbits have positive total energy. This matches the bounded/unbounded distinction: elliptical orbits are bounded, while hyperbolic orbits are unbounded.
cards.source_ref: Fitzpatrick: Newtonian Dynamics, pp. 65-66, §5.10 Orbital Energies

cards.front: For a system of interacting particles, what does the central-force assumption imply about total angular momentum if there is no net external torque?
cards.back: The total angular momentum is conserved: dL/dt = 0. For central internal forces, each interacting pair contributes zero net torque, so internal forces cannot change the system’s total angular momentum.
cards.source_ref: Fitzpatrick: Newtonian Dynamics, pp. 17-18, §Newton’s Laws of Motion

cards.front: In Newtonian gravity with potential energy set to zero at infinity, how does the sign of the total orbital energy per unit mass classify Kepler orbits?
cards.back: For an object orbiting the Sun, the total energy per unit mass can be written as \(E = \frac{GM}{2 r_p}(e - 1)\), where \(e\) is orbital eccentricity and \(r_p\) is perihelion distance. Thus: elliptical orbits have \(e < 1\) and \(E < 0\); parabolic orbits have \(e = 1\) and \(E = 0\); hyperbolic orbits have \(e > 1\) and \(E > 0\). Elliptical orbits are bounded, while hyperbolic orbits are unbounded.
cards.source_ref: Fitzpatrick: Newtonian Dynamics, pp. 65-66, §5.10 Orbital Energies

cards.front: For an attractive central power-law force per unit mass, \(f(r) = -c r^n\) with \(c > 0\), what is the stability condition for a circular orbit against small radial perturbations?
cards.back: The circular orbit is stable for \(n > -3\). Attractive central forces that decay faster than \(r^{-3}\) give unstable circular orbits; the inverse-cube case \(n = -3\) is also unstable.
cards.source_ref: Fitzpatrick: Newtonian Dynamics, pp. 71-72, §5.13 Motion in a Nearly Circular Orbit

cards.front: In a central-force orbit, what is an apsis, and what is the apsidal angle for an elliptical orbit in an inverse-square force field?
cards.back: An apsis is a point where the radial distance \(r\) is either a maximum or a minimum. The apsidal angle is the angle swept by the radius vector between two consecutive apsides. For an elliptical orbit in an inverse-square force field, the apsidal angle is \(\pi\).
cards.source_ref: Fitzpatrick: Newtonian Dynamics, pp. 71-72, §5.13 Motion in a Nearly Circular Orbit

cards.front: In the Kepler inverse-square gravitational problem with potential energy set to zero at infinity, how does the total orbital energy per unit mass distinguish elliptical, parabolic, and hyperbolic orbits?
cards.back: The energy per unit mass is E = (GM/2rp)(e − 1), where e is eccentricity and rp is perihelion distance. Thus: elliptical orbits have e < 1 and E < 0; parabolic orbits have e = 1 and E = 0; hyperbolic orbits have e > 1 and E > 0.
cards.source_ref: Fitzpatrick: Newtonian Dynamics, pp. 65-66, §5.10 Orbital Energies

cards.front: In a central force field with radial force per unit mass \(f(r)\), what is the small-perturbation stability criterion for a circular orbit of radius \(r_c\)? For an attractive power law \(f(r) = -c r^n\) (\(c > 0\)), when are circular orbits stable?
cards.back:
The circular orbit is stable to small radial perturbations if

\[f(r_c) + \frac{r_c}{3} f'(r_c) < 0\].

For \(f(r) = -c r^n\) with \(c > 0\), this gives stability for \(n > -3\). Attractive central forces that decay faster than \(r^{-3}\) are unstable; the inverse-cube case \(n = -3\) is also unstable.
cards.source_ref: Fitzpatrick: Newtonian Dynamics, pp. 71-72, §5.13 Motion in a Nearly Circular Orbit

cards.front: For an object orbiting under Newtonian gravity with potential energy set to zero at infinity, how does the sign of the total orbital energy distinguish elliptical, parabolic, and hyperbolic orbits?
cards.back: Elliptical orbits have negative total energy (e < 1), parabolic orbits have zero total energy (e = 1), and hyperbolic orbits have positive total energy (e > 1). This matches the idea that bounded orbits have negative total energy, while unbounded hyperbolic orbits have positive total energy.
cards.source_ref: Fitzpatrick: Newtonian Dynamics, pp. 65-66, §5.10 Orbital Energies

cards.front: For an attractive central power-law force per unit mass, \(f(r) = -c r^{n}\) with \(c > 0\), what condition on \(n\) makes a circular orbit stable to small perturbations? What about \(n = -3\)?
cards.back: The stability criterion for a circular orbit at radius \(r_c\) is \(f(r_c) + \frac{r_c}{3} f'(r_c) < 0\). For \(f(r) = -c r^n\), this gives stability only if \(n > -3\). Thus attractive forces that decay faster than \(r^{-3}\) are unstable; the inverse-cube case \(n = -3\) is also unstable when higher-order terms are included.
cards.source_ref: Fitzpatrick: Newtonian Dynamics, pp. 71-72, §5.13 Motion in a Nearly Circular Orbit

cards.front: For a conservative system described by generalized coordinates \(q_i\), how is the Lagrangian defined, and what equations of motion follow from it?
cards.back: The Lagrangian is \(L = K - U\), the kinetic energy minus the potential energy. If \(K\) and \(U\) can be expressed in terms of the generalized coordinates and their time derivatives, the equations of motion are Lagrange’s equations: \(\frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) - \frac{\partial L}{\partial q_i} = 0\) for \(i = 1,\ldots,F\).
cards.source_ref: Fitzpatrick: Newtonian Dynamics, p. 130, §9.5 Motion in a Central Potential

cards.front: In Hamiltonian formalism, if the total energy can be written as a Hamiltonian \(H(q_i,p_i)\) with no explicit dependence on the generalized velocities, what first-order equations replace the \(F\) second-order Lagrange equations?
cards.back:
Hamilton’s equations: for \(i = 1,\ldots,F\),

\[
\dot{q}_i = \frac{\partial H}{\partial p_i},
\]

\[
\dot{p}_i = -\frac{\partial H}{\partial q_i}.
\]

Thus the dynamics are expressed as \(2F\) first-order differential equations.
cards.source_ref: Fitzpatrick: Newtonian Dynamics, pp. 151-152, §10.7 Hamilton’s Equations

cards.front: In Hamiltonian dynamics, if the total energy can be written as a Hamiltonian \(H(q_i,p_i)\) with no explicit dependence on the velocities \(\dot{q}_i\), what are Hamilton’s equations for each degree of freedom?
cards.back:
For each \(i = 1,\ldots,F\), Hamilton’s equations are
\[
\dot{q}_i = \frac{\partial H}{\partial p_i},
\]
\[
\dot{p}_i = -\frac{\partial H}{\partial q_i}.
\]
They are \(2F\) first-order differential equations, often used instead of the \(F\) second-order Lagrange equations.
cards.source_ref: Fitzpatrick: Newtonian Dynamics, pp. 151-152, §10.7 Hamilton’s Equations

cards.front: For a \(1\mathrm{D}\) harmonic oscillator with kinetic energy \(K = \tfrac{1}{2}m\dot{x}^2\) and potential energy \(U = \tfrac{1}{2}k x^2\), what is the Hamiltonian in terms of \(x\) and its conjugate momentum \(p\), and what Hamilton equations follow?
cards.back: The conjugate momentum is \(p = m\dot{x}\), so \(K = \frac{p^2}{2m}\). The Hamiltonian is \(H(x,p) = \frac{p^2}{2m} + \tfrac{1}{2}k x^2\). Hamilton’s equations give \(\dot{x} = \frac{\partial H}{\partial p} = \frac{p}{m}\) and \(\dot{p} = -\frac{\partial H}{\partial x} = -kx\).
cards.source_ref: Fitzpatrick: Newtonian Dynamics, pp. 151-152, §10.7 Hamilton’s Equations

cards.front: For a conservative system described by generalized coordinates \(q_i\), what is the Lagrangian and what equations of motion does it satisfy?
cards.back: The Lagrangian is defined as the difference between kinetic and potential energy: \(L = K - U\). If \(K\) and \(U\) are expressed in terms of the generalized coordinates \(q_i\) and their time derivatives, the equations of motion are Lagrange’s equations: \(\frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) - \frac{\partial L}{\partial q_i} = 0\) for each degree of freedom \(i\).
cards.source_ref: Fitzpatrick: Newtonian Dynamics, p. 130, §9.5 Motion in a Central Potential

cards.front: Hamiltonian formalism: For a particle of mass \(m\) moving in a central potential \(U(r)\) in plane polar coordinates \((r, \theta)\), what are the conjugate momenta, Hamiltonian, and conserved momentum implied by Hamilton’s equations?
cards.back: The conjugate momenta are \(p_r = m\dot{r}\) and \(p_\theta = m r^2 \dot{\theta}\). The Hamiltonian is \(H = \frac{1}{2m}\left(p_r^2 + \frac{p_\theta^2}{r^2}\right) + U(r)\). Since \(H\) has no explicit \(\theta\) dependence, Hamilton’s equation gives \(\dot{p}_\theta = -\frac{\partial H}{\partial \theta} = 0\), so \(p_\theta\) is conserved.
cards.source_ref: Fitzpatrick: Newtonian Dynamics, pp. 152-153

cards.front: In Hamilton’s principle for a conservative system with generalized coordinates \(q_i\), how is the Lagrangian defined and what are the Lagrange equations of motion?
cards.back: The Lagrangian is \(L(q_i, \dot{q}_i, t) = K - U\), where \(K\) is kinetic energy and \(U\) is potential energy. The equations of motion are \(\frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) - \frac{\partial L}{\partial q_i} = 0\) for each degree of freedom \(i\).
cards.source_ref: Fitzpatrick: Newtonian Dynamics, p. 146, §10.4 Multi-Function Variation

cards.front: For a particle of mass \(m\) moving in a \(2\mathrm{D}\) central potential \(U(r)\), using polar coordinates \((r, \theta)\), what are the generalized momenta and Hamiltonian?
cards.back: The kinetic energy is \(K = \tfrac{1}{2}m(\dot{r}^2 + r^2\dot{\theta}^2)\). The generalized momenta are \(p_r = m\dot{r}\) and \(p_\theta = m r^2\dot{\theta}\). Written in terms of these momenta, the Hamiltonian is \(H = \tfrac{1}{2m}(p_r^2 + p_\theta^2/r^2) + U(r)\).
cards.source_ref: Fitzpatrick: Newtonian Dynamics, p. 130, §9.5 Motion in a Central Potential

cards.front: For a particle moving in a central potential V(r), what angular momentum operators commute with the Hamiltonian, and what does this imply about the Hamiltonian eigenstates?
cards.back: The Hamiltonian commutes with both \(L_z\) and \(L^2\). Therefore, its eigenstates can be chosen to be simultaneous eigenstates of \(L_z\) and \(L^2\); the simultaneous eigenstates of \(L_z\) and \(L^2\) are the spherical harmonics \(Y_{l,m}(\theta,\phi)\).
cards.source_ref: Fitzpatrick: Quantum Mechanics, p. 117, §Introduction

cards.front: In the rotating reference frame of the three-body problem, what equations define the Lagrange points, and what Jacobi integral restricts the allowed regions of motion?
cards.back: Lagrange points are equilibrium points in the rotating frame: the mass \(m_3\) remains at rest there, so \(\dot{r} = \ddot{r} = 0\). Therefore they satisfy \(\partial U/\partial x = \partial U/\partial y = \partial U/\partial z = 0\). The Jacobi integral is \(C = -2U - v^2\), where \(v^2 = \dot{x}^2 + \dot{y}^2 + \dot{z}^2\), so allowed regions must satisfy \(-2U \geq C\).
cards.source_ref: Fitzpatrick: Newtonian Dynamics, pp. 206-207

cards.front: In Hamiltonian dynamics, for a particle of mass \(m\) moving in a central potential \(U(r)\) with polar coordinates \((r, \theta)\), what are the conjugate momenta and Hamiltonian, and what does Hamilton’s equation imply for \(p_\theta\)?
cards.back: The kinetic energy is \(K = \tfrac{1}{2}m(\dot{r}^{2} + r^{2}\dot{\theta}^{2})\). The conjugate momenta are \(p_r = m\dot{r}\) and \(p_{\theta} = m r^{2}\dot{\theta}\). Thus \(H = \tfrac{1}{2m}(p_r^{2} + p_{\theta}^{2}/r^{2}) + U(r)\). Since \(H\) has no explicit \(\theta\) dependence, \(\dot{p}_{\theta} = -\partial H/\partial \theta = 0\), so \(p_{\theta}\) is constant.
cards.source_ref: Fitzpatrick: Newtonian Dynamics, pp. 152-153

cards.front: In Lagrangian mechanics, what is the generalized momentum conjugate to a generalized coordinate \(q_i\), and what happens if \(q_i\) is ignorable?
cards.back: The generalized momentum conjugate to \(q_i\) is \(p_i = \frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\). Lagrange’s equation can be written \(\frac{dp_i}{dt} = \frac{\partial L}{\partial q_i}\). Therefore, if \(L\) does not depend explicitly on \(q_i\), then \(\frac{\partial L}{\partial q_i} = 0\) and \(p_i\) is conserved.
cards.source_ref: Fitzpatrick: Newtonian Dynamics, pp. 135-136, §9.8 Generalized Momenta

cards.front: For a particle moving in a central potential \(V(r)\), why does the Hamiltonian commute with both \(L_z\) and \(L^2\)?
cards.back: The Hamiltonian can be written as \(H = -\frac{\hbar^2}{2m}\left(\frac{\partial^2}{\partial r^2} + \frac{2}{r}\frac{\partial}{\partial r} - \frac{L^2}{\hbar^2 r^2}\right) + V(r)\). Here \(L_z\) and \(L^2\) depend only on the angular spherical polar coordinates, while functions of \(r\) and radial differential operators commute with them. Also, \(L^2\) commutes with itself and with \(L_z\). Therefore \([H,L_z] = 0\) and \([H,L^2] = 0\), so the Hamiltonian has simultaneous eigenstates with \(L_z\) and \(L^2\).
cards.source_ref: Fitzpatrick: Quantum Mechanics, p. 117, §Introduction

cards.front: Coulomb’s law concept check: For two point charges separated by distance r, how does the electric force depend on charge and distance, and what determines whether the force is attractive or repulsive?
cards.back: The magnitude of the Coulomb force is proportional to the magnitude of each charge and inversely proportional to the square of their separation: \(F \propto \frac{|q_1||q_2|}{r^2}\). Like charges repel; unlike charges attract. The force on each charge has equal magnitude and opposite direction to the force it exerts on the other charge.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 2, p. 218, §5.1 Electric Charge

cards.front: For an electrostatic electric field diagram, what do the field-line direction and field-line density represent?
cards.back: The electric field vector is tangent to the field line at each point. The field points away from positive charges and toward negative charges. The magnitude of the field is proportional to the density of field lines.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 2, pp. 218-219, §5.1 Electric Charge

cards.front: Electrostatics: What is an equipotential surface, and how is it oriented relative to electric field lines?
cards.back: An equipotential surface is the collection of points in space that are all at the same electric potential. Equipotential surfaces are always perpendicular to electric field lines.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 2, p. 325, §7.1 Electric Potential Energy

cards.front: Gauss’s law: For a Gaussian surface, what electric field is used in the flux integral, and which charges appear on the right-hand side?
cards.back: Use the total electric field at every point on the closed Gaussian surface: it includes contributions from charges both inside and outside the surface. However, Gauss’s law relates the net flux only to the net charge enclosed by that closed surface: \(\Phi_E = \frac{Q_{\mathrm{enclosed}}}{\varepsilon_0}\).
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 2, pp. 243-245, §6.2 Explaining Gauss’s Law

cards.front: Capacitance: what does it measure, and what is 1 farad?
cards.back: Capacitance is the amount of charge stored per unit volt. Since capacitance is charge per unit voltage, 1 farad = 1 coulomb per volt: 1 F = 1 C/V.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 2, pp. 337-338, §8.1 Capacitors and Capacitance

cards.front: What happens microscopically when a dielectric made of polar molecules is placed in an external electric field?
cards.back: The polar molecular dipoles tend to align with the external field. Opposite charges on adjacent dipoles within the dielectric neutralize, so there is no net charge in the volume, but equal and opposite induced surface charges appear on opposite faces. These surface charges create an induced electric field that opposes the external field, making the net field inside the dielectric smaller.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 2, pp. 357-358, §8.5 Molecular Model of a Dielectric

cards.front: For two point charges separated by distance r, how does the Coulomb force depend on charge, distance, and sign?
cards.back: The magnitude of the electric (Coulomb) force is proportional to the magnitude of each charge and inversely proportional to the square of their separation distance. Like charges repel; unlike charges attract. The forces on the two charges are equal in magnitude and opposite in direction.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 2, pp. 191-192, §5.3 Coulomb's Law

cards.front: In electrostatics, what is the electric field, what direction does it point relative to charges, and what principle does it obey?
cards.back: The electric field is an alteration of space created by the presence of electric charge; it mediates/transmits the electric force between a source charge and a test charge. It is a vector that points away from positive charges and toward negative charges, and it obeys the superposition principle.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 2, pp. 218-219, §5.1 Electric Charge

cards.front: In electrostatics, what is electric potential, and how is electric potential difference (voltage) defined?
cards.back: Electric potential is electric potential energy per unit charge. Electric potential difference (voltage) is the change in potential energy of a charge moved between two points, divided by that charge. Its units are joules per coulomb, called volts.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 2, p. 324, §7.6 Applications of Electrostatics

cards.front: Gauss’s law: Why is choosing a Gaussian surface with the same symmetry as the charge distribution useful?
cards.back: For spherical, cylindrical, or planar symmetry, a matching Gaussian surface makes the electric field easy to handle on the surface: E can be treated as constant in the flux integral, so the flux becomes E times an appropriate area. Gauss’s law then relates this flux to the enclosed charge divided by ε0, giving an algebraic equation for the magnitude of E. The direction of E follows from the symmetry and the sign of the charge.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 2, pp. 265-267, §6.13 Charge on a Conductor

cards.front: For an isolated charged capacitor (battery disconnected), what happens when a dielectric is inserted between the plates?
cards.back: The dielectric develops induced surface charges opposite in sign to the free charges on the capacitor plates, so it is pulled into the gap. The capacitance increases; because the battery is disconnected, the plate charge is unaffected. The potential difference between the plates decreases, and the stored electrical energy decreases.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 2, pp. 356-357, §8.4 Capacitor with a Dielectric

cards.front: In a dielectric placed in an external electric field, what happens to its molecular dipoles and what electric field is produced by the induced surface charges?
cards.back: Polar molecules align with the external electric field; nonpolar molecules can become polarized by induced polarization. The dielectric remains electrically neutral overall, but equal and opposite induced surface charges appear on opposite faces. These induced surface charges produce an induced electric field that opposes the external field.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 2, pp. 357-358, §8.5 Molecular Model of a Dielectric

cards.front: Coulomb electrostatics: How does the electric force between two charges depend on their signs and their separation?
cards.back: Like charges repel and unlike charges attract. The force between charges decreases with the square of the distance between them.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 2, pp. 217-218, §5.7 Electric Dipoles

cards.front: How do Maxwell’s equations explain the propagation of an electromagnetic wave through free space?
cards.back: A changing magnetic field induces a time-varying electric field, and a changing electric field creates a magnetic field. This self-continuing process propagates through space without a material medium, and Maxwell showed that electromagnetic waves in free space travel at the speed of light.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 2, p. 674, §16.1 Maxwell’s Equations and Electromagnetic Waves

cards.front: For a charged particle in crossed fields with \(\mathbf{E}\perp\mathbf{B}\), what drift velocity is caused by the added electric field, and does it depend on the particle’s charge or mass?
cards.back:
The particle drifts perpendicular to both fields with the fixed \(\mathbf{E}\times\mathbf{B}\) velocity
\[
\mathbf{v}_{EB}=\frac{\mathbf{E}\times\mathbf{B}}{B^2}.
\]
This drift is independent of the particle’s charge and mass.
cards.source_ref: Fitzpatrick: Newtonian Dynamics, pp. 51-52, §4.4 Charged Particle Motion in Electric and Magnetic Fields

cards.front: Why did Maxwell modify Ampère’s law in the case of a charging parallel-plate capacitor?
cards.back: The usual Ampère’s law says the line integral of the magnetic field around a closed loop is proportional to the current passing through any surface bounded by that loop. For a charging capacitor, one such surface can cut the wire and enclose a nonzero current, while another surface spanning the same loop can pass between the capacitor plates and enclose no real current. Maxwell fixed this surface-dependence by adding the displacement current, produced by a changing electric field, which enters Ampère’s law like a real current and can produce a magnetic field even where no real current is present.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 2, pp. 670-671, §16.1 Maxwell’s Equations and Electromagnetic Waves

cards.front: Biot–Savart setup: For a current element in a wire, how are \(d\boldsymbol{\ell}\) and \(\hat{\mathbf r}\) defined, and how do you determine the direction of the magnetic field contribution at point \(P\)?
cards.back: Choose \(d\boldsymbol{\ell}\) in the direction of the positive current. The unit vector \(\hat{\mathbf r}\) points from the current element toward the field point \(P\). The direction of the magnetic field contribution is given by the right-hand rule applied to the vector product \(d\boldsymbol{\ell}\times\hat{\mathbf r}\). The field from a current element has no component parallel to \(\hat{\mathbf r}\); for a finite wire, add contributions by integrating along the wire.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 2, p. 514, §12.1 The Biot-Savart Law

cards.front: For a conducting rod or armature on rails, what happens when a changing magnetic flux induces a current in the rod?
cards.back: The induced current through the rod/armature experiences a magnetic force and is propelled forward. In a constant-velocity rod setup, the applied force must balance this magnetic force.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 2, pp. 567-568, §13.3 Motional Emf

cards.front: In the rail-gun setup described with conducting rails and an armature carrying current, what causes the armature/projectile to be propelled forward?
cards.back: The current through the armature experiences a magnetic force, which propels it forward.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 2, pp. 567-568, §13.3 Motional Emf

cards.front: Ampère’s law: For a closed integration path, which currents determine the line integral of the magnetic field around the path?
cards.back: The line integral of the magnetic field around the closed path is proportional to the total current passing through an open surface whose perimeter is that path. Only currents inside the path of integration need be considered.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 2, pp. 528-529, §12.5 Ampère’s Law

cards.front: For an infinitely long, straight wire carrying current, what is the shape of the magnetic field lines, how does their spacing change with distance, and how is the direction found?
cards.back: The magnetic field lines are circular and centered on the wire, identical in every plane perpendicular to the wire. Because the field decreases with distance from the wire, the field-line spacing increases with distance. The direction is given by the right-hand rule: point your right thumb along the current, and your fingers curl in the direction of the magnetic field.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 2, pp. 517-518, §12.2 Magnetic Field Due to a Thin Straight Wire

cards.front: For a current-carrying loop of any plane shape placed in a uniform magnetic field, what is the net magnetic force on the loop? What loop property is used to calculate its magnetic torque?
cards.back: The net force on the current-carrying loop is zero. Its magnetic torque is calculated using the loop’s magnetic dipole moment and the magnetic field; the magnetic dipole moment is the product of the number of turns, current, and loop area: NIA.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 2, p. 502, §11.4 Magnetic Force on a Current-Carrying

cards.front: In classical electricity and magnetism, what role does the Lorentz force law play alongside Maxwell’s equations?
cards.back: The four Maxwell’s equations together with the Lorentz force law encompass the major laws of electricity and magnetism.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 2, p. 700, §16.1 Maxwell’s Equations and Electromagnetic Waves

cards.front: For an infinitely long, thin, straight wire carrying steady current, what Amperian loop makes Ampère’s law easy to apply, and what symmetry facts simplify the line integral?
cards.back: Choose a circular path centered on the wire in a plane perpendicular to it. Cylindrical symmetry makes the magnetic field constant around this circle, and the radial component is zero because magnetic field lines are continuous closed loops, giving zero net magnetic flux through a closed surface. Along the circular path, the remaining magnetic field is parallel to dℓ, so the line integral simplifies. Only current passing through the surface bounded by the path is included.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 2, pp. 528-529, §12.5 Ampère’s Law

cards.front: Biot–Savart for a circular arc: At the center of a current-carrying circular arc, what geometric simplification makes the integral easier, and how is the field direction handled?
cards.back: For a circular arc, the path element dl is always perpendicular to the radial direction from the element to the center, so the cross product in the Biot–Savart law reduces to multiplication. The contributions at the center are all in the same direction for the arc, so their magnitudes can be integrated; the direction is determined by the right-hand rule. If other wires/arcs are present, add their magnetic-field contributions by vector superposition.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 2, pp. 516-517, §12.1 The Biot-Savart Law

cards.front: In free space, what does Gauss’s law for magnetism imply about magnetic field lines and magnetic monopoles?
cards.back: Magnetic field lines form continuous closed loops: any magnetic field line entering a closed surface must also leave it. No isolated north or south magnetic pole is known to exist; magnetic poles occur in north–south pairs.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 2, pp. 673-674, §16.1 Maxwell’s Equations and Electromagnetic Waves

cards.front: In Maxwell’s equations, which law describes electromagnetic induction, and what role does it play in electromagnetic wave propagation?
cards.back: Faraday’s law of induction, including Lenz’s law, is the Maxwell equation associated with induction. In Maxwell’s theory, changing magnetic fields produce changing electric fields; together with Maxwell’s displacement-current correction, where changing electric fields produce magnetic fields, this symmetry explains electromagnetic wave propagation.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 2, p. 700, §16.1 Maxwell’s Equations and Electromagnetic Waves

cards.front: In Maxwell’s electromagnetic theory, what are the four Maxwell equations/laws, and what was Maxwell’s key correction to Ampère’s law?
cards.back: They are Gauss’s law for electricity, Gauss’s law for magnetism, Faraday’s law of induction including Lenz’s law, and Ampère’s law in a symmetric form. Maxwell’s correction was to add the displacement current: changing electric fields act as another source of magnetism, giving symmetry between changing electric and magnetic fields and explaining electromagnetic-wave propagation.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 2, p. 700, §16.1 Maxwell’s Equations and Electromagnetic Waves

cards.front: In Maxwell’s equations, what correction did Maxwell add to Ampère’s law, and why is it crucial for electromagnetic waves?
cards.back: Maxwell added the displacement current term: changing electric fields act as a source of magnetic fields. This restores symmetry with Faraday’s law, where changing magnetic fields produce electric fields. That symmetry explains electromagnetic wave propagation: changing magnetic and electric fields sustain one another, with the wave’s electric field, magnetic field, and direction of propagation mutually perpendicular.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 2, p. 700, §16.1 Maxwell’s Equations and Electromagnetic Waves

cards.front: In Maxwell’s equations, what symmetry explains electromagnetic wave propagation?
cards.back: Changing magnetic fields produce changing electric fields, and changing electric fields produce magnetic fields. Maxwell introduced this symmetry through the displacement current in Ampère’s law.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 2, p. 700, §16.1 Maxwell’s Equations and Electromagnetic Waves

cards.front: In Maxwell’s explanation of electromagnetic wave propagation, what roles do Faraday’s law and the modified Ampère-Maxwell law play?
cards.back: Faraday’s law says a changing magnetic field induces a time-varying electric field. The modified Ampère-Maxwell law says a changing electric field creates a magnetic field. Together, these changing fields can continually induce one another, producing an electromagnetic wave that propagates through space.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 2, p. 674, §16.1 Maxwell’s Equations and Electromagnetic Waves

cards.front: Faraday induction + Lenz’s law: What condition is required for an induced emf in a coil, and what determines its direction?
cards.back: An induced emf is created only when the magnetic flux changes; if there is no change in magnetic flux, no induced emf is created. The direction of the induced emf is such that it opposes the change in magnetic flux that causes it, which is Lenz’s law.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 2, pp. 590-591, §13.1 Faraday’s Law

cards.front: In Maxwell’s picture of electromagnetic wave propagation, how can time-varying electric and magnetic fields sustain a wave through space?
cards.back: A changing magnetic field induces a time-varying electric field by Faraday’s law. In turn, the changing electric field creates a magnetic field according to the modified Ampère’s law. This repeated process produces time-varying electric and magnetic fields farther and farther away, which can be viewed as an electromagnetic wave propagating through space.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 2, p. 674, §16.1 Maxwell’s Equations and Electromagnetic Waves

cards.front: In Maxwell’s account of electromagnetic wave propagation, what roles do Faraday’s law and the modified Ampère law play?
cards.back: Faraday’s law says a changing magnetic field induces a time-varying electric field. The modified Ampère law says a changing electric field creates a magnetic field. Together, these effects form a self-continuing process of time-varying electric and magnetic fields propagating through space as an electromagnetic wave.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 2, p. 674, §16.1 Maxwell’s Equations and Electromagnetic Waves

cards.front: Electromagnetic induction: What happens if the magnetic flux through a circuit does not change, and what direction does an induced emf take when the flux does change?
cards.back: If there is no change in magnetic flux, no induced emf is created. When an emf is induced, its direction always opposes the change in magnetic flux that causes it; this is Lenz’s law.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 2, pp. 590-591, §13.1 Faraday’s Law

cards.front: In Maxwell’s picture of electromagnetic-wave propagation, what roles do Faraday’s law and the modified Ampère’s law play?
cards.back: A changing magnetic field through a surface induces a time-varying electric field at the boundary of that surface by Faraday’s law. In turn, a changing electric field creates a magnetic field according to the modified Ampère’s law. This repeated process produces time-varying electric and magnetic fields farther and farther away, i.e. an electromagnetic wave propagating through space.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 2, p. 674, §16.1 Maxwell’s Equations and Electromagnetic Waves

cards.front: In Maxwell’s picture of electromagnetic wave propagation, what roles do Faraday’s law and the modified Ampère law play?
cards.back: Faraday’s law says a time-varying magnetic field induces a time-varying electric field. The modified Ampère law says a changing electric field creates a magnetic field. This mutual induction can form a self-continuing electromagnetic wave that propagates through space without a material medium, at the speed of light.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 2, p. 674, §16.1 Maxwell’s Equations and Electromagnetic Waves

cards.front: Electromagnetic induction: What happens if the magnetic flux through a circuit does not change, and what is the direction of an induced emf when the flux does change?
cards.back: If there is no change in magnetic flux, no induced emf is created. When an emf is induced, its direction opposes the change in magnetic flux that causes it; this is Lenz’s law.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 2, pp. 590-591, §13.1 Faraday’s Law

cards.front: In Maxwell’s equations, what does Faraday’s law say a changing magnetic field produces, and what is the direction of the induced emf?
cards.back: A changing magnetic field induces an electromotive force (emf) and hence an electric field. The induced emf is directed so that it opposes the change, as described by Lenz’s law.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 2, pp. 673-674, §16.1 Maxwell’s Equations and Electromagnetic Waves

cards.front: For a plane electromagnetic wave, how are the electric field, magnetic field, and direction of propagation oriented? What defines the wave’s polarization direction?
cards.back: The electric field, magnetic field, and direction of propagation are all mutually perpendicular, so an electromagnetic wave is transverse. The polarization direction is defined as the direction parallel to the electric field.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 3, pp. 40-41, §1.7 Polarization

cards.front: For an electromagnetic wave, how are the electric field, magnetic field, and propagation direction oriented, and what sustains its propagation?
cards.back: The electric field, magnetic field, and direction of propagation are all mutually perpendicular, so an electromagnetic wave is transverse. Its propagation is sustained by changing magnetic fields producing changing electric fields, and changing electric fields producing changing magnetic fields.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 2, p. 700, §16.1 Maxwell’s Equations and Electromagnetic Waves

cards.front: For the same incident electromagnetic wave, how does the radiation pressure compare when the wave is reflected versus absorbed?
cards.back: Electromagnetic waves carry momentum and exert radiation pressure. For the same incident wave, the radiation pressure is twice as large if the wave is reflected as it is if the wave is absorbed.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 2, p. 700, §16.1 Maxwell’s Equations and Electromagnetic Waves

cards.front: For a plane electromagnetic wave, what is the relative orientation of the electric field, magnetic field, and direction of propagation? What direction defines the wave’s polarization?
cards.back: The electric field, magnetic field, and direction of propagation are all mutually perpendicular, so an electromagnetic wave is transverse. The direction of polarization is defined as the direction parallel to the electric field.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 3, pp. 40-41, §1.7 Polarization

cards.front: For a plane electromagnetic wave propagating in free space, what are the relative directions of the electric field, magnetic field, and direction of propagation? Does it require a material medium?
cards.back: Maxwell’s equations predict that the electric field, magnetic field, and propagation direction are all mutually perpendicular, so the electromagnetic wave is transverse. Electromagnetic waves do not require a material medium and can propagate through vacuum at the speed of light.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 2, pp. 675-676, §16.1 Maxwell’s Equations and Electromagnetic Waves

cards.front: What is radiation pressure, and why can an electromagnetic wave exert it on an object?
cards.back: Radiation pressure is force divided by area applied by an electromagnetic wave on a surface. It occurs because electromagnetic waves carry momentum; an object absorbing an electromagnetic wave experiences a force in the direction of wave propagation. If the radiation is reflected rather than absorbed, the force is twice as great.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 2, pp. 687-688, §16.4 Momentum and Radiation Pressure

cards.front: For a plane electromagnetic wave, how are the electric field, magnetic field, and direction of propagation oriented relative to one another?
cards.back: They are all mutually perpendicular. The electric and magnetic fields oscillate perpendicular to the direction of propagation, so an electromagnetic wave is transverse.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 3, pp. 40-41, §1.7 Polarization

cards.front: What is radiation pressure, and how does the force on a perfectly reflecting surface compare with that on an absorbing surface?
cards.back: Electromagnetic waves carry momentum, so an object absorbing an electromagnetic wave experiences a force in the direction of wave propagation; this force corresponds to radiation pressure. If the radiation is reflected rather than absorbed, the force is twice as great.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 2, pp. 687-688, §16.4 Momentum and Radiation Pressure

cards.front: In electromagnetic-wave communication, what is modulated in AM radio versus FM radio, and why do higher-frequency carrier waves allow more data per unit time?
cards.back: AM radio varies the wave’s amplitude to encode the sound signal, while FM radio varies the wave’s frequency. More generally, electromagnetic waves can carry information by controlled variation of amplitude, frequency, or phase. Higher-frequency radio waves can carry more detailed variations per unit time, so more data can be transmitted per unit time.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 2, pp. 693-694, §16.5 The Electromagnetic Spectrum

cards.front: For electromagnetic waves propagating in free space, what does Maxwell’s theory predict about the need for a medium and the propagation speed? What basic relation connects speed, frequency, and wavelength?
cards.back: Electromagnetic waves are combined electric and magnetic fields that can propagate through free space without a material medium. In free space, their propagation speed is the speed of light. For any wave, \(v = f\lambda\), so greater frequency corresponds to smaller wavelength.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 2, pp. 675-676, §16.1 Maxwell’s Equations and Electromagnetic Waves

cards.front: Electromagnetic radiation can exert radiation pressure on an object. In what direction is the force on an absorbing object, and how does the force change if the radiation is reflected instead of absorbed?
cards.back: An absorbing object experiences a force in the direction of propagation of the electromagnetic wave, because electromagnetic waves carry momentum. If the radiation is reflected rather than absorbed, the force is twice as great.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 2, pp. 687-688, §16.4 Momentum and Radiation Pressure

cards.front: A sinusoidal AC source with peak voltage \(170\,\mathrm{V}\) is connected across a \(10\,\Omega\) resistor. What is the peak current, and what is its phase relation to the voltage?
cards.back: Peak current: 17 A. For a resistor in an AC circuit, the current through and voltage across the resistor are in phase.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 2, p. 662, §15.1 AC Sources

cards.front: In circuit terminology, what distinguishes direct current (dc) from alternating current (ac), and for a simple resistor connected to a sinusoidal ac source, what is the phase relation between voltage and current?
cards.back: Direct current (dc) is the flow of electric charge in only one direction and corresponds to a constant source voltage once steady state is established. Alternating current (ac) is produced by a periodically varying source voltage and the charge flow periodically reverses direction. In a simple resistance circuit driven by sinusoidal ac, the voltage and current are in phase: their peaks, troughs, and zero crossings occur together.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 2, pp. 635-636, §15.1 AC Sources

cards.front: In simple AC circuits with sinusoidal sources, what are the phase relationships between current and voltage for a resistor, capacitor, and inductor?
cards.back: Resistor: current through and voltage across are in phase. Capacitor: voltage follows current by one-fourth of a cycle. Inductor: voltage leads current by one-fourth of a cycle.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 2, p. 662, §15.1 AC Sources

cards.front: In simple sinusoidal AC circuits, what is the phase relationship between current and voltage for a resistor, capacitor, and inductor?
cards.back: Resistor: current through and voltage across are in phase. Capacitor: voltage follows the current by one-fourth of a cycle. Inductor: voltage leads the current by one-fourth of a cycle.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 2, p. 662, §15.1 AC Sources

cards.front: In an AC RLC series circuit, what is impedance conceptually?
cards.back: Impedance is the AC analog of resistance in a DC circuit. It is measured in ohms and represents the combined effect of resistance, capacitive reactance, and inductive reactance. The current amplitude is determined by the AC version of Ohm’s law using this impedance.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 2, pp. 645-646, §15.3 RLC Series Circuits with AC

cards.front: In simple AC circuits with a sinusoidal source, how are the voltage and current phased for a resistor, capacitor, and inductor?
cards.back: Resistor: current through and voltage across are in phase. Capacitor: voltage follows current by one-fourth of a cycle. Inductor: voltage leads current by one-fourth of a cycle.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 2, p. 662, §15.1 AC Sources

cards.front: For a series RLC circuit driven by an AC source, what condition identifies resonance, and how does it appear on an average power vs. angular frequency plot?
cards.back: At the resonant frequency, the inductive reactance equals the capacitive reactance. The average power vs. angular frequency plot has a peak at the resonant frequency; the sharpness or width of that peak is called the bandwidth.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 2, p. 662, §15.1 AC Sources

cards.front: In a simple resistance circuit connected to an AC voltage source, what is the phase relationship between the voltage and current?
cards.back: The voltage and current are in phase: their sinusoidal time dependences have peaks, troughs, and nodes at the same times.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 2, p. 636, §15.1 AC Sources

cards.front: In magnetism in matter, what happens to the magnetic field inside a superconductor placed in an applied magnetic field?
cards.back: A superconductor exhibits perfect diamagnetism: the net magnetic field within it is always zero. When a material becomes superconducting, magnetic field lines are expelled from the sample; this is the Meissner effect.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 3, p. 426, §9.8 Superconductivity

cards.front: In simple AC circuit elements, what is the phase relationship between voltage/emf and current for a resistor, inductor, and capacitor?
cards.back: Resistor: voltage and current are in phase. Inductor: emf/voltage leads current. Capacitor: current leads emf/voltage. Mnemonic “ELI the ICE man”: E leads I in L, and I leads E in C.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 2, pp. 646-647, §15.3 RLC Series Circuits with AC

cards.front: In an AC RLC circuit, what is impedance, and what is its unit?
cards.back: Impedance is the AC analog of resistance in a DC circuit. It measures the combined effect of resistance, capacitive reactance, and inductive reactance, and its unit is the ohm.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 2, pp. 645-646, §15.3 RLC Series Circuits with AC

cards.front: What are the two key electromagnetic properties of a material once it becomes superconducting, and what is the associated magnetic-field expulsion called?
cards.back: Below a critical temperature, a superconductor has zero electrical resistivity. It also has perfect diamagnetism: in an applied magnetic field, the net magnetic field inside the superconductor is always zero because magnetic field lines are expelled. This expulsion is the Meissner effect.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 3, p. 426, §9.8 Superconductivity

cards.front: In physics terminology, what is the key distinction between a law and a theory, and what happens if a reliable experiment contradicts either one?
cards.back: A law is a concise, very general statement describing a pattern or single action in nature, often expressible as a single mathematical equation. A theory is more complex and dynamic, explaining an entire group of related phenomena. If a good-quality, verifiable experiment contradicts a law or theory, it must be modified or overthrown.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 1, p. 24, §1.2 Units and Standards

cards.front: In quantum-mechanical formalism, what is a stationary state, and how can such a state be specified?
cards.back: A stationary state is an eigenstate of the Hamiltonian, also called a state of definite energy; its probability density does not vary in time. Such a state can be specified by the quantum numbers identifying the eigenstate.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 3, pp. 316-317, §7.4 The Quantum Particle in a Box

cards.front: In the position representation of quantum mechanics, how are displacement and momentum represented as operators, and how is the expectation value of an operator O written?
cards.back: Displacement is represented by the algebraic operator \(x\), while momentum is represented by the differential operator \(p = -i\hbar \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\). The expectation value of an operator \(O\) is \(\langle O\rangle = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \psi^*(x,t) O \psi(x,t)\, dx\).
cards.source_ref: Fitzpatrick: Quantum Mechanics, pp. 45-46, §Operators

cards.front: In measurement terminology, what is the difference between accuracy and precision, and what quantities describe their deviations?
cards.back: Accuracy is how close a measured value is to an accepted reference value; the discrepancy is the amount by which the measurement differs from that reference. Precision is how closely repeated measurements agree with each other; uncertainty quantifies how much measured values deviate from one another.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 1, p. 46, §1.2 Units and Standards

cards.front: What does the Heisenberg uncertainty principle say about simultaneous measurements of a particle’s x-position and x-momentum?
cards.back: The uncertainties cannot both be made arbitrarily small: the product of the position uncertainty and momentum uncertainty satisfies \(\Delta x\, \Delta p \geq \hbar/2\). This limit is not due to imperfect apparatus; it originates in the wave-like nature of matter.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 3, p. 339, §7.1 Wave Functions

cards.front: In the axiomatic formalism of Newtonian dynamics, what are the fundamental undefined terms, and what plays the role of the axioms?
cards.back: The fundamental undefined terms are mass, position, time, and force. The axioms are Newton’s laws of motion, from which other propositions are derived using logic and mathematical analysis; these laws are justified by experimental observation.
cards.source_ref: Fitzpatrick: Newtonian Dynamics, pp. 9-10, §2.1 Introduction

cards.front: For a system of N non-relativistic particles moving in one dimension, what mathematical object specifies the instantaneous quantum state, and what is its normalization condition?
cards.back: The instantaneous state is specified by a complex wavefunction \(\psi(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_N, t)\). The probability of finding the particles in intervals \(dx_1\, dx_2\, \ldots\, dx_N\) around positions \(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_N\) is \(|\psi(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_N, t)|^2\, dx_1\, dx_2\, \ldots\, dx_N\), so the wavefunction must satisfy \(\int |\psi(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_N, t)|^2\, dx_1\, dx_2\, \ldots\, dx_N = 1\) over all configuration space.
cards.source_ref: Fitzpatrick: Quantum Mechanics, p. 85, §Multi-Particle Systems

cards.front: In the position representation of quantum mechanics, the classical product \(xp\) has an operator-ordering ambiguity because \(x\) and \(p\) do not commute. What Hermitian operator is used for this classical variable?
cards.back:
Use the symmetrized operator
\[
\frac{1}{2}\left[xp+(xp)^\dagger\right]=\frac{1}{2}(xp+px),
\]
where \(x^\dagger=x\), \(p^\dagger=p\), and \((AB)^\dagger=B^\dagger A^\dagger\).
cards.source_ref: Fitzpatrick: Quantum Mechanics, pp. 48-49, §Momentum Representation

cards.front: In the position representation for a continuous quantum variable, what probability density is assigned to measuring position x = x′, and what is the immediate post-measurement wavefunction if that outcome occurs?
