It is the smallest possible measured time between the events; observers in inertial frames moving relative to that rest frame measure a lengthened, or dilated, time interval.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 3, pp. 190-191, §5.3 Time Dilation

cards.front: In special relativity, what is proper length, and how does a length measured by an observer moving relative to the object compare to it?
cards.back: Proper length is the distance between two points measured by an observer at rest relative to both points. Length contraction is the decrease in observed length from the proper length when the object is observed in a frame where it is traveling at speed v. The proper length is the longest measurement; any observer moving relative to the system measures a shorter length.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 3, p. 232, §5.3 Time Dilation

cards.front: In special relativity, if two spatially separated events are simultaneous in one inertial frame, must they be simultaneous in another inertial frame moving relative to the first?
cards.back: No. Simultaneity depends on the frame of reference: the same two events can occur at the same time for a ground observer but at different times for a passenger in another inertial frame. Both observers’ conclusions are equally valid because they use different time axes for the same events.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 3, pp. 212-213, §5.5 The Lorentz Transformation

cards.front: In the spacetime-diagram view of special relativity, how should Lorentz transformations between inertial frames be interpreted, and what do they imply about simultaneity?
cards.back: Lorentz transformations correspond to rotations of axes in four-dimensional spacetime. Different inertial observers use different time axes for the same events, so events that are simultaneous in one frame can occur at different times in another. Both descriptions are equally valid; the effect reflects properties of space and time itself.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 3, pp. 212-213, §5.5 The Lorentz Transformation

cards.front: In special relativity, what quantity between two events is invariant under a Lorentz transformation, and what is it analogous to in ordinary space?
cards.back: The space-time interval between two events is invariant under a Lorentz transformation. This is analogous to how the ordinary distance between two points is invariant under a rotation of spatial coordinate axes.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 3, pp. 207-208, §5.5 The Lorentz Transformation

cards.front: Relativistic energy: What happens to the kinetic energy of an object with mass as its speed approaches the speed of light, and what is the low-speed limit?
cards.back: As the speed of an object with mass approaches c, its relativistic kinetic energy increases without bound; an infinite amount of work/energy input would be required to reach the speed of light, so it can only approach c, not attain it. At low speeds, relativistic kinetic energy reduces to the classical kinetic energy expression.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 3, pp. 222-223, §5.9 Relativistic Energy

cards.front: A spaceship heads directly toward Earth at 0.500c and emits a laser beam toward Earth. The light is measured to leave the ship at speed c. What speed does an Earth observer measure for the light?
cards.back: The Earth observer measures the light approaching at speed c, not 1.500c. At relativistic speeds, simple/Galilean velocity addition is not valid; relativistic velocity transformation preserves the fact that the speed of light is independent of the relative motion of source and observer.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 3, pp. 214-215, §5.6 Relativistic Velocity Transformation

cards.front: In special relativity, for two events that occur at the same location in one inertial frame, which observer measures the proper time, and how does a relatively moving inertial observer’s measured time interval compare?
cards.back: The proper time is measured in the frame where the start and end of the interval occur at the same location, the rest frame of the events. Any inertial observer moving relative to that frame measures a longer elapsed time interval; proper time is the shortest measure of the interval.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 3, p. 232, §5.3 Time Dilation

cards.front: Length contraction: For the Earth–Alpha Centauri distance, which observer measures the proper distance, and what does the astronaut measure?
cards.back: The earthbound observer measures the proper distance because Earth and Alpha Centauri are approximately stationary in that frame. The astronaut sees Earth and Alpha Centauri moving past at the same velocity, so she measures the contracted length: a shorter distance than the proper distance.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 3, pp. 200-201, §5.4 Length Contraction

cards.front: In the Galilean transformation between inertial frames, what assumption is made about time measurements in the two frames?
cards.back: It assumes that time measurements made by observers in both inertial frames are the same. Special relativity replaces this Newtonian view with Lorentz transformations, which are consistent with the postulates of relativity.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 3, p. 203, §5.5 The Lorentz Transformation

cards.front: In special relativity, what specifies an event in an inertial frame, and what do Lorentz transformation equations relate?
cards.back: An event is specified by its location and time coordinates \((x,y,z,t)\) relative to an inertial frame. Lorentz transformation equations give the space and time coordinates of events in one inertial reference frame in terms of those in another, in a way consistent with special relativity.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 3, p. 203, §5.5 The Lorentz Transformation

cards.front: Why are Galilean transformations replaced by Lorentz transformations in special relativity, and what condition can be used to obtain the Lorentz transformation?
cards.back: Galilean transformations are classical/nonrelativistic: they assume lengths are unchanged and that a single universal time applies in all inertial frames. This conflicts with the postulates of special relativity. The Lorentz transformation is the relativistically correct replacement and can be obtained by requiring an expanding spherical light signal to have the same shape and speed of growth, c, in both reference frames.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 3, p. 232, §5.3 Time Dilation

cards.front: In special relativity, how does a particle’s total energy relate to its momentum at rest and as it is accelerated?
cards.back: At rest, the particle’s momentum is zero, so its total energy is its rest energy. As the particle is accelerated, its relativistic momentum increases, which increases its total energy. At sufficiently high velocities, the rest-energy contribution becomes negligible compared with the momentum term.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 3, pp. 228-229, §5.9 Relativistic Energy

cards.front: In special relativity, what does “mass” mean when discussing relativistic momentum, and under what condition is relativistic momentum conserved?
cards.back: “Mass” means rest mass: the mass measured by an observer at rest relative to the object. Relativistic momentum is defined so that momentum conservation holds in all inertial frames; whenever the net external force on a system is zero, relativistic momentum is conserved.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 3, pp. 220-221, §5.8 Relativistic Momentum

cards.front: In relativistic kinematics, what fundamental speed limit must the result for any massive object's motion respect?
cards.back: A massive object's velocity can only approach the speed of light c; it cannot reach c. No object with mass can attain the speed of light.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 3, pp. 223-224, §5.9 Relativistic Energy

cards.front: Time dilation: For a muon moving at constant velocity relative to Earth and then decaying, which observer measures the proper time for the decay, and how does the lifetime measured by an earthbound observer compare?
cards.back: The clock moving with the muon, its internal clock, measures the proper time of the decay process. An earthbound observer measures the muon to live longer than this proper time.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 3, pp. 193-194, §5.3 Time Dilation

cards.front: In special relativity, who measures an object's greater length: an observer moving with the object or an observer moving relative to it? What is the name of this length?
cards.back: The observer moving with the object measures the greater length. This length is the object's proper length; an observer moving relative to the object sees it length-contracted.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 3, pp. 233-234, §5.6 Relativistic Velocity Transformation

cards.front: In a spacetime diagram, what does the Lorentz transformation do to the space and time axes, and what remains unchanged?
cards.back: It produces new space and time axes that are “rotated” in a scissors-like way relative to the original axes. The edge of the light cone is unaffected: the light cone is the same for both inertial frames, consistent with the postulate that light has the same speed in all inertial frames. As a result, simultaneity of separated events can depend on the reference frame.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 3, pp. 211-212, §5.5 The Lorentz Transformation

cards.front: A measured value is reported as \(A = 5.1\,\mathrm{lb}\) with uncertainty \(\delta A = 0.3\,\mathrm{lb}\). What is the percent uncertainty?
cards.back: Percent uncertainty is the ratio of the uncertainty to the measured value, expressed as a percentage: \(\left(\frac{\delta A}{A}\right)\times 100\% = \left(\frac{0.3}{5.1}\right)\times 100\% \approx 5.9\%\).
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 1, pp. 39-40, §1.5 Estimates and Fermi Calculations

cards.front: A measurement is reported as \(A = 5.1\,\mathrm{lb}\) with uncertainty \(\delta A = 0.3\,\mathrm{lb}\). What is the percent uncertainty?
cards.back: Percent uncertainty is the ratio of the uncertainty to the measured value, expressed as a percentage: \(\left(\frac{\delta A}{A}\right)\times 100\% = \left(\frac{0.3}{5.1}\right)\times 100\% \approx 5.9\%\).
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 1, pp. 39-40, §1.5 Estimates and Fermi Calculations

cards.front: For a two-state counting experiment with statistically independent observations, outcomes 1 and 2 have probabilities p and q with p+q=1. What is the probability of observing outcome 1 exactly n1 times in N observations, regardless of order?
cards.back: Use the binomial form: \(P_N(n_1) = C_{N}^{n_1, N-n_1} p^{n_1} q^{N-n_1}\), where \(C_{N}^{n_1, N-n_1}\) counts the number of distinct arrangements of \(n_1\) occurrences of outcome 1 and \(N-n_1\) occurrences of outcome 2. This follows from multiplying probabilities for independent “and” events and adding over alternative “or” arrangements.
cards.source_ref: Fitzpatrick: Thermodynamics and Statistical Mechanics, pp. 12-13, §The two-state system

cards.front: A six-sided die is thrown twice in statistically independent observations. What is the probability of throwing a one and then a two?
cards.back: For statistically independent observations, the joint probability is the product of the individual probabilities: \(P(1\ \mathrm{then}\ 2) = P(1)P(2) = \left(\tfrac{1}{6}\right)\left(\tfrac{1}{6}\right) = \tfrac{1}{36}\).
cards.source_ref: Fitzpatrick: Thermodynamics and Statistical Mechanics, pp. 11-12, §Combining probabilities

cards.front: A measured quantity is reported as \(A = 5.1\,\mathrm{lb}\) with uncertainty \(\delta A = 0.3\,\mathrm{lb}\). What is the percent uncertainty?
cards.back: Percent uncertainty is the ratio of the uncertainty to the measured value, expressed as a percentage: \((\delta A/A) \times 100\%\). Here, \((0.3/5.1) \times 100\% \approx 5.9\%\).
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 1, pp. 39-40, §1.5 Estimates and Fermi Calculations

cards.front: A resistor’s resistance is found from measured voltage and current using R = V/I. If the voltage measurement has 2% uncertainty and the current measurement has 3% uncertainty, what is the percent uncertainty in R?
cards.back: For a quantity calculated by division, the percent uncertainties add. Therefore, percent uncertainty in R = 2% + 3% = 5%.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 1, p. 45, §1.1 The Scope and Scale of Physics

cards.front: In data/error analysis, what is the difference between precision and accuracy, and what quantities measure each?
cards.back: Precision describes how closely repeated measurements agree with each other; it is quantified by the uncertainty, which measures how much measured values deviate from one another. Accuracy describes how close a measured value is to an accepted reference value; it is quantified by the discrepancy, the difference between the measured value and the accepted or expected value.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 1, p. 39, §1.5 Estimates and Fermi Calculations

cards.front: In a Compton-scattering radiation detection experiment, what is measured for each scattering angle, and how does the intensity-vs-wavelength data reveal the Compton shift?
cards.back: For a known incident X-ray beam, the detector measures the intensity and wavelength of the scattered beam at a chosen scattering angle. The data are plotted as scattered intensity versus scattered wavelength. For scattering angles other than zero, two intensity peaks are observed: one at the incident wavelength and one at a different scattered wavelength. The separation between these peaks is the Compton shift, and it depends on the scattering angle.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 3, pp. 257-258, §6.3 The Compton Effect

cards.front: In a high-energy particle detector, what two detector methods are used to measure charged particles and particle energies?
cards.back: Charged particles are measured by bending them in a circle with a magnetic field. Particle energies are measured using calorimeters that absorb the particles.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 3, p. 534, §11.1 Introduction to Particle Physics

cards.front: In a Michelson interferometer using 630-nm red laser light, one fringe moves past a fixed cross-hair when one mirror is displaced. How far did the mirror move?
cards.back: Moving the mirror by a distance \(\Delta x\) changes the path difference by \(2\Delta x\). One fringe crossing corresponds to a path-difference change of one wavelength, so \(2\Delta x = \lambda\). Thus \(\Delta x = \lambda/2 = 630\,\mathrm{nm} / 2 = 315\,\mathrm{nm}\).
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 3, pp. 134-135, §3.5 The Michelson Interferometer

cards.front: In a Michelson interferometer measurement of a gas’s refractive index, why can the fringe count have uncertainty even though the number of fringes is an integer?
cards.back: Because in practice it is easy to lose track while counting fringes. Thus the reported integer fringe count can have an uncertainty, and that uncertainty propagates into the inferred refractive index. This matters because gas refractive indices are very close to that of vacuum, so interferometry is used as a sensitive technique for detecting the small difference.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 3, pp. 135-136, §3.5 The Michelson Interferometer

cards.front: Dimensional analysis: What two conditions must an equation satisfy to be dimensionally consistent?
cards.back: Every term in the equation must have the same dimensions, and the arguments of standard mathematical functions (such as trigonometric, logarithmic, or exponential functions) must be dimensionless pure numbers.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 1, p. 33, §1.4 Dimensional Analysis

cards.front: For a macroscopic thermodynamic system with N particles, how do statistical deviations from average values scale with N, and what does this imply for one mole of gas?
cards.back: Statistical deviations scale approximately as \(1/\sqrt{N}\). For one mole of gas, the deviations from average pressure, volume, or temperature are about \(10^{-12}\), so they are usually too small to measure and the statistical averages are treated as exact physical quantities.
cards.source_ref: Fitzpatrick: Thermodynamics and Statistical Mechanics, p. 6, §Microscopic and macroscopic systems

cards.front: For a complete set of mutually possible outcomes \(X_i\) of an observation, what normalization condition must the probabilities satisfy?
cards.back: They must sum to unity: \(\sum_{i=1}^{M} P(X_i)=1\). This expresses that an observation must result in one of the possible outcomes.
cards.source_ref: Fitzpatrick: Quantum Mechanics, p. 8, §What is Probability?

cards.front: In binomial counting statistics, an outcome has probability p = 0.25 in each of N = 1000 observations. Using q = 1 − p, what are the mean count and standard deviation of the count?
cards.back: Mean count: \(\bar{n} = Np = 250\). Standard deviation: \(\Delta n = \sqrt{Npq} = \sqrt{1000\cdot 0.25\cdot 0.75} \approx 13.7\).
cards.source_ref: Fitzpatrick: Thermodynamics and Statistical Mechanics, p. 16, §The mean, variance, and standard deviation

cards.front: In a two-outcome experiment with probabilities p for outcome 1 and q for outcome 2, what is the binomial probability of getting exactly n1 occurrences of outcome 1 in N observations?
cards.back: \(P_N(n_1) = \left[\frac{N!}{n_1!(N-n_1)!}\right] p^{n_1} q^{N-n_1}\). The distribution is normalized because \(\sum_{n_1=0}^N P_N(n_1) = (p + q)^N = 1\), since \(p + q = 1\).
cards.source_ref: Fitzpatrick: Thermodynamics and Statistical Mechanics, p. 16, §The mean, variance, and standard deviation

cards.front: A poll (or statistical sample) uses N = 1000 unbiased observations. Using the statistical scaling stated in the corpus, what is the order of the intrinsic error, and would a 1 percentage point difference be meaningful?
cards.back: The intrinsic statistical error is of order \(1/\sqrt{N}\). For \(N = 1000\), this is about \(0.032\), or \(3\%\). Therefore, a \(1\) percentage point difference is smaller than the intrinsic error and is statistically meaningless by this estimate.
cards.source_ref: Fitzpatrick: Thermodynamics and Statistical Mechanics, pp. 5-6, §The need for a statistical approach

cards.front: In circuit measurements, which instrument measures current, and which measures potential difference?
cards.back: An ammeter measures current. A voltmeter measures voltage, i.e., the potential difference between two points in an electric circuit, measured in volts.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 2, p. 462, §10.1 Electromotive Force

cards.front: Instrumentation: To measure the voltage across a circuit element, how should a voltmeter be connected, and what resistance should it have? Why?
cards.back: Connect the voltmeter in parallel with the device being measured. A parallel connection is used because objects in parallel experience the same potential difference. Since voltmeters are connected in parallel, the voltmeter must have a very large resistance.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 2, pp. 450-451, §10.4 Electrical Measuring Instruments

cards.front: Diagnostic radiation dose data: A CT abdomen exam has a typical effective dose of 10.0 mSv, while a chest X-ray has 0.02 mSv. By what factor is the CT abdomen dose larger?
cards.back: Use the ratio of effective doses: (10.0 mSv)/(0.02 mSv) = 500. A typical CT abdomen dose is 500 times larger than a typical chest X-ray dose.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 3, pp. 482-483, §10.6 Nuclear Fusion

cards.front: For two charged particles interacting electrostatically, what qualitative dependence should you expect on separation and impact parameter in a scattering experiment?
cards.back: The electromagnetic interaction applies to particles with charge; in static electricity, the electric force varies as the product of the charges and inversely as the square of their separation. In the equivalent one-body central-potential scattering picture, the scattering angle \(\theta\) is a decreasing function of impact parameter \(b\): \(b = 0\) is a head-on collision with \(\theta = \pi\), while large \(b\) gives \(\theta \approx 0\).
cards.source_ref: Fitzpatrick: Newtonian Dynamics, pp. 80-81, §6.4 Scattering in the Center of Mass Frame

cards.front: In nuclear notation, what do the atomic number Z, neutron number N, and mass number A count, and how are they related?
cards.back: Z is the number of protons, N is the number of neutrons, and A is the total number of nucleons. Therefore, A = Z + N.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 3, pp. 441-442, §10.1 Properties of Nuclei

cards.front: In alpha decay, how do the parent nucleus’s atomic number Z and mass number A change in the daughter nucleus?
cards.back: The nucleus loses two protons and two neutrons: the atomic number decreases by 2 and the mass number decreases by 4.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 3, pp. 456-457, §10.4 Nuclear Reactions

cards.front: In a U-235 fission chain reaction/reactor, what roles do emitted neutrons, water, and cadmium control rods play?
cards.back: A neutron can induce U-235 fission, splitting the nucleus into lighter nuclei and emitting typically two or three neutrons. Those neutrons can induce further fissions, enabling a self-sustaining chain reaction. In a reactor, the produced neutrons move too fast to initiate fission efficiently, so water is used to slow/thermalize them. Cadmium control rods absorb neutrons, adjusting the neutron flux and thereby controlling the reaction rate.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 3, p. 468, §10.5 Fission

cards.front: In nuclear physics, what is nuclear fusion, and under what condition is it exothermic for light nuclei?
cards.back: Nuclear fusion is the combining of two lighter nuclei to form a larger nucleus. Energy is released when light nuclei fuse to form medium-mass nuclei; the released energy is called the Q value, and the reaction is exothermic when Q > 0.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 3, p. 486, §10.5 Fission

cards.front: Why can’t an electron and positron at rest annihilate into a single photon?
cards.back: Because producing a single photon would violate conservation of energy and linear momentum. Electron–positron annihilation instead produces two photons.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 3, pp. 498-499, §11.1 Introduction to Particle Physics

cards.front: How are quarks and leptons distinguished by force interactions, and what are mesons and baryons?
cards.back: Quarks participate in the strong force; leptons do not. Quarks bind via the strong force into hadrons: mesons consist of two quarks, while baryons consist of three quarks. Examples of baryons include the proton and neutron; a proton is two up quarks and one down quark, and a neutron is one up quark and two down quarks.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 3, pp. 495-496, §11.1 Introduction to Particle Physics

cards.front: Why do heavier nuclei generally require excess neutrons for stability?
cards.back: Protons in a nucleus repel each other through the long-range electrostatic (Coulomb) force, while the short-range strong nuclear force attracts neighboring nucleons. In heavy nuclei, extra neutrons add attractive strong-force interactions without adding proton-proton Coulomb repulsion, helping keep the nucleus from breaking apart.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 3, p. 445, §10.1 Properties of Nuclei

cards.front: In alpha decay, how do the parent nucleus’s atomic number Z and mass number A change?
cards.back: The nucleus emits an alpha particle, losing two protons and two neutrons. Therefore the daughter nucleus has atomic number Z − 2 and mass number A − 4.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 3, pp. 456-457, §10.4 Nuclear Reactions

cards.front: In nuclear fission, how does the total mass of the product nuclei compare with the mass of the reactants, and what curve is used to understand the energy change?
cards.back: In nuclear fission, the sum of the masses of the product nuclei is less than the mass of the reactants. The energy change is understood in terms of the binding energy per nucleon curve.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 3, p. 486, §10.5 Fission

cards.front: In nuclear physics, what is nuclear fusion, and why can it release energy?
cards.back: Nuclear fusion is the combining of lighter nuclei to make a heavier/larger nucleus. Energy is released when light nuclei fuse to form medium-mass nuclei because the product nuclei have a larger binding energy per nucleon than the nuclei that were fused; the energy difference is carried away as radiation. The energy released is called the Q value.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 3, p. 486, §10.5 Fission

cards.front: In particle reactions, why can an electron and positron at rest not annihilate into just one photon?
cards.back: Because producing a single photon would violate conservation of linear momentum. Energy, momentum, and angular momentum are conserved in particle interactions.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 3, p. 499, §11.2 Particle Conservation Laws

cards.front: In an ionic sodium chloride crystal, what crystal structure gives the lowest-energy arrangement, and how many nearest neighbors of the opposite ion species does each ion have?
cards.back: Sodium chloride ions form a face-centered cubic (FCC) structure. In this FCC structure, each ion is closest to six ions of the other species.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 3, pp. 403-404, §9.3 Bonding in Crystalline Solids

cards.front: In X-ray crystallography, what produces the diffraction pattern, and what can analysis of the pattern tell you about the sample?
cards.back: The pattern is produced by interference of X-rays scattered/reflected from planes of atoms in a crystal lattice. Constructive-interference maxima occur at Bragg angles. Analyzing the diffraction pattern gives information about the crystal structure, including atomic arrangements; different families of crystal planes can contribute to a complex pattern.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 3, p. 168, §4.6 X-Ray Diffraction

cards.front: For a solid element at ordinary temperatures, what does the Dulong–Petit law predict for the molar specific heat, and what simple model motivates it?
cards.back: Model each atom in the solid as attached to neighboring atoms by springs. Each atom has six degrees of freedom: kinetic and potential energy for motion in each of the x, y, and z directions. Equipartition then predicts the molar specific heat of a metal is 3R. This Dulong–Petit result works fairly well experimentally at room temperature, but fails at low temperatures for quantum-mechanical reasons.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 2, pp. 98-99, §2.3 Heat Capacity and Equipartition of Energy

cards.front: In the free electron model of a metal, how are conduction electrons modeled, and what is the Fermi energy?
cards.back: A metal is modeled as a system of essentially free electrons that wander throughout the solid, like an electron gas. In the simple model, electrons move in an infinite square well, electron-electron interactions are ignored, and Pauli exclusion is respected: energy levels fill from lowest to highest, two electrons per level with opposite spin. The highest filled energy level is called the Fermi energy.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 3, p. 409, §9.4 Free Electron Model of Metals

cards.front: In semiconductor doping, where do n-type and p-type impurity levels appear relative to the conduction and valence bands?
cards.back: n-type doping creates and fills new energy levels just below the conduction band. p-type doping creates new energy levels just above the valence band.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 3, p. 435, §9.3 Bonding in Crystalline Solids

cards.front: In superconductors, why is the Meissner effect not simply a consequence of zero electrical resistance?
cards.back: If a material in a magnetic field merely had its resistivity drop to zero, Ohm’s law would require the internal electric field to be zero for a finite current density. By Faraday’s law, zero electric field implies the magnetic flux through the sample cannot change, so the magnetic field lines would not necessarily be expelled. The Meissner effect, expulsion of magnetic field so the net magnetic field inside is zero, is therefore a special property of superconductors, not just a result of zero resistance.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 3, pp. 426-427, §9.8 Superconductivity

cards.front: For an NaCl crystal in its lowest-energy FCC structure, how many nearest neighbors of the opposite ion species does each ion have, and what is the approximate Madelung constant for FCC ionic solids?
cards.back: Each ion is closest to 6 ions of the other species. For FCC ionic solids, the Madelung constant is approximately 1.75.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 3, pp. 403-404, §9.3 Bonding in Crystalline Solids

cards.front: In X-ray diffraction from a crystalline solid, what produces the diffraction/interference pattern, and what structural information can it reveal?
cards.back: X-rays incident on a crystalline solid can reflect from the surface and from planes of atoms below the surface. Interference between these reflected X-rays at different incidence angles produces an X-ray diffraction pattern. Analysis of the pattern can provide detailed information about the crystal’s structure, including families of Bragg planes.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 3, pp. 380-381, §8.5 Atomic Spectra and X-rays

cards.front: For a solid whose lattice vibrations behave classically, what molar heat capacity at constant volume is predicted by equipartition?
cards.back: Each normal mode has mean energy \(kT\), giving mean internal energy per mole \(E = 3\nu RT\). Thus \(c_V = \frac{1}{\nu}\left(\frac{\partial E}{\partial T}\right)_V = 3R \approx 24.9\,\mathrm{J}\,\mathrm{mol}^{-1}\,\mathrm{degree}^{-1}\). This is Dulong-Petit behavior and holds fairly well for many metals at room temperature.
cards.source_ref: Fitzpatrick: Thermodynamics and Statistical Mechanics, p. 150, §Specific heats of solids

cards.front: In band theory of solids, how does doping alter a semiconductor’s energy structure for n-type vs. p-type material?
cards.back: Doping substitutes one type of atom for another, altering the semiconductor’s energy structure. n-type doping creates and fills new energy levels just below the conduction band. p-type doping creates new energy levels just above the valence band.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 3, p. 435, §9.3 Bonding in Crystalline Solids

cards.front: In band theory of doped semiconductors, where do n-type and p-type doping create impurity energy levels?
cards.back: n-type doping creates and fills new energy levels just below the conduction band. p-type doping creates new energy levels just above the valence band; acceptor impurities can create mobile holes in the valence band, making holes the primary positive charge carriers.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 3, pp. 418-419, §9.6 Semiconductors and Doping

cards.front: Astrophysics: The cosmological expansion rate is approximately 70 km/s/Mpc. What recessional velocity is expected for a galaxy 100 Mpc away, and how can such velocities be measured?
cards.back: \(v \approx (70\,\mathrm{km/s/Mpc})(100\,\mathrm{Mpc}) = 7.0 \times 10^3\,\mathrm{km/s}\). Recessional velocities can be measured using the Doppler shift of light; farther galaxies have greater speeds.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 3, pp. 534-535, §11.1 Introduction to Particle Physics

cards.front: In the circular restricted three-body problem (e.g., Sun–Jupiter–comet with the comet’s mass negligible), is the comet’s energy conserved? What conserved quantity remains?
cards.back: No. The comet’s energy is not conserved because the other two masses are moving, and its angular momentum h is not a constant of the motion. The conserved quantity is the Jacobi integral C; in this problem, it is the only constant of the motion.
cards.source_ref: Fitzpatrick: Newtonian Dynamics, p. 203, §13.4 Tisserand Criterion

cards.front: For EM communication applications, what kind of electromagnetic wave carries most satellite-transmitted information, and how is computer data represented for transmission?
cards.back: Most satellite-transmitted information is carried on microwaves. Computer data is transmitted as a sequence of binary ones and zeros, with each one or zero constituting one bit of data.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 2, p. 694, §16.5 The Electromagnetic Spectrum

cards.front: Astrophysics/cosmology: What observational relation connects a galaxy’s distance to its recessional velocity, and how are these velocities measured?
cards.back: In the expanding universe, more distant galaxies have greater recessional velocities. The expansion rate is measured to be approximately 70 km/s/Mpc, and the recessional velocities are measured using the Doppler shift of light.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 3, pp. 534-535, §11.1 Introduction to Particle Physics

cards.front: In the circular restricted three-body problem, is the comet/test mass’s energy conserved? What conserved quantity replaces it?
cards.back: No. The test mass’s energy per unit mass is not conserved because the other two masses are moving, and its angular momentum \(h\) is not constant. The conserved quantity is the Jacobi integral \(C\); in fact, it is the only constant of motion in the circular restricted three-body problem. The relation given is \(E = \vec{\omega} \cdot \vec{h} - \frac{C}{2}\).
cards.source_ref: Fitzpatrick: Newtonian Dynamics, p. 203, §13.4 Tisserand Criterion

cards.front: In the context of computer/data-storage applications, what role does magnetism play according to OpenStax?
cards.back: Magnetism is used in computer hard drives and tape recording; these are listed as applications of magnetic fields/magnetic properties.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 2, pp. 477-478, §11.1 Magnetism and Its Historical Discoveries

cards.front: Astrophysics: The expansion rate of the universe is approximately 70 km/s/Mpc. Using this rate, what recessional speed is expected for a galaxy 100 Mpc away?
cards.back: Multiply the expansion rate by distance: (70 km/s/Mpc)(100 Mpc) = 7000 km/s. More distant galaxies have greater recessional speeds, which can be measured using the Doppler shift of light.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 3, pp. 534-535, §11.1 Introduction to Particle Physics

cards.front: In the circular restricted three-body problem, is the small body's energy per unit mass generally conserved? What conserved quantity is identified instead?
cards.back: No. The energy per unit mass E is not generally conserved because the other two masses are moving, and the angular momentum h of the small body is not a constant of motion. The conserved quantity identified is the Jacobi integral C; in this problem it is the only constant of the motion.
cards.source_ref: Fitzpatrick: Newtonian Dynamics, p. 203, §13.4 Tisserand Criterion

cards.front: In gas chromatography–mass spectrometry, what roles do the two stages play in identifying an unknown substance?
cards.back: The gas chromatography portion breaks down the substance; the mass spectrometer then separates the resulting ionized molecules. This combined technique is widely used to identify unknown substances.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 2, pp. 497-498, §11.7 Applications of Magnetic Forces and Fields

cards.front: In Hubble’s law, what relationship is observed between a galaxy’s distance from Earth and its recession velocity/redshift?
cards.back: Galaxies appear to move away from Earth, and their recession velocity is proportional to their distance from Earth. In a Hubble graph, redshift versus distance is approximately linear, with larger redshifts at greater distances.
cards.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 3, p. 521, §11.6 The Big Bang

problems.stem: A small toy car starts from rest on a frictionless track at height h above the bottom of a vertical loop-the-loop of radius R. The track constrains the car only by a normal force, and the car must remain in contact with the track throughout the loop. What is the minimum value of h?

distractors.label: A
distractors.rationale: Error: Apply only the top-of-loop contact condition and omit the gravitational work needed to raise the car from the bottom to the top. At the threshold of contact, \(mg = \frac{mv^2}{R}\), so \(v^2 = gR\). Equating the initial energy only to the kinetic energy associated with this speed gives \(mgh = \tfrac{1}{2}mgR\), producing \(h = \frac{R}{2}\).

distractors.label: B
distractors.rationale: Error: Use the contact condition correctly but take the top of the loop to be only one radius above the bottom instead of two radii. Then energy gives \(mgh = mgR + \tfrac{1}{2}mgR\), producing \(h = \tfrac{3R}{2}\).

distractors.label: C
distractors.rationale: Error: Assume that barely making it around the loop means the car has zero speed at the top. Then the initial gravitational energy is set equal only to the increase in gravitational potential energy to the top: mgh = mg(2R), producing h = 2R.

distractors.label: E
distractors.rationale: Error: Require the normal force at the top to equal the weight, rather than recognizing that the limiting contact case has the normal force just vanish. This gives \(mg + mg = mv^2/R\), so \(v^2 = 2gR\). Energy then gives \(mgh = mg(2R) + \tfrac{1}{2}m(2gR)\), producing \(h = 3R\).

solution_decomposition.subgoal: Identify the force condition at the top of the loop for the limiting case of contact.
solution_decomposition.rubric: At the top, gravity and the normal force point toward the center. For the minimum release height, the normal force is just zero, which fixes the smallest allowed speed at the top.

solution_decomposition.subgoal: Apply the work-energy theorem from release to the top of the loop.
solution_decomposition.rubric: The normal force does no work on a frictionless track because it is perpendicular to the displacement, so gravitational work accounts for the change in kinetic energy.

solution_decomposition.subgoal: Combine the top-of-loop speed condition with the vertical rise.
solution_decomposition.rubric: Use the height increase from the bottom to the top of the loop and the kinetic energy required at the top to solve for the release height in units of R.

problems.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 1, pp. 337-338, §7.3 Work-Energy Theorem

subproblems.prompt: Find the limiting contact condition at the top of a vertical loop.

variants.stem: A toy car is at the top of a frictionless vertical loop of radius \(0.90\,\text{m}\). In the limiting case where it is just about to lose contact, what is the minimum speed at the top? Use \(g=10\,\text{m/s}^2\).

distractor_rationales.B: This assumes the car can barely contact the track while momentarily at rest, but circular motion at the top still requires centripetal acceleration.
distractor_rationales.C: This uses \(v^2=2gR\), confusing the top-force condition with an energy drop through a height \(R\).
distractor_rationales.D: This uses \(gR\) directly as a speed, but \(gR\) has units of speed squared.
distractor_rationales.E: This is too small by a factor of \(\sqrt{2}\), as if only half the needed centripetal acceleration were required.

variants.explain_why: At the top, both gravity and the normal force point toward the center. For the limiting case, \(N=0\), so \(mg=mv^2/R\), giving \(v=\sqrt{gR}=\sqrt{10\cdot0.90}=3.0\,\text{m/s}\).
variants.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 1, p. 338, §7.3 Work-Energy Theorem

variants.stem: A toy car is at the top of a frictionless vertical loop of radius \(0.40\,\text{m}\). In the limiting case where contact is just maintained, what is the minimum speed at the top? Use \(g=10\,\text{m/s}^2\).

distractor_rationales.A: This treats \(gR\) as a speed instead of as speed squared.
distractor_rationales.B: This uses \(v^2=2gR\), which is an energy-style expression, not the top-of-loop force condition.
distractor_rationales.D: This is too small by a factor of \(\sqrt{2}\), as if the needed centripetal force were half as large.
distractor_rationales.E: This ignores that the car needs nonzero centripetal acceleration at the top of the loop.

variants.explain_why: At the top in the limiting case, the normal force is zero, so gravity alone supplies the centripetal force: \(mg=mv^2/R\). Thus \(v=\sqrt{gR}=\sqrt{10\cdot0.40}=2.0\,\text{m/s}\).
variants.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 1, p. 338, §7.3 Work-Energy Theorem

subproblems.prompt: Use the work-energy theorem from release to the top of the loop, noting that the normal force does no work.

variants.stem: A toy car starts from rest at height \(H=1.50\,\text{m}\) above the bottom of a frictionless loop of radius \(R=0.50\,\text{m}\). What is \(v_{\text{top}}^2\) at the top of the loop? Use \(g=10\,\text{m/s}^2\).

distractor_rationales.A: This misses the factor of 2 in \(\frac12 mv^2=mg\Delta h\).
distractor_rationales.C: This subtracts only one radius from the release height instead of using the top height \(2R\).
distractor_rationales.D: This uses the full release height as though the final point were the bottom, not the top of the loop.
distractor_rationales.E: This misuses the fact that the normal force does no work; gravity still changes the kinetic energy.

variants.explain_why: The top of the loop is at height \(2R=1.00\,\text{m}\). The vertical drop from release to the top is \(1.50-1.00=0.50\,\text{m}\), so \(\frac12 mv^2=mg(0.50)\), giving \(v^2=10\,\text{m}^2/\text{s}^2\).
variants.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 1, p. 386, §8.4 Potential Energy Diagrams and Stability

variants.stem: A toy car starts from rest at height \(H=1.20\,\text{m}\) above the bottom of a frictionless loop of radius \(R=0.40\,\text{m}\). What is \(v_{\text{top}}^2\) at the top of the loop? Use \(g=10\,\text{m/s}^2\).

distractor_rationales.A: This misses the factor of 2 in the work-energy relation.
distractor_rationales.B: This uses a height drop of \(H-R\) instead of \(H-2R\).
distractor_rationales.C: This uses the full height above the bottom, as if the car ended at the bottom rather than at the top.
distractor_rationales.D: This incorrectly concludes that no work is done at all; only the normal force does no work, while gravity does.

variants.explain_why: The top of the loop is at \(2R=0.80\,\text{m}\). The drop from release to the top is \(1.20-0.80=0.40\,\text{m}\). Since the normal force does no work, \(\frac12 mv^2=mg(0.40)\), so \(v^2=8\,\text{m}^2/\text{s}^2\).
variants.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 1, p. 386, §8.4 Potential Energy Diagrams and Stability

subproblems.prompt: Combine the required top speed with the vertical rise to the top of the loop.

variants.stem: For a frictionless loop of radius \(0.60\,\text{m}\), suppose the limiting contact condition at the top requires \(v_{\text{top}}^2=6.0\,\text{m}^2/\text{s}^2\). If the car starts from rest, what release height above the bottom gives that top speed? Use \(g=10\,\text{m/s}^2\).

distractor_rationales.A: This includes only the height of the top of the loop and ignores the kinetic energy still needed there.
distractor_rationales.C: This subtracts the kinetic-energy height instead of adding it.
distractor_rationales.D: This uses \(v^2/g\) instead of \(v^2/(2g)\) for the kinetic-energy height.
distractor_rationales.E: This gives only the extra height needed for kinetic energy, omitting the height of the top of the loop.

variants.explain_why: The top is \(2R=1.20\,\text{m}\) above the bottom. The kinetic energy needed there corresponds to an additional height \(v^2/(2g)=6.0/20=0.30\,\text{m}\), so the release height is \(1.20+0.30=1.50\,\text{m}\).
variants.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 1, p. 338, §7.3 Work-Energy Theorem

variants.stem: For a frictionless loop of radius \(0.75\,\text{m}\), suppose the limiting contact condition at the top requires \(v_{\text{top}}^2=7.5\,\text{m}^2/\text{s}^2\). If the car starts from rest, what release height above the bottom gives that top speed? Use \(g=10\,\text{m/s}^2\).

distractor_rationales.A: This subtracts the kinetic-energy height from the top height rather than adding it.
distractor_rationales.B: This is just the height of the top of the loop, leaving zero speed there.
distractor_rationales.C: This is only the kinetic-energy height and omits the vertical rise to the top.
distractor_rationales.D: This uses \(v^2/g\) rather than \(v^2/(2g)\), doubling the kinetic-energy contribution.

variants.explain_why: The top is \(2R=1.50\,\text{m}\) above the bottom. The needed kinetic energy at the top corresponds to \(v^2/(2g)=7.5/20=0.375\,\text{m}\). Adding these gives \(1.875\,\text{m}\).
variants.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 1, pp. 386-387, §8.4 Potential Energy Diagrams and Stability

parent_variants.stem: A small toy cart starts from rest on a frictionless track at height \(h\) above the bottom of a vertical loop-the-loop of radius \(0.40\,\text{m}\). The track can only exert a normal force, and the cart must remain in contact throughout the loop. What is the minimum value of \(h\)?

problems.stem: A uniform spring of total mass M and equilibrium length L has its left end fixed. At one instant, its right end (at x = L) is moving in the +x direction with speed v, and the speed of a small spring element at position x is proportional to x. What is the total kinetic energy of the spring at that instant?

distractors.label: B
distractors.misconception: treating an extended object as a point object moving with the endpoint speed
distractors.rationale: If every mass element is incorrectly assigned speed v, then summing particle kinetic energies gives \(K=\frac12 Mv^2\).

distractors.label: C
distractors.misconception: using the average speed as though all mass elements have that speed
distractors.rationale: The mean speed along the spring is \(\bar u=v/2\). Substituting this single speed for every element gives \(K=\frac12 M(v/2)^2=\frac18 Mv^2\).

distractors.label: D
distractors.misconception: averaging speed instead of averaging speed squared inside kinetic energy
distractors.rationale: With \(u(x)=vx/L\), incorrectly replacing the needed average of \(u^2\) by \(v\langle u\rangle\) gives \(K=\frac12 M\,v(v/2)=\frac14 Mv^2\).

distractors.label: E
distractors.misconception: omitting the factor of one-half in the kinetic energy of each element
distractors.rationale: Using \(dK=u(x)^2\,dm\) rather than \(dK=\frac12 u(x)^2\,dm\), with \(u(x)=vx/L\) and \(dm=(M/L)dx\), gives \(K=\int_0^L (vx/L)^2(M/L)dx=\frac13 Mv^2\).

solution_decomposition.subgoal: Model the spring as a system of infinitesimal particles.
solution_decomposition.rubric: Use the corpus principle that the kinetic energy of a system is the sum of the kinetic energies of its constituent particles.

solution_decomposition.subgoal: Write the velocity field and mass element.
solution_decomposition.rubric: Because the fixed end has zero speed and the right end has speed v, use \(u(x)=vx/L\) for a uniform spring and \(dm=(M/L)dx\).

solution_decomposition.subgoal: Integrate the elemental kinetic energy.
solution_decomposition.rubric: Set up \(K=\int_0^L \frac12 u(x)^2\,dm\), keeping the square on the local speed before integrating over position.

problems.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 1, pp. 352-353, §7.4 Power

subproblems.prompt: Use the proportional-speed condition and the fixed-end boundary condition to write the local velocity field.

variants.stem: A uniform spring has length \(4.0\,\text{m}\). Its left end at \(x=0\) is fixed, and its right end at \(x=4.0\,\text{m}\) moves to the right at \(6.0\,\text{m/s}\). If the speed of a spring element is proportional to its position \(x\), what is \(u(x)\), and what is the speed at \(x=1.0\,\text{m}\)?

distractor_rationales.B: This reverses the boundary conditions, making the fixed end move fastest and the free end stationary.
distractor_rationales.C: This treats the spring like a rigid object with every element moving at the end speed.
distractor_rationales.D: This uses proportionality to \(x\) but forgets to normalize by the full length, so it would not give \(6.0\,\text{m/s}\) at \(x=4.0\,\text{m}\).
distractor_rationales.E: This incorrectly makes the speed proportional to \(x^2\), not to \(x\).

variants.explain_why: A proportional speed field has the form \(u(x)=Cx\). The fixed end gives \(u(0)=0\), and the right-end condition gives \(C(4.0)=6.0\), so \(C=6.0/4.0\).
variants.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 1, pp. 352-353, §7.4 Power

variants.stem: A uniform spring has length \(5.0\,\text{m}\). Its left end at \(x=0\) is fixed, and its right end at \(x=5.0\,\text{m}\) moves to the right at \(8.0\,\text{m/s}\). If each element’s speed is proportional to \(x\), what is \(u(x)\), and what is the speed at \(x=2.0\,\text{m}\)?

distractor_rationales.A: This puts the maximum speed at the fixed end instead of the moving end.
distractor_rationales.B: This ignores that different spring elements have different speeds.
distractor_rationales.D: This omits the division by the total length, so the right end would not have the specified speed.
distractor_rationales.E: This uses a quadratic position dependence even though the statement says the speed is proportional to position.

variants.explain_why: Since the speed is proportional to \(x\), write \(u(x)=Cx\). The condition \(u(5.0)=8.0\) gives \(C=8.0/5.0\), so at \(x=2.0\,\text{m}\), \(u=3.2\,\text{m/s}\).
variants.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 1, pp. 352-353, §7.4 Power

subproblems.prompt: Express the kinetic energy of one infinitesimal spring element using the local speed and the uniform mass density.

variants.stem: A uniform spring has mass \(12\,\text{kg}\) and length \(3.0\,\text{m}\). Its local speed is \(u(x)=2.0x\), with \(x\) in meters and \(u\) in \(\text{m/s}\). For a small element of width \(dx\) at position \(x\), what is \(dK\)?

distractor_rationales.B: This uses the speed linearly instead of using \(u^2\) in kinetic energy.
distractor_rationales.C: This omits the factor of \(1/2\) in \(dK=\frac12 u^2dm\).
distractor_rationales.D: This effectively uses the reciprocal of the mass density rather than \(dm=(M/L)dx\).
distractor_rationales.E: This uses the full spring mass for each infinitesimal element instead of the element mass \(dm\).

variants.explain_why: For a uniform spring, \(dm=(M/L)dx=(12/3.0)dx=4dx\). The kinetic energy of the element is \(dK=\frac12 u(x)^2dm=\frac12(2.0x)^2(4dx)=8x^2dx\), following the particle-sum idea for kinetic energy.
variants.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 1, pp. 352-353, §7.4 Power

variants.stem: A uniform spring has mass \(10\,\text{kg}\) and length \(5.0\,\text{m}\). Its local speed is \(u(x)=3.0x\), with \(x\) in meters and \(u\) in \(\text{m/s}\). For a small element of width \(dx\) at position \(x\), what is \(dK\)?

distractor_rationales.A: This fails to square the local speed in the kinetic-energy formula.
distractor_rationales.B: This is twice too large because it leaves out the factor \(1/2\).
distractor_rationales.C: This uses \(L/M\) instead of the uniform mass per length \(M/L\).
distractor_rationales.E: This treats the entire spring mass as if it belonged to each small element.

variants.explain_why: The uniform linear mass density is \(M/L=10/5.0=2.0\,\text{kg/m}\), so \(dm=2.0dx\). Thus \(dK=\frac12(3.0x)^2(2.0dx)=9x^2dx\).
variants.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 1, pp. 352-353, §7.4 Power

subproblems.prompt: Integrate the elemental kinetic energy over the spring, keeping the square on the local speed before integrating.

variants.stem: A uniform spring of mass \(9.0\,\text{kg}\) and length \(3.0\,\text{m}\) has its left end fixed. At an instant, the right end moves at \(6.0\,\text{m/s}\), and the speed varies linearly with position from the fixed end. What is the total kinetic energy at that instant?

distractor_rationales.B: This treats every part of the spring as moving at the right-end speed.
distractor_rationales.C: This uses the average speed and then squares it, rather than averaging \(u^2\) through the integral.
distractor_rationales.D: This averages the endpoint kinetic energies directly, which is not the same as integrating over position.
distractor_rationales.E: This is twice the correct integral result, corresponding to omitting the \(1/2\) in kinetic energy.

variants.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 1, pp. 352-353, §7.4 Power

variants.stem: A uniform spring of mass \(12\,\text{kg}\) and length \(6.0\,\text{m}\) has its left end fixed. At an instant, the right end moves at \(9.0\,\text{m/s}\), and the speed varies linearly with position from the fixed end. What is the total kinetic energy at that instant?

distractor_rationales.B: This assigns the maximum speed to the whole spring.
distractor_rationales.C: This computes kinetic energy using the average speed \(9.0/2\) for the whole mass.
distractor_rationales.D: This averages the zero-speed and maximum-speed endpoint kinetic energies instead of integrating all elements.
distractor_rationales.E: This misses the factor \(1/2\) in the elemental kinetic energy.

variants.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 1, pp. 352-353, §7.4 Power

parent_variants.stem: A uniform spring of total mass \(3.0\,\text{kg}\) and equilibrium length \(2.0\,\text{m}\) has its left end fixed. At one instant, its right end at \(x=2.0\,\text{m}\) is moving in the \(+x\) direction with speed \(4.0\,\text{m/s}\), and the speed of a small spring element at position \(x\) is proportional to \(x\). What is the total kinetic energy of the spring at that instant?

problems.stem: A particle of mass \(m\) and charge \(q\) starts from rest at the origin in the \(x\)-\(y\) plane. It is acted on only by a rotating electric field \(E_x = E_0 \cos(\omega t)\), \(E_y = E_0 \sin(\omega t)\). Assume \(qE_0 > 0\) and define \(\ell = \frac{qE_0}{m\omega^2}\). Which choice correctly gives the subsequent trajectory and the net work done by the electric force during one full field rotation?
choices: \(x=\ell(1-\cos\omega t)\), \(y=\ell(\omega t-\sin\omega t)\); the trajectory is a cycloid with cusps separated by \(2\pi\ell\), and the net work over one full rotation is \(0\).
\(x=\ell \sin \omega t\), \(y=\ell(1-\cos \omega t)\); the trajectory is a circle tangent to the origin, and the net work over one full rotation is \(0\).
\(x=\ell(1-\cos\omega t)\), \(y=-\ell \sin\omega t\); the trajectory is a closed circle tangent to the origin, and the net work over one full rotation is \(0\).
\(x=\ell \cos \omega t, y=\ell \sin \omega t\); the particle simply follows the rotating field direction on a circle centered at the origin, and the net work over one full rotation is \(0\).
\(x = \frac{qE_0}{2m}t^2\), \(y = 0\); the trajectory is a straight line, and the net work over one full rotation is \(\frac{2\pi^2q^2E_0^2}{m\omega^2}\).

distractors.label: B
distractors.rationale: Error: stop after one integration and interpret the velocity components, scaled by \(1/\omega\), as coordinates. Integrating the acceleration once gives \(v_x=\frac{qE_0}{m\omega}\sin\omega t\) and \(v_y=\frac{qE_0}{m\omega}(1-\cos\omega t)\); replacing \(v/\omega\) by \(r\) gives the circular expression listed in this choice.

distractors.label: C
distractors.misconception: lost integration constant in the y-velocity
distractors.rationale: Error: integrate \(a_y = \frac{qE_0}{m}\sin \omega t\) as though \(v_y = -\frac{qE_0}{m\omega}\cos \omega t\), omitting the constant required by the initial rest condition. A subsequent integration with only \(y(0)=0\) imposed gives \(y = -\ell \sin \omega t\), producing the closed-circle expression in this choice.

distractors.label: D
distractors.misconception: instantaneous following of the applied field
distractors.rationale: Error: assume the particle displacement is always parallel to the instantaneous electric field, so that \(\vec{r}\) is taken proportional to \(\cos\omega t\,\hat{x} + \sin\omega t\,\hat{y}\). This makes the particle move on the circle shown in this choice, rather than solving Newton’s second law with the stated initial conditions.

distractors.label: E
distractors.misconception: treating the rotating field as a constant field
distractors.rationale: Error: replace the time-dependent electric field by its value at \(t=0\). Then \(a_x=\frac{qE_0}{m}\) and \(a_y=0\), so \(x=\frac{qE_0}{2m}t^2\); evaluating \(qE_0x\) at \(t=\frac{2\pi}{\omega}\) gives the work value displayed in this choice.

solution_decomposition.subgoal: Set up Newton’s second law in components.
solution_decomposition.rubric: Use F=qE and write separate x and y acceleration equations with their explicit time dependence.

solution_decomposition.subgoal: Apply the initial conditions during the integrations.
solution_decomposition.rubric: Integrate each acceleration component twice and retain the constants needed to enforce both zero initial velocity and zero initial position.

solution_decomposition.subgoal: Classify the parametric curve.
solution_decomposition.rubric: Compare the resulting parametric equations with the standard form of a cycloidal curve, allowing for an interchange of axes.

solution_decomposition.subgoal: Evaluate the work over a full field rotation.
solution_decomposition.rubric: Use the work-energy theorem by comparing the kinetic energy at the beginning and end of one period, rather than multiplying a force magnitude by a path length.

problems.source_ref: Fitzpatrick: Newtonian Dynamics, pp. 53-55, §4.5 Exercises

problems.stem: A small bead of mass \(0.50\,\mathrm{kg}\) is released from rest on a smooth, frictionless track. By the time it reaches a lower point on the track, it has dropped a vertical distance of \(5.0\,\mathrm{m}\) while traveling an arc length of \(8.0\,\mathrm{m}\) along the track. The bead remains in contact with the track throughout. Taking \(g = 9.8\,\mathrm{m}/\mathrm{s}^2\), what is the bead's speed at the lower point?

distractors.label: B
distractors.misconception: Using path length instead of vertical drop for gravitational work
distractors.rationale: This treats the work by gravity as \(mg\) times the \(8.0\,\mathrm{m}\) distance along the track: \(\tfrac{1}{2}mv^2 = mg(8.0\,\mathrm{m})\), giving \(v = \sqrt{2g \cdot 8.0} = 12.5\,\mathrm{m}/\mathrm{s}\).

distractors.label: C
distractors.misconception: Omitting the factor of 1/2 in kinetic energy
distractors.rationale: This uses \(K = mv^2\) rather than \(K = \tfrac{1}{2}mv^2\), so \(mg(5.0\,\mathrm{m}) = mv^2\), giving \(v = \sqrt{g \cdot 5.0} = 7.0\,\mathrm{m/s}\).

distractors.label: D
distractors.misconception: Assigning positive work to the normal force
distractors.rationale: This incorrectly lets the normal force add an amount of work comparable to gravity, so \(W_{\mathrm{net}} = 2mg(5.0\,\mathrm{m})\), giving \(v = \sqrt{4g \cdot 5.0} = 14.0\,\mathrm{m/s}\).

distractors.label: E
distractors.misconception: Assuming the normal force cancels the gravitational energy change
distractors.rationale: This treats the constraint force as canceling gravity's effect on the bead's kinetic energy, so the change in kinetic energy is taken to be zero and the bead remains at rest.

solution_decomposition.subgoal: Identify which forces can do work on the bead.
solution_decomposition.rubric: Gravity and the normal force act on the bead; for frictionless motion along the track, the normal force is perpendicular to the instantaneous displacement and does no work.

solution_decomposition.subgoal: Relate net work to change in kinetic energy.
solution_decomposition.rubric: Apply the work-energy theorem, using the gravitational work determined by the vertical drop rather than the arc length.

solution_decomposition.subgoal: Compute the speed from the resulting kinetic energy.
solution_decomposition.rubric: Set the bead's final kinetic energy equal to the gravitational work and solve for the speed using the given value of g and the 5.0 m drop.

problems.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 1, pp. 507-508, §10.8 Work and Power for Rotational Motion

problems.stem: A 2.0-kg block is pulled across a horizontal floor through a displacement of 4.0 m by a constant force of magnitude 20 N directed 60° above the horizontal. During the motion, kinetic friction has constant magnitude 6.0 N. The block remains on the floor. What is the net work done on the block by all forces?

distractors.label: B
distractors.rationale: Ignoring friction and counting only the pulling force gives W = Fd cos(theta) = (20 N)(4.0 m)cos60° = 40 J.

distractors.label: C
distractors.rationale: Using the full pulling force along the displacement and then subtracting friction gives \(W = Fd - f_k d = (20\,\mathrm{N})(4.0\,\mathrm{m}) - (6.0\,\mathrm{N})(4.0\,\mathrm{m}) = 56\,\mathrm{J}\).

distractors.label: D
distractors.rationale: Projecting the applied force with \(\sin 60^\circ\) instead of \(\cos 60^\circ\) and subtracting friction gives \(W = Fd \sin 60^\circ - f_k d = (20\,\mathrm{N})(4.0\,\mathrm{m})\sin 60^\circ - (6.0\,\mathrm{N})(4.0\,\mathrm{m}) \approx 45\,\mathrm{J}\).

distractors.label: E
distractors.rationale: Taking friction to do positive work while using the correct horizontal component of the pull gives \(W = Fd \cos 60^\circ + f_k d = (20\,\mathrm{N})(4.0\,\mathrm{m})\cos 60^\circ + (6.0\,\mathrm{N})(4.0\,\mathrm{m}) = 64\,\mathrm{J}\).

solution_decomposition.subgoal: Identify which forces do work over the horizontal displacement.
solution_decomposition.rubric: The applied force and kinetic friction contribute; gravity and the normal force are perpendicular to the displacement and therefore do zero work.

solution_decomposition.subgoal: Compute the work done by the applied force using the dot product.
solution_decomposition.rubric: Use W = Fd cos(theta), where theta is the angle between the applied force and the displacement.

solution_decomposition.subgoal: Compute the work done by kinetic friction.
solution_decomposition.rubric: Because friction is opposite the displacement, its work is negative with magnitude \(f_k d\).

solution_decomposition.subgoal: Add the work contributions to obtain the net work.
solution_decomposition.rubric: Sum the applied-force work and the friction work, with perpendicular-force contributions excluded.

problems.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 1, pp. 507-508, §10.8 Work and Power for Rotational Motion

problems.stem:
A weakly damped driven oscillator obeys
\[\frac{d^2x}{dt^2}+2\nu\frac{dx}{dt}+\omega_0^2x=\omega_0^2X_1\cos(\omega t),\]
with steady-state amplitude as described for forced, damped oscillations. For the same driving-force amplitude, the oscillator’s amplitude at resonance is measured to be 25 times its amplitude for very low driving frequency. Defining the resonance width \(\Delta\omega\) as the separation between the two frequencies where the amplitude has fallen to \(1/\sqrt{2}\) of its resonant value, what is \(\Delta\omega/\omega_0\)?

distractors.label: A
distractors.rationale: The error is to treat the fractional width as scaling like the inverse square of the amplitude enhancement. With the measured enhancement taken as 25, this gives \(1/25^2=0.0016\).

distractors.label: B
distractors.misconception: Confuses half-width with full resonance width
distractors.rationale: The error is to use only the offset from resonance to one half-maximum point. Since the full width spans both sides of the peak, using a one-sided width gives \(1/(2\cdot 25)=0.020\).

distractors.label: D
distractors.misconception: Doubles the full width by counting both sides twice
distractors.rationale: The error is to start from the full width and then multiply by 2 again, as if each side contributed a full \(2\nu\). This gives \(2/25=0.080\).

distractors.label: E
distractors.rationale: The error is to identify the fractional width with the quality factor itself rather than its reciprocal. Using the measured amplitude enhancement directly as the fractional width gives 25.

solution_decomposition.subgoal: Connect the measured resonance enhancement to the quality factor.
solution_decomposition.rubric: Use the weak-damping result that the ratio of resonant driven amplitude to the low-frequency driven amplitude is the quality factor \(Q\).

solution_decomposition.subgoal: Use the weak-damping resonance-width relation.
solution_decomposition.rubric: Apply the result that the fractional resonance width is \(\Delta\omega/\omega_0=1/Q\), where the width is defined at the \(1/\sqrt{2}\) amplitude points.

problems.source_ref: Fitzpatrick: Newtonian Dynamics, pp. 33-34, §One-Dimensional Motion

subproblems.prompt: Identify the quality factor from a measured resonance enhancement.

variants.stem: For a weakly damped driven oscillator with the same driving-force amplitude at all frequencies, the steady-state amplitude at resonance is measured to be 16 times the amplitude for very low driving frequency. Using the weak-damping resonance result, what is the quality factor \(Q\)?

distractor_rationales.A: This halves the observed amplitude ratio, as if an extra factor of 2 belonged in the resonance-enhancement relation.
distractor_rationales.C: This inverts the enhancement; the resonant amplitude is larger than the low-frequency amplitude for large \(Q\).
distractor_rationales.D: This doubles the observed amplitude ratio, confusing the damping-width factor \(2\nu\) with the amplitude ratio.
distractor_rationales.E: This takes a square root, which would be a mistake from mixing amplitude ratios with energy ratios.

variants.explain_why: For weak damping, the corpus result gives \(x_1(\omega_0)/x_1(0)=Q\) for the same drive amplitude. Therefore the measured amplitude enhancement directly equals \(Q\).
variants.source_ref: Fitzpatrick: Newtonian Dynamics, pp. 33-34, §One-Dimensional Motion

variants.stem: A weakly damped oscillator is driven with the same force amplitude at every frequency. Its steady-state amplitude at resonance is 60 times its amplitude in the very-low-frequency limit. What value of \(Q\) is implied?

distractor_rationales.A: This squares the amplitude ratio, a common error from treating amplitude like stored energy.
distractor_rationales.B: This introduces an unnecessary factor of 2; the resonance enhancement itself is \(Q\).
distractor_rationales.C: This uses the reciprocal of the enhancement rather than the enhancement.
distractor_rationales.E: This takes a square root, which is not part of the weak-damping amplitude-enhancement formula.

variants.explain_why: In the weakly damped limit, \(x_1(\omega_0)/x_1(0)=Q\). The measured resonance-to-low-frequency amplitude ratio is therefore the quality factor.
variants.source_ref: Fitzpatrick: Newtonian Dynamics, pp. 31-32, §3.6 Quality Factor

subproblems.prompt: Use the quality factor to find the fractional resonance width.

variants.stem: For a weakly damped driven oscillator, the resonance width \(\Delta\omega\) is defined as the separation between the two frequencies where the amplitude has fallen to \(1/\sqrt{2}\) of its resonant value. If \(Q=10\), what is \(\Delta\omega/\omega_0\)?

distractor_rationales.B: This uses \(1/Q^2\), which would over-narrow the peak by squaring the reciprocal.
distractor_rationales.C: This uses \(2/Q\), double-counting the full-width factor already included in the standard relation.
distractor_rationales.D: This uses \(Q\) itself, but the fractional width scales inversely with \(Q\).
distractor_rationales.E: This uses \(1/(2Q)\), treating the half-width as if it were the full separation \(\Delta\omega\).

variants.explain_why: For weak damping at the \(1/\sqrt{2}\) amplitude points, the corpus relation is \(\Delta\omega/\omega_0=1/Q\).
variants.source_ref: Fitzpatrick: Newtonian Dynamics, pp. 33-34, §One-Dimensional Motion

variants.stem: A weakly damped oscillator has quality factor \(Q=80\). The resonance width is measured between the two frequencies where the driven amplitude is \(1/\sqrt{2}\) of the maximum. What is the fractional width \(\Delta\omega/\omega_0\)?

distractor_rationales.A: This uses \(1/Q^2\), incorrectly squaring the inverse dependence.
distractor_rationales.B: This uses \(2/Q\), adding an extra factor of 2 even though \(\Delta\omega\) is already the full width.
distractor_rationales.C: This gives \(Q\), but the resonance peak becomes narrower as \(Q\) increases.
distractor_rationales.E: This uses \(1/(2Q)\), confusing the half-width with the full separation between the two half-maximum-amplitude points.

variants.explain_why: The weak-damping result for the full width at the \(1/\sqrt{2}\) amplitude points is \(\Delta\omega/\omega_0=1/Q\).
variants.source_ref: Fitzpatrick: Newtonian Dynamics, pp. 33-34, §One-Dimensional Motion

parent_variants.stem: A weakly damped driven oscillator obeys \[\frac{d^2x}{dt^2}+2\nu\frac{dx}{dt}+\omega_0^2x=\omega_0^2X_1\cos(\omega t).\] For the same driving-force amplitude, the oscillator's amplitude at resonance is measured to be 40 times its amplitude for very low driving frequency. Defining the resonance width \(\Delta\omega\) as the separation between the two frequencies where the amplitude has fallen to \(1/\sqrt{2}\) of its resonant value, what is \(\Delta\omega/\omega_0\)?

problems.stem: A weakly damped harmonic oscillator has natural angular frequency \(\omega_0 = 240\,\mathrm{rad/s}\) and damping parameter \(\nu = 3.0\,\mathrm{rad/s}\). For the same sinusoidal driving-force amplitude, its displacement amplitude in the zero-frequency limit is \(0.50\,\mathrm{mm}\). Using the weak-damping resonance relation from driven oscillations, what is the displacement amplitude when driven at resonance?

distractors.label: A
distractors.rationale: The likely error is treating the quality factor as a reduction rather than an amplification. This uses \(x_{\mathrm{res}} = x_0/Q\) with \(Q = 240/(2\cdot 3.0) = 40\), giving \(0.50/40 = 0.0125\,\mathrm{mm}\).

distractors.label: B
distractors.misconception: Assumes resonance does not change the amplitude
distractors.rationale: The likely error is overlooking that a weakly damped oscillator has a much larger response near its natural frequency. This leaves the amplitude equal to the zero-frequency value, giving 0.50 mm.

distractors.label: C
distractors.misconception: Uses the damping parameter itself as the quality factor
distractors.rationale:
The likely error is confusing \(
u\) with \(Q\). Multiplying the zero-frequency amplitude by \(
u\) gives \(0.50 \cdot 3.0 = 1.5\,\mathrm{mm}\), even though \(
u\) has units of angular frequency and is not the dimensionless enhancement factor.

distractors.label: E
distractors.misconception: Drops the factor of 2 in the quality factor
distractors.rationale: The likely error is using \(Q = \frac{\omega_0}{\nu}\) instead of \(Q = \frac{\omega_0}{2\nu}\). That gives \(Q = \frac{240}{3.0} = 80\), so the predicted amplitude is \(0.50 \cdot 80 = 40\,\mathrm{mm}\).

solution_decomposition.subgoal: Identify the resonance amplification relation
solution_decomposition.rubric: Use the weak-damping driven-oscillator relation \(x_1(\omega_0)/x_1(0) = Q = \frac{\omega_0}{2\nu}\), not the bandwidth alone and not the phase-lag relation.

solution_decomposition.subgoal: Compute the quality factor from the given angular frequencies
solution_decomposition.rubric: Substitute \(\omega_0\) and \(\nu\) into the dimensionless ratio, keeping the factor of \(2\) in the denominator.

solution_decomposition.subgoal: Scale the zero-frequency amplitude
solution_decomposition.rubric: Multiply the given zero-frequency displacement amplitude by the computed dimensionless enhancement factor and keep the result in millimeters.

problems.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 1, pp. 751-752, §15.6 Forced Oscillations

subproblems.prompt: Choose the correct resonance-amplification relation for a weakly damped driven oscillator, using the same sinusoidal driving-force amplitude for the resonance and zero-frequency cases.

variants.stem: A weakly damped oscillator has \(\omega_0=180\,\mathrm{rad/s}\) and \(\nu=3.0\,\mathrm{rad/s}\). If \(x_1(0)\) is the zero-frequency displacement amplitude for a given drive amplitude, which relation should be used to find the resonant displacement amplitude \(x_1(\omega_0)\)?

distractor_rationales.B: This inverts the quality-factor amplification, treating resonance as a reduction rather than an enhancement.
distractor_rationales.C: This omits the factor of \(2\) in \(Q=\omega_0/(2\nu)\).
distractor_rationales.D: This uses the resonance width \(\Delta \omega=2\nu\) as though it were the amplitude multiplier; it also has units, so it cannot multiply a displacement by itself.
distractor_rationales.E: This confuses the half-power or peak-edge amplitude ratio with the resonance-to-static amplification.

variants.explain_why: For weak damping, the driven-oscillator resonance relation is \(x_1(\omega_0)/x_1(0)=Q\), and Fitzpatrick gives \(Q\simeq \omega_0/(2\nu)\).
variants.source_ref: Fitzpatrick: Newtonian Dynamics, pp. 33-34, §One-Dimensional Motion

variants.stem: A weakly damped oscillator has \(\omega_0=320\,\mathrm{rad/s}\) and \(\nu=4.0\,\mathrm{rad/s}\). For the same driving-force amplitude, which equation correctly connects the resonant amplitude to the zero-frequency amplitude?

distractor_rationales.B: This uses a small damping-to-frequency ratio instead of the large quality-factor amplification.
distractor_rationales.C: This recognizes that larger \(\omega_0\) and smaller \(\nu\) increase the response, but it misses the required factor of \(2\).
distractor_rationales.D: This subtracts angular frequencies and treats the result as an amplitude multiplier; the units do not match a dimensionless amplification factor.
distractor_rationales.E: This is the reciprocal of the quality factor, so it would shrink the amplitude at resonance.

variants.explain_why: The resonance height relative to the zero-frequency response is the quality factor: \(x_1(\omega_0)/x_1(0)=Q=\omega_0/(2\nu)\) for weak damping.
variants.source_ref: Fitzpatrick: Newtonian Dynamics, pp. 33-34, §One-Dimensional Motion

subproblems.prompt: Compute the quality factor from the angular frequency and damping parameter, keeping the factor of \(2\) in the denominator.

variants.stem: For a weakly damped oscillator with \(\omega_0=400\,\mathrm{rad/s}\) and \(\nu=8.0\,\mathrm{rad/s}\), what is \(Q\simeq \omega_0/(2\nu)\)?

distractor_rationales.B: This is \(\omega_0/\nu\), which drops the factor of \(2\) in the denominator.
distractor_rationales.C: This is approximately \(2\nu/\omega_0\), the reciprocal of the quality factor.
distractor_rationales.D: This is the bandwidth \(2\nu\) in \(\mathrm{rad/s}\), not the dimensionless quality factor.
distractor_rationales.E: This is \(\nu/\omega_0\), another inverted small ratio rather than the resonance amplification.

variants.explain_why: Substitute directly: \(Q=400/(2\cdot 8.0)=400/16=25\), a dimensionless amplification factor.
variants.source_ref: Fitzpatrick: Newtonian Dynamics, pp. 33-34, §One-Dimensional Motion

variants.stem: For a weakly damped oscillator with \(\omega_0=270\,\mathrm{rad/s}\) and \(\nu=4.5\,\mathrm{rad/s}\), what is \(Q\simeq \omega_0/(2\nu)\)?

distractor_rationales.A: This is \(\omega_0/\nu\), so it misses the required factor of \(2\).
distractor_rationales.C: This is roughly the reciprocal of the correct quality factor, confusing amplification with reduction.
distractor_rationales.D: This is \(2\nu\), the resonance width in angular-frequency units, not \(Q\).
distractor_rationales.E: This is \(\nu/\omega_0\), a small damping ratio rather than the quality factor.

variants.explain_why: Using the weak-damping expression gives \(Q=270/(2\cdot 4.5)=270/9.0=30\).
variants.source_ref: Fitzpatrick: Newtonian Dynamics, pp. 33-34, §One-Dimensional Motion

subproblems.prompt: Scale the zero-frequency displacement amplitude by the dimensionless quality factor, keeping the displacement units unchanged.

variants.stem: For the same driving-force amplitude, a weakly damped oscillator has zero-frequency displacement amplitude \(x_1(0)=0.40\,\mathrm{mm}\). If its quality factor is \(Q=25\), what is the resonant displacement amplitude?

distractor_rationales.B: This divides the zero-frequency amplitude by \(Q\), reversing the resonance amplification.
distractor_rationales.C: This ignores the resonance enhancement and leaves the amplitude unchanged.
distractor_rationales.D: This uses \(Q\) itself as though it were a displacement; \(Q\) is dimensionless and must multiply \(x_1(0)\).
distractor_rationales.E: This applies an extra scale-factor error rather than simply multiplying in millimeters.

variants.explain_why: The resonance relation gives \(x_1(\omega_0)=Qx_1(0)=25(0.40\,\mathrm{mm})=10\,\mathrm{mm}\).
variants.source_ref: Fitzpatrick: Newtonian Dynamics, pp. 33-34, §One-Dimensional Motion

variants.stem: For the same driving-force amplitude, a weakly damped oscillator has zero-frequency displacement amplitude \(x_1(0)=0.30\,\mathrm{mm}\). If its quality factor is \(Q=30\), what is the resonant displacement amplitude?

distractor_rationales.A: This resembles dividing or otherwise under-scaling instead of multiplying by the full quality factor.
distractor_rationales.C: This divides by \(Q\), giving a reduced amplitude rather than a resonant enhancement.
distractor_rationales.D: This ignores the increase in amplitude at resonance.
distractor_rationales.E: This uses the dimensionless \(Q\) almost as the displacement value itself, rather than multiplying it by \(0.30\,\mathrm{mm}\).

variants.explain_why: At resonance, the amplitude is enhanced by \(Q\): \(x_1(\omega_0)=30(0.30\,\mathrm{mm})=9.0\,\mathrm{mm}\).
variants.source_ref: Fitzpatrick: Newtonian Dynamics, pp. 33-34, §One-Dimensional Motion

parent_variants.stem: A weakly damped harmonic oscillator has natural angular frequency \(\omega_0=300\,\mathrm{rad/s}\) and damping parameter \(\nu=5.0\,\mathrm{rad/s}\). For the same sinusoidal driving-force amplitude, its displacement amplitude in the zero-frequency limit is \(0.40\,\mathrm{mm}\). Using the weak-damping resonance relation from driven oscillations, what is the displacement amplitude when driven at resonance?

problems.stem: A simple pendulum clock has period \(T=2\pi\sqrt{L/g}\) for small-amplitude oscillations. It keeps correct time in one city, then is moved to another city where the acceleration due to gravity is \(1.0\%\) greater. To keep the same period, how should the pendulum length be adjusted?

distractors.label: B
distractors.misconception: Inverse scaling of length with gravity
