Compute the intensity-weighted mean wavelength of each whole spectrum and compare those means between angles.
Treat the longer-wavelength peak as background and use only the peak at the incident wavelength.

distractors.label: B
distractors.misconception: confusing peak intensity with wavelength shift
distractors.rationale: Error: using vertical information to infer a horizontal displacement. Because the spectra are in arbitrary intensity units and detector gain may vary, this procedure turns gain changes or relative scattering strengths into a supposed Compton-effect measure.

distractors.label: C
distractors.misconception: treating spectra at different angles as directly subtractable without normalization
distractors.rationale: Error: assuming intensity scales are identical between spectra. A point-by-point subtraction would mix true profile differences with arbitrary vertical normalization, producing an area that depends on detector response as well as scattering angle.

distractors.label: D
distractors.misconception: using a centroid of a two-peak distribution as if it were a peak displacement
distractors.rationale: Error: averaging over both the unshifted and shifted peaks. The resulting mean wavelength changes when peak heights change, so it can vary even if the actual separation of the two wavelength peaks is unchanged.

distractors.label: E
distractors.misconception: classical-scattering expectation that only the incident wavelength is physical
distractors.rationale: Error: dismissing the additional peak predicted by the photon-collision interpretation and observed in the experiment. This leaves only a reference wavelength and discards the feature whose angular behavior is being measured.

solution_decomposition.subgoal: Identify the observable that defines the Compton shift in the experiment.
solution_decomposition.rubric: Use the fact that the measured effect is the wavelength separation between the incident-wavelength peak and the second scattered peak.

solution_decomposition.subgoal: Separate horizontal calibration information from vertical intensity information.
solution_decomposition.rubric: Recognize that arbitrary intensity units and uncertain detector gain make peak heights or areas unreliable for measuring a wavelength displacement.

solution_decomposition.subgoal: Choose an analysis robust to gain variation.
solution_decomposition.rubric: Prefer extracting peak positions along the wavelength axis within each spectrum and comparing the separation with scattering angle.

problems.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 3, pp. 257-258, §6.3 The Compton Effect

subproblems.prompt: Identify the horizontal observable that represents the Compton shift in a two-peak X-ray spectrum.

variants.stem: At one scattering angle, a graphite Compton spectrum has one peak centered at the incident wavelength, \\(62.0\\,\\text{pm}\\), and a second peak centered at \\(64.7\\,\\text{pm}\\). What quantity represents the Compton shift for this spectrum?
choices: The difference between the two peak heights

distractor_rationales.A: Averaging the two wavelengths confuses the location of the whole spectrum with the shift between the two spectral features.
distractor_rationales.C: Using only the longer wavelength ignores the reference peak at the incident wavelength.
distractor_rationales.D: Taking a ratio treats the shift as fractional rather than as the wavelength separation defined in the Compton-effect data.
distractor_rationales.E: Using peak heights confuses vertical intensity information with the horizontal wavelength displacement.

variants.explain_why: In the Compton spectra described in the text, the shift is the separation along the wavelength axis between the incident-wavelength peak and the longer-wavelength scattered peak: \\(64.7-62.0=2.7\\,\\text{pm}\\).
variants.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 3, pp. 257-258, §6.3 The Compton Effect

variants.stem: At another scattering angle, the peak at the incident wavelength is centered at \\(71.0\\,\\text{pm}\\), while the shifted peak is centered at \\(74.4\\,\\text{pm}\\). What is the experimental Compton shift for that spectrum?
choices: The ratio of the two peak intensities

distractor_rationales.A: Adding the wavelengths does not measure the displacement between the two peaks.
distractor_rationales.B: Averaging the wavelengths gives a central location, not the separation.
distractor_rationales.C: Intensity ratios use vertical information, but the Compton shift is read horizontally from the wavelength axis.
distractor_rationales.E: The shifted peak position alone is not the shift unless compared with the incident-wavelength peak.

variants.explain_why: The Compton shift is the horizontal separation of the two wavelength centroids, so \\(\\Delta\\lambda=74.4-71.0=3.4\\,\\text{pm}\\).
variants.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 3, pp. 259-260, §6.3 The Compton Effect

subproblems.prompt: Separate wavelength-axis information from intensity-axis information when detector gain changes.

variants.stem: Two spectra are taken at different detector gain settings. In both spectra, the peak centers are at \\(80.0\\,\\text{pm}\\) and \\(82.6\\,\\text{pm}\\). With one gain setting the peak heights are \\(18\\) and \\(6\\) arbitrary units; with another they are \\(54\\) and \\(18\\) arbitrary units. Which quantity is unaffected by the gain change and is relevant to the shift?
choices: The larger total peak area at the higher gain
The sum of the two peak heights
The intensity-weighted mean of the whole plotted curve

distractor_rationales.A: Total area scales with vertical gain, so it is not a stable measure of a horizontal displacement.
distractor_rationales.B: Even if a ratio happens to stay the same under a simple common gain, it is still an intensity comparison rather than the definition of the Compton shift.
distractor_rationales.D: The sum of heights directly changes when the gain changes.
distractor_rationales.E: An intensity-weighted mean mixes horizontal positions with unreliable vertical weights.

variants.explain_why: The corpus emphasizes that the spectra are plotted in arbitrary intensity units, while the Compton shift is the separation between peak positions on the wavelength axis. The centers give \\(82.6-80.0=2.6\\,\\text{pm}\\).
variants.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 3, pp. 257-258, §6.3 The Compton Effect

variants.stem: A detector gain change multiplies all recorded intensities by \\(0.40\\). Before the change, two peaks are centered at \\(48.0\\,\\text{pm}\\) and \\(49.9\\,\\text{pm}\\), with heights \\(25\\) and \\(10\\) arbitrary units. After the change, the heights are \\(10\\) and \\(4\\) arbitrary units, while the centers are unchanged. Which comparison best avoids the gain issue?
choices: Compare the decrease in total height, \\(35-14\\) arbitrary units
Compare only the shifted peak height
Compare the height ratio because it uses both peaks
Compare the full-spectrum area after subtracting one spectrum from the other

distractor_rationales.B: The decrease in total height is caused by the gain change, not by a wavelength shift.
distractor_rationales.C: A single peak height is vertical information and is especially sensitive to gain.
distractor_rationales.D: A height ratio is still an intensity-based observable rather than the wavelength separation that defines the shift.
distractor_rationales.E: Subtracting spectra point-by-point in intensity mainly reflects the gain mismatch and does not directly measure peak displacement.

variants.explain_why: A gain factor changes vertical arbitrary units, but not the wavelength coordinates of the peak centroids. The robust horizontal quantity is \\(49.9-48.0=1.9\\,\\text{pm}\\).
variants.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 3, pp. 257-258, §6.3 The Compton Effect

subproblems.prompt: Choose a multi-angle comparison that tracks the angle dependence of the Compton effect.

variants.stem: Three spectra have different vertical gains. Their fitted peak centers are: at \\(30^\\circ\\), \\(55.0\\,\\text{pm}\\) and \\(55.3\\,\\text{pm}\\); at \\(90^\\circ\\), \\(55.0\\,\\text{pm}\\) and \\(57.4\\,\\text{pm}\\); at \\(150^\\circ\\), \\(55.0\\,\\text{pm}\\) and \\(59.5\\,\\text{pm}\\). Which data should be compared versus angle?
choices: The shifted-peak heights at the three angles
The residual areas after subtracting the \\(30^\\circ\\) spectrum from the others

distractor_rationales.A: Peak heights are vertical intensity measurements and are unreliable when gain differs between angles.
distractor_rationales.B: Intensity-weighted means depend on both peak positions and gain-dependent weights, so they can move for non-shift reasons.
distractor_rationales.C: Residual areas from point-by-point subtraction are dominated by intensity scaling and line-shape differences, not just horizontal peak separation.
distractor_rationales.E: The unshifted peak stays at the incident wavelength and therefore does not track the angle-dependent displaced peak.

variants.explain_why: For each angle, extract the two wavelength centroids and subtract them. That produces a horizontal shift value for each angle, independent of arbitrary vertical scaling.
variants.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 3, pp. 259-260, §6.3 The Compton Effect

variants.stem: A set of spectra at several angles has unreliable gain. Fitted peak centers are: at \\(45^\\circ\\), \\(90.0\\,\\text{pm}\\) and \\(90.7\\,\\text{pm}\\); at \\(100^\\circ\\), \\(90.0\\,\\text{pm}\\) and \\(93.0\\,\\text{pm}\\); at \\(135^\\circ\\), \\(90.0\\,\\text{pm}\\) and \\(94.1\\,\\text{pm}\\). Which angle-dependent list best represents the Compton effect?
choices: The total recorded intensities at \\(45^\\circ\\), \\(100^\\circ\\), and \\(135^\\circ\\)
The longer-wavelength peak positions \\(90.7\\,\\text{pm}\\), \\(93.0\\,\\text{pm}\\), and \\(94.1\\,\\text{pm}\\) without reference to the incident peak
The unshifted peak position \\(90.0\\,\\text{pm}\\) at all angles
The peak-height ratios at the three angles

distractor_rationales.A: Total recorded intensities are vertical measurements and can change because of gain rather than scattering physics.
distractor_rationales.B: The shifted peak positions become meaningful as shifts only when compared with the incident-wavelength peak.
distractor_rationales.C: The unshifted peak is the reference wavelength and does not show the growing separation with angle.
distractor_rationales.D: Height ratios are intensity-based and do not define the wavelength displacement.

variants.explain_why: The Compton effect is isolated by comparing the separation between the incident-wavelength peak and the longer-wavelength peak at each angle: \\(90.7-90.0\\), \\(93.0-90.0\\), and \\(94.1-90.0\\).
variants.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 3, pp. 257-258, §6.3 The Compton Effect

parent_variants.stem: In a Compton-scattering measurement, monochromatic X-rays of wavelength \\(68\\,\\text{pm}\\) are incident on graphite. A detector records intensity versus scattered wavelength at \\(40^\\circ\\), \\(80^\\circ\\), and \\(140^\\circ\\). Each plotted spectrum uses arbitrary intensity units above background and may have a different vertical gain setting. Except at special angles, the spectra show one peak at \\(68\\,\\text{pm}\\) and another at a longer wavelength. Which analysis best isolates the angle-dependent Compton effect?
choices: Compare the ratio of the longer-wavelength peak height to the \\(68\\,\\text{pm}\\) peak height at each angle.
Compute the intensity-weighted mean wavelength of each entire spectrum and compare those means between angles.
Fit the wavelength centroids of the two peaks in each spectrum and compare their horizontal separations as a function of scattering angle.
Subtract the \\(40^\\circ\\) spectrum point-by-point from the other spectra in intensity and use the total residual area as the shift.
Treat the longer-wavelength peak as background and analyze only the peak at \\(68\\,\\text{pm}\\).

problems.stem: A \(5.00\,\mathrm{MeV}\) alpha particle is fired head-on toward a gold nucleus and is turned around by the Coulomb interaction. Treat the gold nucleus as fixed, take the alpha charge to be \(+2e\) and the gold nuclear charge to be \(+79e\), and use \(k e^2 = 1.440\,\mathrm{MeV}\cdot\mathrm{fm}\). If the only experimental uncertainty is the alpha-particle kinetic energy, \(K = 5.00 \pm 0.05\,\mathrm{MeV}\), what is the distance of closest approach with its 1-sigma uncertainty?

distractors.label: B
distractors.misconception: Using the alpha particle as singly charged
distractors.rationale: Omitting the alpha particle's factor of 2 gives r = (1.440)(79)/(5.00) = 22.8 fm. Since r is inversely proportional to K, the propagated uncertainty is 1% of this value, about 0.2 fm.

distractors.label: C
distractors.misconception: Double-counting the energy uncertainty in propagation
distractors.rationale: Using the correct central Coulomb expression but treating the inverse dependence as if it contributed twice gives a fractional uncertainty of \(2(0.05/5.00)\), producing an uncertainty of about \(0.9\,\mathrm{fm}\).

distractors.label: D
distractors.misconception: Varying the input before reporting the central value
distractors.rationale: Substituting \(K + \sigma_K = 5.05\,\mathrm{MeV}\) into the distance formula gives \(r = \frac{(1.440)(2)(79)}{5.05} = 45.1\,\mathrm{fm}\), which is not the central estimate from the measured value.

distractors.label: E
distractors.rationale: Treating \(5.00\,\mathrm{MeV}\) as \(5.00\,\mathrm{eV}\) while still using \(1.440\,\mathrm{MeV}\cdot\mathrm{fm}\) makes the denominator \(5.00 \times 10^{-6}\,\mathrm{MeV}\), giving \(r = 4.55 \times 10^7\,\mathrm{fm}\) with the same \(1\%\) fractional uncertainty.

solution_decomposition.subgoal: Model the turning point energetically
solution_decomposition.rubric: Equate the initial kinetic energy to the Coulomb potential energy at closest approach, using the two nuclear charges specified in the prompt.

solution_decomposition.subgoal: Compute the central distance
solution_decomposition.rubric: Use \(r = \frac{k(2e)(79e)}{K}\) and the given value of \(k e^2\) in \(\mathrm{MeV}\cdot\mathrm{fm}\) so that the result is directly in femtometers.

solution_decomposition.subgoal: Propagate the measurement uncertainty
solution_decomposition.rubric: Because \(r\) is proportional to \(K^{-1}\) and the charges and constant are treated as exact, use \(\sigma_r/r = \sigma_K/K\) for the \(1\)-\(\sigma\) uncertainty.

problems.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 2, p. 332, §7.6 Applications of Electrostatics

problems.stem: A laser beam deposits energy into a circular spot, so its intensity is computed as \(I=\frac{E}{At}\), with \(A=\pi\left(\frac{d}{2}\right)^2\). In a measurement like the laser-tissue example, suppose \(E=(500.0\pm10.0)\,\mathrm{J}\), \(d=(2.00\pm0.02)\,\mathrm{mm}\), and \(t=(4.00\pm0.04)\,\mathrm{s}\). Using the method of adding percent uncertainties for multiplication and division, what is the percent uncertainty in the intensity?

distractors.label: A
distractors.rationale: Error: treating the problem as if independent random uncertainties must be combined in quadrature rather than using the stated method of adding percent uncertainties. The energy contributes \(2.0\%\), the squared diameter contributes \(2.0\%\), and the time contributes \(1.0\%\), giving \(\sqrt{(2.0)^2+(2.0)^2+(1.0)^2}\approx 3.0\%\).

distractors.label: B
distractors.rationale: Error: counting the diameter uncertainty only once, as though the area were proportional to \(d\) rather than \(d^2\). This gives \(2.0\%\) from \(E\), \(1.0\%\) from \(d\), and \(1.0\%\) from \(t\), for \(4.0\%\).

distractors.label: D
distractors.rationale: Error: converting \(d\) to \(r=d/2\) but keeping the absolute uncertainty as \(\pm 0.02\,\mathrm{mm}\) instead of also halving it. This makes \(r=(1.00\pm0.02)\,\mathrm{mm}\), a \(2.0\%\) radius uncertainty, so the area term is counted as \(4.0\%\); adding \(2.0\%\) from \(E\) and \(1.0\%\) from \(t\) gives \(7.0\%\).

distractors.label: E
distractors.rationale: Error: adding the numerical absolute uncertainties without first forming percent uncertainties. Using 10.0, 0.02, and 0.04 as if they were directly comparable percent contributions gives about 10.06%, which rounds to 10.1%.

solution_decomposition.subgoal: Relate the measured quantities to the laser intensity.
solution_decomposition.rubric: Use \(I = \frac{E}{At}\) and \(A = \pi\left(\frac{d}{2}\right)^2\), so the intensity is proportional to \(E d^{-2} t^{-1}\).

solution_decomposition.subgoal: Find the individual percent uncertainties.
solution_decomposition.rubric: Compute percent uncertainty as uncertainty divided by measured value, times 100%, for E, d, and t.

solution_decomposition.subgoal: Apply the method of adding percent uncertainties.
solution_decomposition.rubric: For products and quotients, add percent uncertainties; because d appears squared, include the diameter contribution twice.

problems.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 1, pp. 814-815, §16.6 Standing Waves and Resonance

problems.stem: A Michelson interferometer is used to measure the wavelength of monochromatic light. One mirror is translated by \(D = 45.0\,\mu\mathrm{m}\), during which \(m = 150\) fringes pass a fixed point on the screen. The mirror displacement uncertainty is negligible, but the fringe count could be off by \(\pm 3\) fringes. Using the Michelson round-trip path change, what wavelength should be reported, with the count-limited uncertainty?

distractors.label: A
distractors.rationale: The likely error is treating the mirror displacement as the optical path change. This uses \(\lambda = D/m = 45.0\,\mu\mathrm{m}/150 = 0.300\,\mu\mathrm{m}\), and the same fractional count uncertainty gives \(\delta\lambda = 300\,\mathrm{nm} \times 3/150 = 6\,\mathrm{nm}\).

distractors.label: B
distractors.rationale: The likely error is computing the central wavelength with the Michelson factor but then converting \(\pm 3\) counted fringes directly into \(\pm 3\,\mathrm{nm}\), rather than using fractional uncertainty in the quotient.

distractors.label: D
distractors.rationale: The likely error is applying the round-trip factor twice, using \(\lambda = 4D/m\). That gives \(\lambda = 4(45.0\,\mu\mathrm{m})/150 = 1.20\,\mu\mathrm{m}\), with fractional uncertainty \(3/150\), giving \(\pm 24\,\mathrm{nm}\).

distractors.label: E
distractors.rationale: The likely error is treating the possible fringe-count error as increasing the wavelength estimate additively by one fractional uncertainty, giving \(600\,\mathrm{nm} \times (1 + \tfrac{3}{150}) = 612\,\mathrm{nm}\), while still attaching the propagated uncertainty.

solution_decomposition.subgoal: Relate fringe count to mirror displacement in a Michelson interferometer.
solution_decomposition.rubric: Use the fact that moving one mirror by D changes the optical path length by 2D, so the number of fringes corresponds to the number of wavelengths in that path change.

solution_decomposition.subgoal: Compute the central wavelength estimate.
solution_decomposition.rubric: Solve the Michelson fringe relation algebraically for \(\lambda\) and substitute \(D\) and \(m\) with consistent length units.

solution_decomposition.subgoal: Propagate the count uncertainty.
solution_decomposition.rubric: Because \(\lambda\) is inversely proportional to \(m\) when \(D\) is fixed, use the fractional uncertainty \(\delta\lambda/\lambda \approx \delta m/m\) for the magnitude of the count-limited uncertainty.

problems.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 3, pp. 141-142, §3.5 The Michelson Interferometer

problems.stem: A cylindrical sample has density computed from \(\rho=m/(\pi r^2 h)\), where \(m\) is its mass, \(r\) its radius, and \(h\) its height. The percent uncertainties in \(m\), \(r\), and \(h\) are 2%, 1%, and 3%, respectively. Taking \(\pi\) to be dimensionless and using the method of adding percents for multiplication and division, which choice gives the dimensions of \(\rho\) and its percent uncertainty?

distractors.label: B
distractors.misconception: Ignoring the exponent in uncertainty propagation
distractors.rationale: This treats the radius as contributing only one 1% factor to the percent uncertainty, giving 2% + 1% + 3% = 6%, while still using the length powers correctly for the dimensional part.

distractors.label: C
distractors.rationale: This treats division as causing percent uncertainties in the denominator to cancel against the numerator, giving |2% - 2(1%) - 3%| = 3%, although percent uncertainties are not combined this way under the stated rule.

distractors.label: D
distractors.misconception: Dropping the height factor from the volume
distractors.rationale: This analyzes the denominator as though it contained only \(r^2\), producing dimensions \(M/L^2\), and adds only the mass and two radius percent contributions: 2% + 2(1%) = 4%.

distractors.label: E
distractors.misconception: Inverting the role of volume in density
distractors.rationale: This effectively treats the expression as proportional to \(m\pi r^2h\), putting the length factors in the numerator and producing dimensions \(ML^3\), while still adding the listed percent contributions.

solution_decomposition.subgoal: Determine the dimensional dependence of the formula.
solution_decomposition.rubric: Use \([m]=M\), \([r]=[h]=L\), and \([\pi]=1\). Track that \(r\) appears twice and \(h\) appears once in the denominator.

solution_decomposition.subgoal: Propagate the percent uncertainty using the stated rule.
solution_decomposition.rubric: For a product or quotient, add percent uncertainties; a squared measured quantity contributes its percent uncertainty twice.

problems.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 1, p. 45, §1.1 The Scope and Scale of Physics

problems.stem: A measurement is repeated on \(N\) statistically independent microscopic events. Using the \(1/\sqrt{N}\) law for statistical deviations quoted for macroscopic systems, about how many events are needed so that the fractional statistical deviation is below \(1\%\)?

distractors.label: A
distractors.rationale: If the deviation is incorrectly taken to scale as \(1/N\), setting \(1/N = 0.01\) gives \(N = 10^2\).

distractors.label: B
distractors.misconception: rounding from the poll example rather than solving the threshold condition
distractors.rationale: A thousand observations give \(\frac{1}{\sqrt{1000}} \approx 3.2\%\), so choosing \(10^3\) treats a few-percent fluctuation as if it were already below the stated one-percent target.

distractors.label: D
distractors.rationale: If \(1\%\) is mistakenly converted to \(0.001\), then \(1/\sqrt{N} = 0.001\) gives \(N = 10^6\).

distractors.label: E
distractors.rationale: If \(1\%\) is first written as \(0.01\) but then an extra factor of \(0.01\) is introduced before solving, the condition becomes \(1/\sqrt{N} = 10^{-4}\), giving \(N = 10^{8}\).

solution_decomposition.subgoal: Translate the percent requirement into a dimensionless fractional deviation.
solution_decomposition.rubric: Recognize that one percent corresponds to 0.01 as a fractional uncertainty.

solution_decomposition.subgoal: Apply the statistical scaling law from the corpus.
solution_decomposition.rubric: Use the relation that typical fractional statistical deviations scale as \(1/\sqrt{N}\).

solution_decomposition.subgoal: Solve the threshold equation for the order of magnitude of N.
solution_decomposition.rubric: Set the scaling expression equal to the required fractional level and isolate N by squaring both sides.

problems.source_ref: Fitzpatrick: Thermodynamics and Statistical Mechanics, p. 6, §Microscopic and macroscopic systems

problems.stem: A spacecraft navigation file lists an impulse command as the number \(200\) in units of \(\mathrm{lbf}\cdot\mathrm{s}\), but another program reads the same numerical entry as \(200\,\mathrm{N}\cdot\mathrm{s}\). Given \(1\,\mathrm{lbf} = 4.45\,\mathrm{N}\), what is the percent error of the impulse used by the second program relative to the intended SI impulse? Use \(\mathrm{percent\ error} = 100 \times \frac{\mathrm{used} - \mathrm{intended}}{\mathrm{intended}}\).

distractors.label: B
distractors.misconception: wrong reference value for percent error
distractors.rationale: The intended impulse is first converted to SI, but the difference is divided by the smaller numerical value read by the program: \(100 \times (890 - 200)/200 = 345\%\), with the sign also reported as positive.

distractors.label: C
distractors.rationale: The conversion is applied, but the fractional error is normalized to the used value rather than the intended value: \(100 \times \frac{200 - 890}{200} = -345\%\).

distractors.label: D
distractors.rationale: The magnitude of the relative discrepancy is computed using the intended SI impulse as the reference, but the order of subtraction is reversed, producing a positive value.

distractors.label: E
distractors.rationale: The two entries are treated as the same physical impulse because both have the number 200, so the difference is taken to be zero without applying the lbf-to-newton conversion.

solution_decomposition.subgoal: Convert the intended impulse into SI units.
solution_decomposition.rubric: Multiply the numerical value in \(\mathrm{lbf}\cdot\mathrm{s}\) by \(4.45\) to express it in \(\mathrm{N}\cdot\mathrm{s}\).

solution_decomposition.subgoal: Compare the used impulse with the intended SI impulse.
solution_decomposition.rubric: Use the stated percent-error formula with the converted intended value in the denominator.

solution_decomposition.subgoal: Interpret the sign of the percent error.
solution_decomposition.rubric: A negative sign indicates that the impulse used by the second program is smaller than the intended SI impulse.

problems.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 1, p. 32, §1.4 Dimensional Analysis

problems.stem: A massive star has undergone nucleosynthesis until its core is primarily iron-nickel metal. According to the binding-energy argument described for fusion, what is the consequence for nuclear reactions involving iron in this core?
choices: Further fusion of iron releases energy, increasing the outward thermal pressure and stabilizing the core.
Further fission of iron releases energy, so the core can be supported by fission after fusion stops.
Both fusion and fission involving iron absorb energy, so nuclear reactions no longer supply the outward pressure needed to balance gravity.
Iron nuclei beta decay into lighter nuclei, and the emitted electrons supply the star’s outward pressure.
Iron nuclei combine with helium to form oxygen, releasing gamma radiation that supports the core.

distractors.label: A
distractors.rationale: A student remembers that fusing light nuclei into medium-mass nuclei releases energy, then extends that rule past the range where the corpus says it applies, producing the choice that iron fusion would keep supporting the core.

distractors.label: B
distractors.rationale: A student treats fission as the natural fallback once fusion stops and reasons that splitting iron should release energy, producing the choice that fission can replace fusion as the core’s power source.

distractors.label: D
distractors.rationale: A student imports the idea of beta decay from nuclear processes and assumes emitted electrons can provide the relevant stellar energy source, producing the choice centered on beta emission rather than the fusion/fission energy balance.

distractors.label: E
distractors.rationale: A student recalls the carbon-plus-helium-to-oxygen fusion example but reverses the role of oxygen and misassigns that reaction pattern to iron, producing the choice involving helium fusion to oxygen.

solution_decomposition.subgoal: Identify which part of the binding-energy discussion governs iron-rich stellar cores.
solution_decomposition.rubric: Credit recognizing that the corpus gives iron a special limiting role in nucleosynthesis rather than treating it like lighter nuclei.

solution_decomposition.subgoal: Relate nuclear energy flow to stellar pressure balance.
solution_decomposition.rubric: Credit connecting the absence of a usable nuclear energy source to the loss of outward thermal support against gravity.

problems.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 3, pp. 471-472, §10.6 Nuclear Fusion

subproblems.prompt: Use the binding-energy-per-nucleon idea to decide the energy sign for splitting a nucleus near the peak of the binding-energy curve.

variants.stem: A simplified binding-energy graph gives a near-iron nucleus with mass number \(A=56\) a binding energy per nucleon of \(8.80\,\text{MeV}\). Suppose it is split into fragments whose total mass number is still \(56\), but whose average binding energy per nucleon is \(8.45\,\text{MeV}\). What is the sign and approximate size of the nuclear energy change?
choices: There is no energy change because the total mass number is unchanged.

distractor_rationales.A: This reverses the sign: the fragments have less total binding energy, so the process needs energy input.
distractor_rationales.C: This uses the initial total binding energy as though all of it were released, rather than comparing initial and final binding energies.
distractor_rationales.D: This uses the final total binding energy as though it were the energy absorbed, rather than the difference between final and initial binding energies.
distractor_rationales.E: Mass number conservation does not imply zero energy change; binding energy can change even when the nucleon count is fixed.

variants.explain_why: Initial binding energy is \(56(8.80)\), while final binding energy is \(56(8.45)\). Since the final state is less tightly bound by \(56(0.35)=19.6\,\text{MeV}\), that much energy must be supplied.
variants.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 3, pp. 471-472, §10.6 Nuclear Fusion

variants.stem: A simplified binding-energy graph gives a near-iron nucleus with mass number \(A=58\) a binding energy per nucleon of \(8.79\,\text{MeV}\). It is split into fragments whose total mass number is \(58\), with average binding energy per nucleon \(8.37\,\text{MeV}\). What is the sign and approximate size of the nuclear energy change?
choices: There is no energy change because the same number of nucleons is present.

distractor_rationales.B: This has the sign backward: moving to fragments with smaller binding energy per nucleon requires energy input.
distractor_rationales.C: This treats the initial total binding energy as the amount released, instead of using the change in binding energy.
distractor_rationales.D: This treats the final total binding energy as the amount absorbed, instead of using the difference.
distractor_rationales.E: The number of nucleons can be conserved while the nuclear binding energy changes.

variants.explain_why: Compare total binding energies: \(58(8.37)-58(8.79)=58(-0.42)=-24.36\,\text{MeV}\). The negative change means the process is endothermic, so about \(24.4\,\text{MeV}\) is absorbed.
variants.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 3, pp. 471-472, §10.6 Nuclear Fusion

subproblems.prompt: Use the peak of the binding-energy curve to decide the energy sign for fusing nuclei that are already near the iron region into a heavier nucleus.

variants.stem: Two identical nuclei each have \(A=56\) and binding energy per nucleon \(8.80\,\text{MeV}\). They fuse into a single nucleus with \(A=112\) and binding energy per nucleon \(8.50\,\text{MeV}\). What is the sign and approximate magnitude of the reaction energy?
choices: It must release energy because fusion always releases energy in stars.

distractor_rationales.A: This gets the magnitude of the binding-energy change but reverses the sign.
distractor_rationales.C: This uses the final total binding energy as if it were the released energy.
distractor_rationales.D: This uses the initial total binding energy as if it were the absorbed energy.
distractor_rationales.E: Fusion of light nuclei can be exothermic, but the binding-energy curve shows fusion near and beyond iron is not generally energy-releasing.

variants.explain_why: The initial total binding energy is \(112(8.80)\), and the final total binding energy is \(112(8.50)\). The final nucleus is less tightly bound by \(112(0.30)=33.6\,\text{MeV}\), so the reaction absorbs energy.
variants.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 3, pp. 472-473, §10.6 Nuclear Fusion

variants.stem: Two identical nuclei each have \(A=58\) and binding energy per nucleon \(8.79\,\text{MeV}\). They fuse into a single nucleus with \(A=116\) and binding energy per nucleon \(8.48\,\text{MeV}\). What is the sign and approximate magnitude of the reaction energy?
choices: There is no energy change because \(58+58=116\).

distractor_rationales.A: This reverses the sign: the product has lower binding energy per nucleon than the reactants.
distractor_rationales.B: This uses approximately the final total binding energy as the energy released.
distractor_rationales.D: This uses approximately the initial total binding energy as the energy absorbed.
distractor_rationales.E: Conserving total mass number does not prevent a change in binding energy.

variants.explain_why: The binding-energy change is \(116(8.48)-116(8.79)=116(-0.31)=-35.96\,\text{MeV}\). A negative reaction energy means about \(36.0\,\text{MeV}\) must be supplied.
variants.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 3, pp. 472-473, §10.6 Nuclear Fusion

subproblems.prompt: Connect nuclear energy generation in a stellar core to the pressure balance against gravity.

variants.stem: In a simplified main-sequence-like core model, fusion had been supplying \(1.0\times10^{31}\,\text{W}\) of power to maintain thermal gas pressure. If the available nuclear reactions in the core stop supplying net energy, what does the OpenStax pressure-balance picture imply next?
choices: The core gains extra outward pressure because gravity converts directly into fusion power.
The inward gravitational effect disappears once fusion power stops.
The thermal pressure is no longer replenished by nuclear energy, so gravity tends to make the core contract.
The core is automatically supported by beta-decay electrons instead of thermal pressure.
The star becomes stable because lower power output always means lower inward gravity.

distractor_rationales.A: This confuses gravitational contraction with a replacement nuclear energy source; gravity does not automatically create outward fusion pressure.
distractor_rationales.B: Gravity remains present whether or not nuclear reactions produce energy.
distractor_rationales.D: This imports a different mechanism not described as the thermal-pressure support from fusion in the cited stellar-core discussion.
distractor_rationales.E: Reducing nuclear power removes outward support; it does not reduce the star’s gravitational pull in the needed way.

variants.explain_why: The corpus describes stellar equilibrium as a balance between outward thermal gas pressure from nuclear energy release and inward gravity. If nuclear reactions no longer provide usable power, that outward support is not maintained, so contraction is expected.
variants.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 3, pp. 472-473, §10.6 Nuclear Fusion

variants.stem: A star’s core was previously producing \(2.5\times10^{30}\,\text{W}\) by exothermic fusion, giving outward thermal gas pressure. If the core composition changes so that reactions there provide no net energy source, which consequence follows from the same pressure-balance reasoning?
choices: The loss of nuclear heating weakens outward thermal support, allowing gravitational contraction.
The core must expand because no heat is being produced.
The star’s gravity turns off after the fuel is exhausted.
Fission automatically takes over as the new long-term pressure source in any core.
The outward pressure increases because the temperature must instantly rise to compensate.

distractor_rationales.B: Removing heat input does not by itself cause expansion; it removes a source of outward thermal pressure.
distractor_rationales.C: Gravity is due to mass and remains important after fuel exhaustion.
distractor_rationales.D: The corpus does not say fission automatically replaces fusion as a stellar support mechanism, especially near the binding-energy peak.
distractor_rationales.E: A temperature rise from contraction is not the same as a sustained nuclear energy source maintaining equilibrium.

variants.explain_why: In the OpenStax picture, exothermic fusion supplies energy that helps maintain outward thermal gas pressure. Without a usable nuclear energy source, that support is lost and inward gravity drives contraction.
variants.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 3, pp. 470-471, §10.6 Nuclear Fusion

parent_variants.stem: A \(30M_\odot\) star has completed successive stages of nucleosynthesis until its core is dominated by nuclei in the iron-nickel region. Using the binding-energy-per-nucleon argument for stellar fusion, what consequence follows for nuclear reactions in that core?
choices: Fusion of those nuclei becomes a stronger energy source, so thermal support increases.
Reactions involving those nuclei do not provide usable net nuclear energy, so the core lacks fusion-powered pressure support against gravity.
Iron-region nuclei beta decay into hydrogen, restarting the proton-proton chain as the main support mechanism.
Fission of those nuclei becomes the dominant exothermic process and replaces fusion pressure.
Helium capture on iron-region nuclei forms oxygen and releases enough gamma radiation to stabilize the core.

problems.stem: An electron and a positron are initially at rest and annihilate. Assume the final state consists only of photons, and take \(m_e c^2 = 0.511\,\mathrm{MeV}\). Which final state is consistent with conservation of energy and linear momentum?
choices: Two photons, each with energy 0.511 MeV, emitted in opposite directions
Two photons, each with energy 1.022 MeV, emitted in opposite directions
Two photons, each with energy 0.256 MeV, emitted in opposite directions
Two photons, each with energy 0.511 MeV, emitted at right angles to one another

distractors.label: B
distractors.rationale: The error is to compute the total rest energy as \(2m_e c^2 = 1.022\,\mathrm{MeV}\) and then give that entire amount to each of two photons, producing this choice.

distractors.label: C
distractors.rationale: The error is to count only one particle’s rest energy, \(m_e c^2 = 0.511\,\mathrm{MeV}\), and then divide it equally between two photons: \(E_{\gamma} = 0.511/2\,\mathrm{MeV}\), producing this choice.

distractors.label: D
distractors.rationale: The error is to use energy conservation alone, giving a single photon \(E_\gamma = 2m_e c^2 = 1.022\,\mathrm{MeV}\). But a single photon carrying nonzero energy also carries nonzero momentum, while the initial total momentum is zero.

distractors.label: E
distractors.rationale: The error is to split the available rest energy between two photons but allow an arbitrary angle. At 90 degrees, the photon momenta add to a nonzero vector, so the initial zero momentum would not be matched.

solution_decomposition.subgoal: Find the total initial energy.
solution_decomposition.rubric: Because both particles are initially at rest, add the electron and positron rest energies using the given value of \(m_e c^2\).

solution_decomposition.subgoal: Apply momentum conservation to the photon directions.
solution_decomposition.rubric: The initial total momentum is zero, so the two photon momentum vectors must cancel.

solution_decomposition.subgoal: Relate photon energy to momentum.
solution_decomposition.rubric: For photons, nonzero energy implies nonzero momentum, so a lone photon cannot represent a zero-total-momentum final state.

solution_decomposition.subgoal: Determine the photon energies.
solution_decomposition.rubric: With two photons whose momenta cancel, the equal momentum magnitudes imply equal photon energies; divide the total available energy evenly.

problems.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 3, pp. 498-499, §11.1 Introduction to Particle Physics

subproblems.prompt: Compute the total initial energy when a particle and its antiparticle are both initially at rest.

variants.stem: An electron and a positron are both initially at rest. In this simplified case, take each particle's rest energy to be \(0.400\,\mathrm{MeV}\). What is the total initial energy available before annihilation?

distractor_rationales.A: This halves the rest energy of one particle instead of adding both rest energies.
distractor_rationales.B: This counts only one particle's rest energy and omits the antiparticle.
distractor_rationales.D: This adds an extra rest-energy contribution that is not present when there are only two particles.
distractor_rationales.E: This doubles the already-combined rest energy, effectively counting four particles.

variants.explain_why: Because both particles are initially at rest, the available energy is just the sum of the two rest energies: \(E_{\text{tot}}=2(0.400\,\mathrm{MeV})=0.800\,\mathrm{MeV}\).
variants.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 3, pp. 539-541, §11.6 The Big Bang

variants.stem: A particle-antiparticle pair is initially at rest. Each particle has rest energy \(0.650\,\mathrm{MeV}\), and their initial kinetic energies are negligible. What is the total initial energy?

distractor_rationales.A: This incorrectly takes half of one rest energy.
distractor_rationales.B: This counts only one member of the pair.
distractor_rationales.C: This is a partial sum, not the sum of both complete rest energies.
distractor_rationales.E: This counts twice as much energy as the two-particle rest-energy total.

variants.explain_why: For two particles initially at rest, total initial energy is the sum of their rest energies: \(E_{\text{tot}}=0.650+0.650=1.300\,\mathrm{MeV}\).
variants.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 3, p. 239, §5.9 Relativistic Energy

subproblems.prompt: Use linear momentum conservation to decide which photon directions can produce zero total momentum.

variants.stem: A system initially has zero total momentum. One photon in the final state has energy \(0.700\,\mathrm{MeV}\) and travels in the \(+x\) direction. Which additional photon would make the total photon momentum zero?
choices: A \(0.700\,\mathrm{MeV}\) photon traveling in the \(-x\) direction
A \(0.700\,\mathrm{MeV}\) photon traveling in the \(+x\) direction
A \(0.700\,\mathrm{MeV}\) photon traveling in the \(+y\) direction
A \(0.350\,\mathrm{MeV}\) photon traveling in the \(-x\) direction
A \(1.400\,\mathrm{MeV}\) photon traveling in the \(-x\) direction

distractor_rationales.B: Same-direction photon momenta add instead of canceling.
distractor_rationales.C: Right-angle photon momenta do not cancel vectorially; a nonzero resultant remains.
distractor_rationales.D: The direction is opposite, but the smaller energy means smaller photon momentum, so cancellation is incomplete.
distractor_rationales.E: The direction is opposite, but the larger energy means larger photon momentum, so it over-cancels and leaves net momentum.

variants.explain_why: For photons, momentum magnitude is proportional to energy. To cancel the first photon's momentum, the second must have equal energy and travel in the opposite direction.
variants.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 3, p. 239, §5.9 Relativistic Energy

variants.stem: A final state contains one photon of energy \(0.450\,\mathrm{MeV}\) traveling in the \(+x\) direction. The initial total momentum was zero. Which second photon would allow momentum conservation?
choices: A \(0.900\,\mathrm{MeV}\) photon traveling in the \(-x\) direction
A \(0.450\,\mathrm{MeV}\) photon traveling in the \(+x\) direction
A \(0.450\,\mathrm{MeV}\) photon traveling in the \(-x\) direction
A \(0.450\,\mathrm{MeV}\) photon traveling in the \(+y\) direction
No second photon is needed because photons have no rest mass

distractor_rationales.A: This photon points opposite, but its larger energy gives it too much momentum to cancel exactly.
distractor_rationales.B: This photon has the right energy but the wrong direction; the momenta add.
distractor_rationales.D: Equal energy at a right angle gives a diagonal nonzero net momentum, not zero.
distractor_rationales.E: Photons have zero rest mass, but a photon with nonzero energy still carries nonzero momentum.

variants.explain_why: Zero initial momentum requires the final photon momentum vectors to sum to zero. Equal-energy photons traveling in opposite directions have equal and opposite momenta.
variants.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 3, p. 536, §11.7 Evolution of the Early Universe

subproblems.prompt: Given a zero-momentum two-photon final state, divide the available energy consistently.

variants.stem: A particle-antiparticle annihilation at rest has total available energy \(1.600\,\mathrm{MeV}\). The final state is two photons moving in opposite directions with equal momentum magnitudes. What is the energy of each photon?

distractor_rationales.A: This would give photons no energy and therefore no annihilation radiation.
distractor_rationales.C: This assigns the entire available energy to each photon, doubling the total final energy.
distractor_rationales.D: This doubles the total available energy instead of conserving it.
distractor_rationales.E: This divides the total energy among four equal parts rather than two photons.

variants.explain_why: Opposite photon momenta cancel only when their momentum magnitudes, and hence their energies, are equal. Energy conservation then gives \(E_{\gamma}=1.600/2=0.800\,\mathrm{MeV}\).
variants.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 3, pp. 539-541, §11.6 The Big Bang

variants.stem: An annihilation at rest produces exactly two back-to-back photons. The total energy available to the photons is \(2.400\,\mathrm{MeV}\). What energy must each photon have?

distractor_rationales.A: This incorrectly divides the total energy into four equal parts.
distractor_rationales.B: This would leave some of the available energy unaccounted for.
distractor_rationales.C: This gives each photon the full total energy, so the final energy would be too large.
distractor_rationales.E: This doubles the total energy and violates energy conservation.

variants.explain_why: For two back-to-back photons with zero net momentum, the photon energies are equal. Thus each carries half the total available energy: \(2.400/2=1.200\,\mathrm{MeV}\).
variants.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 3, pp. 539-541, §11.6 The Big Bang

parent_variants.stem: An electron and a positron are initially at rest and annihilate. Assume the final state consists only of photons, and take \(m_e c^2 = 0.600\,\mathrm{MeV}\). Which final state is consistent with conservation of energy and linear momentum?
choices: Two photons, each with energy \(1.200\,\mathrm{MeV}\), emitted in opposite directions
Two photons, each with energy \(0.600\,\mathrm{MeV}\), emitted in opposite directions
Two photons, each with energy \(0.300\,\mathrm{MeV}\), emitted in opposite directions
Two photons, each with energy \(0.600\,\mathrm{MeV}\), emitted at right angles to one another

problems.stem: In the Standard Model description summarized in the passage, a proton is a baryon made of two up quarks and one down quark. Which exchanged boson is responsible for binding the quarks together in such a hadron?

distractors.label: A
distractors.rationale: Likely error: treating any interaction among charged constituents as Coulombic. This would point to photons, since photons carry electromagnetic interactions between charged particles, but it overlooks the interaction specifically associated with quark binding.

distractors.label: B
distractors.rationale: Likely error: confusing the force responsible for certain particle decays with the force that holds quarks inside hadrons. This would point to W and Z bosons, since they carry weak interactions and are involved in weak decays.

distractors.label: D
distractors.rationale: Likely error: reasoning that because quarks have mass, their mutual attraction must be gravitational. This would point to gravitons, but the passage identifies gravity as far weaker than the other forces in particle-scale interactions.

distractors.label: E
distractors.rationale: Likely error: interpreting the Higgs boson as the particle that produces all binding because it is associated with mass. This would point to Higgs bosons, but the passage distinguishes giving particles mass from carrying an ordinary force.

solution_decomposition.subgoal: Classify the constituents of a proton as quarks in a baryon.
solution_decomposition.rubric: Use the given quark content of the proton and the statement that baryons are three-quark hadrons.

solution_decomposition.subgoal: Identify the relevant fundamental interaction for quark binding.
solution_decomposition.rubric: Recognize from the passage that quarks in hadrons bind through the strong interaction.

solution_decomposition.subgoal: Map the interaction to its exchange boson in the Standard Model.
solution_decomposition.rubric: Select the boson listed as the force carrier for the strong interaction between quarks.

problems.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 3, p. 496, §11.1 Introduction to Particle Physics

problems.stem: Oxygen has three stable isotopes with the following isotopic masses and percent abundances: O-16 has mass 15.9949 u and abundance 99.76%, O-17 has mass 16.9991 u and abundance 0.04%, and O-18 has mass 17.9992 u and abundance 0.20%. Using the abundance-weighted average, what atomic mass should be quoted for naturally occurring oxygen?

distractors.label: B
distractors.rationale: Because O-16 is overwhelmingly abundant, a student may ignore the rarer stable isotopes entirely and report only the O-16 isotopic mass, giving 15.9949 u.

distractors.label: C
distractors.rationale: A student may weight the integer mass numbers instead of the listed isotopic masses: 0.9976(16)+0.0004(17)+0.0020(18)=16.0044 u.

distractors.label: D
distractors.rationale: A student may average the three listed masses as if the isotopes were equally abundant: (15.9949+16.9991+17.9992)/3=16.9977 u.

distractors.label: E
distractors.rationale: A student may multiply by percent abundances directly rather than by fractional abundances: 99.76(15.9949)+0.04(16.9991)+0.20(17.9992)=1599.93 u.

solution_decomposition.subgoal: Identify the needed averaging method
solution_decomposition.rubric: Use the isotope abundances as weights for the corresponding listed isotopic masses.

solution_decomposition.subgoal: Convert abundances consistently
solution_decomposition.rubric: Change percent abundances to fractional abundances before multiplying by masses.

solution_decomposition.subgoal: Combine isotope contributions
solution_decomposition.rubric: Sum the three weighted mass contributions and round to the precision supported by the data.

problems.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 3, pp. 444-445, §10.1 Properties of Nuclei

problems.stem: In the thorium decay series, suppose the net effect from \(^{232}\mathrm{Th}\) with \(Z=90\) to stable \(^{208}\mathrm{Pb}\) with \(Z=82\) consists only of alpha and beta-minus decays. An alpha decay lowers \(Z\) by 2 and \(A\) by 4; a beta-minus decay raises \(Z\) by 1 and leaves \(A\) unchanged. How many of each decay are required?

distractors.label: B
distractors.rationale: Use the decrease in mass number to count alpha emissions, then stop because the mass number has been matched; this produces 6 alpha decays and 0 beta-minus decays.

distractors.label: C
distractors.rationale: Divide the atomic-number drop by 2, treating the change in element as due only to alpha emission; this produces 4 alpha decays and 0 beta-minus decays.

distractors.label: D
distractors.rationale: After using the mass-number change to count alpha emissions, note that several unit changes in atomic number remain, but use beta-plus instead of beta-minus for the needed upward correction; this produces 6 alpha decays and 4 beta-plus decays.

distractors.label: E
distractors.rationale: Treat alpha emission as lowering the mass number by only 2 rather than 4, so \(a=(232-208)/2=12\); then enforce atomic-number balance with \(90-2(12)+b=82\), giving 16 beta-minus decays.

solution_decomposition.subgoal: Track the mass number through the decay series.
solution_decomposition.rubric: Let \(a\) be the number of alpha decays and set \(232-4a=208\), since beta-minus decay leaves the mass number unchanged.

solution_decomposition.subgoal: Track the atomic number through the decay series.
solution_decomposition.rubric: Let \(b\) be the number of beta-minus decays and use \(90-2a+b=82\), since alpha decay lowers \(Z\) by 2 and beta-minus decay raises \(Z\) by 1.

solution_decomposition.subgoal: Match the counts to the listed option.
solution_decomposition.rubric: Choose the option whose counts satisfy both the mass-number equation and the atomic-number equation.

problems.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 3, p. 460, §10.4 Nuclear Reactions

problems.stem: In the one-dimensional free electron model of a very thin metal rod, electrons fill the allowed energy levels from lowest to highest, two electrons per level because of spin up and spin down. If the rod has 14 conduction electrons, what is the quantum number n of the highest occupied one-dimensional energy level?

distractors.label: B
distractors.rationale: Treating each spatial energy level as if it could hold only one electron makes the level count equal to the electron count, producing n = 14.

distractors.label: C
distractors.rationale: Misreading the two spin states as requiring two distinct spatial levels per electron doubles the electron count, producing n = 28.

distractors.label: D
distractors.rationale: Using a classical-gas picture in which all electrons can occupy the lowest level ignores the exclusion principle and leads to selecting n = 1.

distractors.label: E
distractors.rationale: Choosing the next available level after the paired filling is complete uses N/2 + 1 rather than the occupied-level count, producing n = 8.

solution_decomposition.subgoal: Identify the occupancy rule for the model.
solution_decomposition.rubric: Use the stated free-electron filling rule: each one-dimensional spatial energy level can accommodate two electrons, one spin up and one spin down.

solution_decomposition.subgoal: Convert the electron count into the highest occupied level index.
solution_decomposition.rubric: Count the occupied levels from lowest upward and stop at the level containing the last electron rather than advancing to the next empty level.

problems.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 3, p. 409, §9.4 Free Electron Model of Metals

problems.stem: A silicon crystal is doped with acceptor impurities. According to the band-theory description of doped semiconductors, which statement best describes the impurity levels and the majority charge carriers?
choices: New filled energy levels are created just below the conduction band, and free electrons are the majority carriers.
New empty electron states are created just above the valence band; when valence electrons fill these states, mobile holes in the valence band are produced as the majority carriers.
The band gap disappears, so the material conducts like a metal with no distinction between majority and minority carriers.
New empty electron states are created just below the conduction band, and thermal excitation from the valence band directly makes electrons the majority carriers.
No impurity band or band-gap state is involved; doping only lowers the lattice resistivity by reducing atomic vibrations.

distractors.label: A
distractors.misconception: confusing acceptor doping with donor/n-type doping
distractors.rationale: If acceptor impurities are mistakenly treated as donor impurities, one uses the n-type rule: impurity levels are placed just below the conduction band and electrons supplied by impurity atoms are taken to be the dominant mobile charges, leading to choice A.

distractors.label: C
distractors.misconception: treating doping as closing the semiconductor band gap
distractors.rationale: If one assumes that adding impurities turns the semiconductor into an ordinary metal, then the distinction between valence band, conduction band, band gap, and carrier type is erased, leading to choice C.

distractors.label: D
distractors.misconception: misplacing acceptor levels while retaining thermal excitation language
distractors.rationale: If one remembers that acceptor states are empty but incorrectly places them near the conduction band, then valence-band excitation is interpreted as producing conduction electrons as the dominant carriers, leading to choice D.

distractors.label: E
distractors.misconception: confusing semiconductor doping with temperature-dependent resistivity in metals
distractors.rationale: If one focuses on lattice vibrations as the cause of resistivity changes and ignores the creation of impurity states in the band gap, doping is incorrectly described as merely reducing scattering, leading to choice E.

solution_decomposition.subgoal: Classify the impurity type.
solution_decomposition.rubric: Use the corpus distinction that an acceptor impurity corresponds to p-type semiconductor behavior, not n-type behavior.

solution_decomposition.subgoal: Locate the impurity-related energy states.
solution_decomposition.rubric: Apply the band-theory statement that p-type doping introduces states near the valence-band edge on the gap side, whereas n-type doping introduces states near the conduction-band edge.

solution_decomposition.subgoal: Infer the mobile carrier created by filling the acceptor state.
solution_decomposition.rubric: Track what is left behind in the valence band when an electron occupies an acceptor-related state, and identify the corresponding majority-carrier description.

problems.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 3, pp. 418-419, §9.6 Semiconductors and Doping

problems.stem: A conducting sample is placed in a static magnetic field. Suppose that, on cooling, its resistivity drops exactly to zero, but you are not allowed to assume any special superconducting property beyond zero resistance. Using Ohm’s law and Faraday’s law, which statement is correct?
choices: The magnetic field must be expelled from the sample, because zero resistance automatically produces perfect diamagnetism.
The magnetic flux through the sample remains unchanged; expulsion of magnetic field is a special Meissner-effect property of superconductors, not a consequence of zero resistance alone.
The magnetic flux slowly decays because all low-temperature conductors retain a small residual resistance.
The internal electric field becomes infinite when the resistivity is zero, so Faraday’s law requires rapid expulsion of flux.
Magnetic-field expulsion occurs only in high-temperature Type II superconductors; Type I superconductors merely have zero resistance.

distractors.label: A
distractors.misconception: Conflating zero resistance with the Meissner effect
distractors.rationale: The likely error is to assume that zero resistivity by itself implies perfect diamagnetism. That shortcut treats the vanishing-resistance condition as if it forced the net internal magnetic field to be zero, producing the choice that the field must be expelled automatically.

distractors.label: C
distractors.misconception: Replacing ideal superconductivity with ordinary residual resistance
distractors.rationale: The likely error is to import the idea that practical resistance measurements may only show a very small nonzero value. If the sample is treated as an ordinary conductor with residual resistance, one may imagine magnetic flux relaxing gradually, leading to the slow-decay choice.

distractors.label: D
distractors.misconception: Algebraic inversion of Ohm’s law at zero resistivity
distractors.rationale: The likely error is to read the relation between current density, electric field, and resistivity backward: taking zero resistivity to require an infinite electric field for finite current. Substituting such a large electric field into Faraday’s law suggests a rapid change of flux, which produces this choice.

distractors.label: E
distractors.misconception: Confusing superconductor type with the existence of the Meissner effect
distractors.rationale: The likely error is to associate magnetic-field expulsion only with high-temperature Type II materials. The corpus distinguishes Type I and Type II mainly by critical fields and practical current densities, so this mistaken classification leads to the claim that Type I materials only have zero resistance.

solution_decomposition.subgoal: Use Ohm’s law in the zero-resistivity limit.
solution_decomposition.rubric: Recognize that for finite current density, the net internal electric field must vanish when the resistivity is taken to zero.

solution_decomposition.subgoal: Connect the electric field to magnetic flux using Faraday’s law.
solution_decomposition.rubric: Use the vanishing line integral of the electric field to determine whether the magnetic flux is permitted to change in time.

solution_decomposition.subgoal: Separate zero resistance from superconducting perfect diamagnetism.
solution_decomposition.rubric: Identify the Meissner effect as an additional defining property of superconductors rather than a result derived from zero resistance alone.

problems.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 3, pp. 426-427, §9.8 Superconductivity

problems.stem: The excerpt states that an FCC ionic solid such as NaCl has Madelung constant about 1.75, while a BCC structure has Madelung constant about 1.76. Taking the FCC value as the reference, by what percentage does the BCC Madelung constant exceed the FCC value?

distractors.label: B
distractors.rationale: Likely error: subtract the constants and attach a percent sign without normalizing. This gives 1.76 - 1.75 = 0.01, then misread as 1.0%.

distractors.label: C
distractors.rationale: Likely error: compute the fractional excess but forget to multiply by \(100\). This gives \(\frac{0.01}{1.75} \approx 0.0057\), then writing that number with a percent sign gives about \(0.006\%\).

distractors.label: D
distractors.rationale: Likely error: compute the ratio of the two Madelung constants and report it as a percentage rather than subtracting the reference value. This gives \(\frac{1.76}{1.75} \times 100 \approx 100.6\%\).

distractors.label: E
distractors.rationale: Likely error: use the coordination numbers mentioned in the excerpt instead of the Madelung constants. Comparing BCC's \(8\) nearest neighbors with FCC's \(6\) gives \(\frac{8 - 6}{6} \times 100 \approx 33\%\).

solution_decomposition.subgoal: Identify the relevant quantities from the crystal-structure description.
solution_decomposition.rubric: Use the quoted Madelung constants for FCC and BCC, not the nearest-neighbor counts.

solution_decomposition.subgoal: Compute a percentage excess relative to FCC.
solution_decomposition.rubric: Subtract the FCC value from the BCC value, divide by the FCC value, and multiply by 100.

problems.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 3, pp. 403-404, §9.3 Bonding in Crystalline Solids

problems.stem: X-rays of wavelength 0.085 nm are incident on a crystal whose reflecting planes are separated by 0.541 nm. Using Bragg diffraction, what is the smallest angle, measured from the planes, at which a diffraction maximum can be observed?

distractors.label: A
distractors.rationale: The student correctly forms the small dimensionless ratio \(\lambda/(2d)=0.0786\), but then treats this radian-scale value as if it were already an angle in degrees.

distractors.label: C
distractors.misconception: Missing factor of two in Bragg path difference
distractors.rationale: Using \(d\sin\theta = n\lambda\) for first order gives \(\sin\theta = \frac{0.085}{0.541}\), which leads to \(9.04^\circ\).

distractors.label: D
distractors.misconception: Omitted factor of two and used the complementary angle
distractors.rationale: After using \(d\sin\theta = n\lambda\) to get \(9.04^\circ\) relative to the planes, the student reports the angle relative to the normal by subtracting from \(90^\circ\).

distractors.label: E
distractors.misconception: Angle measured from the normal instead of from the planes
distractors.rationale: Bragg angles in this setup are measured relative to the reflecting planes; reporting the complementary angle gives 85.49°.

solution_decomposition.subgoal: Identify the applicable diffraction condition.
solution_decomposition.rubric: Use the Bragg equation \(n\lambda = 2d\sin\theta\), where \(\theta\) is measured with respect to the reflecting planes.

solution_decomposition.subgoal: Choose the order corresponding to the smallest maximum angle.
solution_decomposition.rubric: Use first order, \(n = 1\), because increasing \(n\) increases \(\sin\theta\) for fixed \(\lambda\) and \(d\).

solution_decomposition.subgoal: Evaluate the inverse sine.
solution_decomposition.rubric: Compute \(\theta = \sin^{-1}\left[\frac{\lambda}{2d}\right]\) with \(\lambda = 0.085\,\mathrm{nm}\) and \(d = 0.541\,\mathrm{nm}\), then express the result in degrees.

problems.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 3, pp. 178-179, §4.6 X-Ray Diffraction

problems.stem:
In the lunar-theory setup, let \(\vec{R} = \vec{r}_E\) be the Earth’s heliocentric position and \(\vec{r} = \vec{r}_M - \vec{r}_E\) be the Moon’s geocentric position. The equations of motion are

\[
  \frac{d^2\vec{r}_E}{dt^2} = -n'^2 a'^3 \frac{\vec{R}}{|\vec{R}|^3},
\]
\[
  \frac{d^2\vec{r}_M}{dt^2} = -n^2 a^3 \frac{\vec{r}}{|\vec{r}|^3} - n'^2 a'^3 \frac{\vec{R} + \vec{r}}{|\vec{R} + \vec{r}|^3}.
\]

Assume \(|\vec{r}| \ll |\vec{R}|\) and \(|\vec{R}| = a'\). Keeping only terms through first order in \(r/a'\), which expression gives the relative acceleration \(d^2\vec{r}/dt^2\)?

distractors.label: B
distractors.rationale: If the solar contribution to the relative acceleration is formed with the difference in the opposite order, the first-order expansion of (R + r)/|R + r|³ − R/|R|³ is multiplied by the wrong overall sign, producing choice B.

distractors.label: C
distractors.misconception: Forgot the indirect acceleration of the Earth
distractors.rationale: Using the Sun’s force on the Moon but not subtracting the Sun’s force on the Earth leaves the uniform heliocentric term −n'²a' Rhat in addition to the first-order tidal correction, producing choice C.

distractors.label: D
distractors.misconception: Treated the tidal field as purely one-dimensional radial stretching
distractors.rationale: Expanding only the inverse-square magnitude along the Earth–Sun line gives a radial differential acceleration of \(2n'^{2}\hat{R}(\hat{R}\cdot\vec{r})\), but it omits the transverse part of the vector-field expansion, producing choice D.

distractors.label: E
distractors.misconception: Wrong sign in the binomial expansion
distractors.rationale: Taking \(\lvert \vec{R} + \vec{r}\rvert^{-3} \approx \lvert \vec{R}\rvert^{-3}\left[1 + \frac{3(\hat{R}\cdot \vec{r})}{\lvert \vec{R}\rvert}\right]\) instead of using the negative sign in the linear term makes the solar finite difference proportional to \(\vec{r} + 3\hat{R}(\hat{R}\cdot \vec{r})\), producing choice E.

solution_decomposition.subgoal: Form the relative equation of motion.
solution_decomposition.rubric: Use \(\frac{d^2\vec{r}}{dt^2} = \frac{d^2\vec{r}_M}{dt^2} - \frac{d^2\vec{r}_E}{dt^2}\); keep the Earth–Moon inverse-square term exact and write the Sun’s effect as a difference of two heliocentric gravitational fields.

solution_decomposition.subgoal: Linearize the solar vector field.
solution_decomposition.rubric: Apply the first-order Taylor expansion of \(\vec{x}/|\vec{x}|^3\) about \(\vec{x} = \vec{R}\), equivalently using the Jacobian \((\delta_{ij} - 3 \hat{R}_i \hat{R}_j)/|\vec{R}|^3\), and discard terms of order \((r/a')^2\) and higher.

solution_decomposition.subgoal: Simplify using the circular-orbit scale.
solution_decomposition.rubric: Set |R| = a' so that a'³/|R|³ = 1; express the remaining linear term using Rhat and verify that the uniform solar acceleration cancels.

problems.source_ref: Fitzpatrick: Newtonian Dynamics, pp. 220-221, §14.2 Preliminary Analysis

problems.stem: A spacecraft communicates computer data to Earth as a sequence of binary bits and also uses echo timing to determine the distance to nearby objects. Based on the electromagnetic-spectrum applications described, which choice best identifies the electromagnetic range most directly associated with both satellite-transmitted information and radar distance measurements?
choices: Radio waves, because all long-distance communication is classified as radio broadcasting
Microwaves, because satellite information is commonly carried on them and radar determines distance by timing their echoes
Infrared waves, because they are emitted by thermal agitation and therefore naturally encode computer data
Visible light, because human vision detects it and therefore it is best suited for interpreting binary information
Magnetic fields, because computer hard drives use magnetism to store information

distractors.label: A
distractors.rationale: The table lists radio waves as useful for communications, so a student may treat every communication technology as ordinary radio broadcasting and choose the radio category instead of distinguishing the specific satellite and radar applications.

distractors.label: C
distractors.rationale: Because infrared waves are associated with thermal agitation and thermal imaging, a student may infer that naturally emitted radiation is the appropriate carrier for information, leading to the infrared choice.

distractors.label: D
distractors.rationale: Since visible light is associated with human vision, a student may assume that information must be carried in the range humans can directly see, producing the visible-light choice.

distractors.label: E
distractors.rationale: The corpus notes that computer hard drives involve magnetism, so a student may transfer that storage mechanism to spacecraft communication and echo ranging, yielding the magnetic-field choice.

solution_decomposition.subgoal: Identify how computer data are represented in transmission.
solution_decomposition.rubric: Use the statement that computer data are transmitted as binary ones and zeros, with each one or zero constituting one bit.

solution_decomposition.subgoal: Connect satellite information transmission to an electromagnetic range.
solution_decomposition.rubric: Find the range explicitly described as carrying most satellite-transmitted information.

solution_decomposition.subgoal: Connect echo timing for distance measurements to an electromagnetic range.
solution_decomposition.rubric: Use the described radar application in which timed echoes from the same range determine distances to objects.

solution_decomposition.subgoal: Eliminate application-based near misses.
solution_decomposition.rubric: Reject choices based only on broad communication use, thermal emission, human vision, or magnetic storage rather than the paired satellite-and-radar functions.

problems.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 2, p. 694, §16.5 The Electromagnetic Spectrum

problems.stem: The colliding galaxies nicknamed “The Mice” are described as being about 300 million light-years from Earth. Using the approximate Hubble expansion rate 70 km/s/Mpc and 1 Mpc = 3.26 million light-years, what recessional velocity would Hubble’s law predict for galaxies at that distance?

distractors.label: A
distractors.rationale: Uses distance divided by the Hubble rate instead of multiplying by it: after converting the distance to about \(92\,\mathrm{Mpc}\), computes \(92/70 \approx 1.3\), with units then incorrectly treated as \(\mathrm{km}/\mathrm{s}\).

distractors.label: C
distractors.rationale: Skips the light-year to megaparsec conversion and treats \(300\) million light-years as \(300\,\mathrm{Mpc}\), giving \(70 \times 300 = 2.1 \times 10^{4}\,\mathrm{km/s}\).

distractors.label: D
distractors.rationale: Multiplies by \(3.26\) instead of dividing by \(3.26\) when converting from million light-years to megaparsecs, giving about \(978\,\mathrm{Mpc}\) and then \(70 \times 978 \approx 6.8 \times 10^{4}\,\mathrm{km/s}\).

distractors.label: E
distractors.rationale: Assumes the relevant cosmological speed must be the universal speed limit rather than using the proportionality between recessional velocity and distance, producing approximately \(3.0 \times 10^5\,\mathrm{km/s}\).

solution_decomposition.subgoal: Convert the stated distance into megaparsecs.
solution_decomposition.rubric: Recognize that the Hubble rate is expressed per megaparsec, so 300 million light-years should be divided by 3.26 million light-years per Mpc.

solution_decomposition.subgoal: Apply the Hubble proportionality between recessional velocity and distance.
solution_decomposition.rubric: Multiply the converted distance in Mpc by 70 km/s/Mpc, keeping the resulting units as km/s.

problems.source_ref: OpenStax University Physics Volume 1, p. 20, §1.1 The Scope and Scale of Physics

problems.stem:
A spherical star has mass \(M\), radius \(R\), and mass density varying with radius as \(\rho(r) \propto r^{-\alpha}\), with \(\alpha < \tfrac{5}{2}\). For small-amplitude radial pulsations, the virial-theorem result is

\[
\omega = \left[ \left(\frac{5 - \alpha}{5 - 2\alpha}\right) \left(\frac{GM}{R^3}\right) \right]^{1/2}
\].

If \(\alpha = 1\), what is the pulsation angular frequency?

distractors.label: B
distractors.rationale: Likely error: reverse the ratio \((5 - \alpha)/(5 - 2\alpha)\). With \(\alpha = 1\), this gives \((5 - 2)/(5 - 1) = 3/4\), so \(\omega = \sqrt{3GM/(4R^3)}\).

distractors.label: C
distractors.rationale: Likely error: keep only the dimensional dynamical frequency scale and ignore the \(\alpha\)-dependent prefactor. This gives \(\omega = \sqrt{\frac{GM}{R^3}}\).

distractors.label: D
distractors.rationale: Likely error: substitute \(\alpha = 1\) but retain only \(5 - 2\alpha\) as the multiplicative factor. This gives \(5 - 2 = 3\), so \(\omega = \sqrt{\frac{3GM}{R^3}}\).

distractors.label: E
distractors.rationale: Likely error: simplify the \(\alpha\)-dependent factor incorrectly as \(5 - 2\alpha\) divided by neither numerator nor denominator, then combine with the square root. With \(\alpha = 1\), this produces a factor \(2\) under the radical, so \(\omega = \sqrt{\frac{2GM}{R^3}}\).

solution_decomposition.subgoal: Identify the relevant pulsation formula for a density law \(\rho \propto r^{-\alpha}\).
solution_decomposition.rubric: Use the virial-theorem expression in which \(\omega\) is proportional to \(\sqrt{\frac{GM}{R^3}}\) multiplied by the square root of an \(\alpha\)-dependent dimensionless ratio.

solution_decomposition.subgoal: Substitute the given density exponent.
solution_decomposition.rubric: Evaluate 5 - alpha and 5 - 2 alpha for alpha = 1, then form the ratio before taking the square root.

solution_decomposition.subgoal: Check dimensional consistency.
solution_decomposition.rubric: Confirm that \(GM/R^3\) has units of inverse time squared, so the square root has units of angular frequency.

problems.source_ref: Fitzpatrick: Newtonian Dynamics, pp. 19-20, §2.8 Exercises
